clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 24-year-old athlete. 4 weeks ago had knee surgery for a ligament injury. Reports pain and swelling around the surgical site. Struggling to regain normal movement and function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform sport-specific movements. Favours right leg, walks with limp. Struggles particularly with loaded knee bending (stairs, squats, kneeling).

clues.label: History
clues.text: Knee ligament tear from skiing injury. Reconstruction with autograft hamstring. Early post-operative phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Knee flexion 80 degrees, extension lag 5-10 degrees. Quadriceps strength 3+/5. Hamstring strength weak 3/5.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-rays show graft positioning adequate. Tunnel placement appropriate. MRI shows intact graft.

puzzles.explanation: Post-ACL Reconstruction shows early post-operative period (4 weeks) with expected swelling, weakness, and need for intensive neuromuscular rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old presents with sudden onset of unilateral facial drooping and weakness over 24-48 hours. Notices asymmetry in mirror, difficulty closing eye on affected side, and mild discomfort behind the ear.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot whistle or puff cheeks evenly. Drooling from corner of mouth on affected side. Difficulty with facial expressions—smiling appears lopsided. Eye waters excessively or feels dry depending on position.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Previously healthy. No trauma, surgery, or recent dental work. Symptoms appeared over one night without preceding illness or systemic complaints. No hearing loss, dizziness, or other neurological symptoms. Lives in temperate climate with seasonal outdoor exposure.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Unilateral facial weakness affecting forehead, eye, and mouth—unable to elevate eyebrow or close eye completely on affected side. Intact sensation across face. No vesicles in ear canal. Mild hyperacusis on affected side. Taste intact. Other cranial nerves and motor/sensory exam normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows acute denervation pattern and reduced motor unit recruitment in affected facial muscles. Brain MRI unremarkable—no mass, stroke, or inflammation. Cerebrospinal fluid normal. No evidence of systemic infection on laboratory screening.

puzzles.explanation: Lyme disease-associated facial nerve palsy with asymmetric facial involvement and confirmed Borrelia infection in CSF.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged woman with intermittent hip pain after returning to recreational dancing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain during hip adduction and internal rotation; worse with repetitive flexion movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong hip tightness; no prior hip injury. Recently increased dance frequency and intensity over past 3 months before symptom onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion passive range of motion 100 degrees with pain; positive impingement sign.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows labral fraying without full-thickness tear; acetabular version abnormality.

puzzles.explanation: FAI can develop insidiously with activity increase. Labral pathology often accompanies bony impingement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports ear pain, jaw stiffness, and headaches; symptoms worse in morning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain provoked by talking and chewing; morning symptoms suggest nocturnal grinding.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of psychological stress and parafunctional habits; associated neck muscle tension and frequent tension-type headaches; no prior jaw surgery or significant dental work.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Jaw deviation on opening, severe masticatory muscle tenderness, tooth wear visible, limited mouth opening.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows normal disc position with early degenerative joint surface changes; EMG demonstrates sustained nocturnal masseter and temporalis hyperactivity consistent with parafunctional grinding activity.

puzzles.explanation: TMJ disorder presents with myogenous pain from muscle dysfunction and bruxism with morning symptoms and cervical association.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young female athlete reports persistent fatigue, mood changes, and recurrent lower limb injuries over past 6 months despite continued training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain usual training intensity; reports disproportionate fatigue on exertion and slow recovery between sessions despite high motivation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Training volume increased 40% over 3 months; coincided with deliberate caloric restriction for competition. Multiple stress fractures in past 4 months. No previous injury history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: HR and BP lower than expected for age; slow resting heart rate. Decreased muscle bulk in legs. Generalized weakness on manual muscle testing (4/5). Menarche at age 14, normal cycle until 8 months ago.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA scan: T-score −2.5 spine, −2.1 femoral neck (osteopenia range). Serum 25-OH vitamin D 18 ng/mL, iron studies low-normal, TSH elevated at 5.2 mIU/L.

puzzles.explanation: Overtraining syndrome presents as relative energy deficiency in sport (RED-S) with disordered eating, amenorrhea, and multiple stress fractures.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old reports progressive shortness of breath over 4 months. Initially noticed only with vigorous exercise; now occurs with normal daily activities. Also reports unusual fatigue and occasional ankle swelling by evening.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot climb stairs without stopping to catch breath. Struggles to walk briskly on level ground. Swelling in ankles improves somewhat with leg elevation and overnight rest. Symptoms worse in evenings and when lying flat.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Treated for breast cancer 18 months ago with doxorubicin-based chemotherapy (4 cycles completed). No prior cardiac symptoms or hypertension. Recent weight gain of 3 kg over 2 months despite stable diet. No chest pain or palpitations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Elevated jugular venous pressure (6 cm H₂O). Bilateral lower limb pitting edema to mid-shin. Displaced apex beat (6th intercostal space). Quiet S3 gallop at apex. Mild bilateral crackles at lung bases. Hepatojugular reflux present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiography shows reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF 38%). Dilated left ventricle (LVEDV 145 mL/m²). Grades of diastolic dysfunction. Chest X-ray demonstrates bilateral pulmonary edema with increased interstitial markings and mild cardiomegaly.

puzzles.explanation: Cardiotoxicity from cancer chemotherapy (anthracycline-induced cardiomyopathy). Early detection through screening reveals reduced EF and reduced strain before clinical symptoms develop.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Distance runner reports groin or anterior hip pain that worsens during long runs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to run without severe limp; pain worse with impact activities. Antalgic gait restricts hip extension and loading.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Significant training volume increase, female athlete with energy deficiency. Menarche delayed or amenorrhea.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Groin pain with passive hip flexion, adduction, and internal rotation (FADI). Anterior hip pain with hip extension and axial loading. Hip abductor weakness noted. Positive hop test reproduction of groin pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays may appear normal initially, CT or MRI shows stress fracture on femoral neck. Compression-side fractures seen.

puzzles.explanation: Femoral stress fracture shows compression-side femoral neck fracture in female endurance athlete with energy deficiency.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adult with insidious progressive back pain and progressive height loss.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain upright posture for prolonged periods; stooping and kyphotic positioning for comfort; difficulty walking distances due to postural fatigue.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple previous compression fractures, chronic condition, and associated with corticosteroid use.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Increased thoracic kyphosis with fixed deformity; acute midline tenderness over multiple thoracic vertebrae; no neurological deficit; pain reproduced on axial loading.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows multiple level compression fractures, significant kyphotic deformity, and dEXA scan confirms osteoporosis.

puzzles.explanation: Vertebral compression fracture involves multiple fractures with progressive deformity and chronic pain management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 72-year-old with sudden onset weakness and loss of sensation following a fall from standing height. Acute onset of limb heaviness and numbness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to move arms or legs voluntarily. Cannot grip objects, lift head off pillow, or roll in bed. Difficulty breathing deeply; respiratory effort appears shallow.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall occurred 2 hours ago without protective mechanism. Known osteoporosis. No prior spinal surgery, trauma, or neurological disease. Immediate paralysis from moment of impact.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Flaccid paralysis all four limbs. Complete loss of motor power below C5 level. Sensory level: pinprick and light touch loss below shoulders; preserved sensation at face and neck. Upper trapezius weak but functional. Lower extremity reflexes absent; biceps reflexes present and brisk. Respiratory: shallow breath sounds, paradoxical abdominal breathing pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine: fracture-dislocation at C4-C5 with posterior ligamentous disruption. Spinal cord shows T2 hyperintensity from C4 through C6 indicating acute cord edema and contusion. No hemorrhage. Alignment severely disrupted with cord compression.

puzzles.explanation: Complete cervical spinal cord injury with total motor and sensory loss requiring mechanical ventilation and full support.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 24-year-old athlete with acute knee pain and swelling. Onset 4 weeks ago after injury during skiing. Unable to tolerate walking and light activities without discomfort.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with dynamic control: cutting and pivoting movements cause sense of instability or 'giving way'. Single-leg stance poor.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury mechanism: hyperextension and sudden deceleration during skiing. Treated surgically with graft reconstruction 4 weeks ago. No prior knee surgery or ligament injury history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Knee flexion 125 degrees with soft end-feel, full extension achieved. Quadriceps 4+/5, hamstring 4/5. Anterior drawer test 1+ with firm endpoint. Lachman test mildly positive with firm endpoint. No effusion on palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows intact patellar tendon graft with good incorporation. Knee shows normal alignment. No meniscal involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Post-ACL Reconstruction shows mid-phase recovery (4 months) with good range and pain control but persistent weakness and proprioceptive deficits limiting return to sport.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged person with widespread musculoskeletal pain that comes and goes across the body.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with stress, cold weather, and poor sleep; improves with gentle movement and warmth.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over weeks following major life stressor; concurrent non-musculoskeletal symptoms including unexplained fatigue, poor concentration, and sleep disturbance. No prior significant medical history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tender points at characteristic sites (bilateral occiput, trapezius, supraspinatus, lateral epicondyle, gluteus, greater trochanter, knee). Normal power 5/5 throughout. Full active and passive ROM all joints. No focal neurological signs. Widespread allodynia to light palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: All standard imaging normal; diagnosis based on clinical criteria including widespread pain index.

puzzles.explanation: Fibromyalgia is a centralized pain condition affecting multiple body regions with characteristic tender points, fatigue, and sleep disturbance. presents in typical demographic with clear psychological trigger.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old manual worker. Gradual onset low back pain over 6 months, now constant. Describes aching quality, no radiation. No trauma or acute injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with repetitive bending and lifting. Unable to sustain loads >15 kg. Prolonged sitting (>2 hours) increases symptoms significantly. Symptoms ease with rest and recumbency.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior back injury. No leg symptoms, numbness, or bowel/bladder changes. Symptoms developed gradually during same job role. Tried NSAIDs with minimal relief. No morning stiffness pattern.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar flexion reduced to 40° with pain onset at 20°. Marked bilateral hip flexor tightness. Core strength 3/5. Left-sided paraspinal muscle tenderness and guarding. Straight leg raise negative bilaterally. Lower limb reflexes and sensation normal. No neural tension signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI lumbar spine shows disc bulging L4-L5 and L5-S1 without neural compression. No foraminal stenosis. Mild facet hypertrophy. No significant degenerative changes. Findings correlate with activity-triggered mechanical pain pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Lower back pain work-related shows occupational mechanical pain from lifting and bending demands.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old man with progressive difficulty walking and cognitive slowing over 5 years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walks very slowly with walker, fatigue significantly limits activity, problems with attention.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset without clear relapses. History of single demyelinating event 6 years ago. Progressive worsening since.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Spastic paraparesis (MRC 3/5), hyperreflexia and extensor plantars bilaterally, positive Romberg test, marked slowing of thought processing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spinal MRI shows cervical cord atrophy; brain MRI shows extensive white matter changes and gadolinium-enhancing lesions.

puzzles.explanation: Primary progressive MS with insidious onset, progressive myelopathy, cognitive decline, and spinal cord involvement characteristic of PPMS.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old male is referred to cardiac rehabilitation with breathlessness, exercise intolerance, and bilateral ankle swelling that worsens throughout the day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Becomes breathless walking from the bedroom to the kitchen (NYHA Class III). Reports needing 3 pillows to sleep (orthopnoea) and waking at night gasping for air (paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea).

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of myocardial infarction 5 years ago. Hypertension and type 2 diabetes. On multiple cardiac medications including ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, and diuretic.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Elevated JVP. Bilateral pitting oedema to mid-shins. Bibasal fine crackles on auscultation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram shows significantly reduced ejection fraction (severely depressed LV systolic function). BNP markedly elevated. Chest X-ray shows enlarged heart silhouette, upper lobe pulmonary venous redistribution, and bilateral pleural effusions.

puzzles.explanation: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) post-MI with classic signs of fluid overload, orthopnoea, PND, elevated JVP, and severely reduced EF on echocardiography.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-year-old boy with insidious hip pain and antalgic gait; pain often referred to thigh and knee region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Antalgic gait, limitation of hip abduction and internal rotation. Weight-bearing difficult.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset, no trauma. Otherwise healthy, similar condition in maternal uncle.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Trendelenburg sign, hip flexion contracture. Limited internal rotation, pain on log roll test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows femoral head deformity with widened physis and early sclerosis; MRI reveals signal loss in femoral epiphysis consistent with bone ischemia.

puzzles.explanation: Legg-Calvé-Perthes is idiopathic avascular necrosis of the femoral head epiphysis in children, causing hip pain and gait disturbance.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Cancer survivor with gradually progressive numbness and burning pain in the feet, starting during treatment and continuing for 2 years after.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk barefoot on uneven ground, frequent falls, difficulty with fine motor tasks like fastening buttons, sleep disrupted by foot pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Single-agent platinum exposure for gynecological malignancy completed 2 years ago. Neuropathy onset coincided with treatment cycles, has not improved despite stopping chemotherapy. No prior peripheral neuropathy or diabetes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked sensory loss distally, weakness of foot intrinsics, absent reflexes, protective gait, hyperesthesia to touch.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG/NCS shows distal lower-limb axonal sensorimotor neuropathy with reduced amplitudes and normal conduction velocities, consistent with length-dependent pattern. No active denervation.

puzzles.explanation: Platinum-induced chronic peripheral neuropathy persisting long after chemotherapy completion with significant functional impairment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Sudden severe pain in the back of the lower leg during a pushing or jumping movement. Patient heard or felt a pop.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk normally. Refuses to put weight on the leg and drags foot when attempting to move. Cannot plantarflex the ankle actively.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient was pushing off to jump or sprint when injury occurred. No prior ankle or calf problems. Age 45.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Thompson test, palpable gap in tendon, complete loss of plantarflexion strength and severe swelling with ecchymosis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI confirms tendon discontinuity and imaging shows acute rupture with gap between tendon ends. Hemorrhage visible.

puzzles.explanation: Achilles tendon rupture is a complete traumatic injury requiring urgent assessment and typically surgical repair.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old with gradual onset of ankle discomfort and stiffness over 3-4 months. No recent trauma recalled. Pain described as deep, achy, worse at end of range in multiple directions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Progressive loss of ankle mobility affecting gait. Difficulty going up stairs and walking on uneven ground. Symptoms worse with attempts to stretch or move ankle to end-range; little relief from rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset without clear injury. Minimal improvement despite 2 months of standard ankle strengthening and stretching. No significant swelling, redness, or systemic symptoms. No prior ankle surgery or prolonged immobilization.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked restriction in plantarflexion, dorsiflexion, inversion, and eversion. End-feel capsular (hard, non-elastic). Ankle mortise and subtalar joints both restricted. Pain at extreme ranges. No effusion, no instability, neurovascular intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs normal. MRI shows synovial proliferation and fibrosis within talocrural joint capsule with thickened joint lining. No fracture, no cartilage loss, no ligament rupture.

puzzles.explanation: Frozen ankle is a rare but recognized complication involving ankle joint capsule fibrosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older patient with chronic knee pain and recurrent episodes of giving way during daily activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Stairs, uneven terrain provoke instability, patient has adapted with activity avoidance, and no recent acute injury recalled.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Remote injury from years prior, progressive compensatory movement patterns, and concurrent symptoms of mechanical catching.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive anterior drawer and Lachman tests with firm endpoints. Moderate quadriceps atrophy. Reports brief episodes of knee 'locking' or catching sensation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows degenerative appearance of ACL remnant with bone marrow edema and secondary osteoarthritis changes.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic ACL insufficiency often presents with subtle instability and secondary degenerative changes rather than acute symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 9-year-old girl presents with recurring joint pain affecting multiple joints and fatigue. Her parents report she bruises easily and has always seemed more flexible than her peers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Ability to perform unusual contortions, frequent joint dislocations that worsen with movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong excessive flexibility noted in childhood; multiple family members with similar presentation and skin fragility. Prior treatments have been mainly supportive.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Beighton score 7/9; marked passive range of motion in fingers, thumbs, and trunk flexion. Skin appears thin and translucent over joints with visible venous pattern. Multiple small scars and residual bruising on forearms and shins.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows no structural joint damage or ligamentous injury despite recurrent dislocations. Genetic testing unavailable in routine practice; diagnosis based on clinical criteria and family history consistent with heritable connective tissue disorder.

puzzles.explanation: Hypermobility syndrome from connective tissue disorder involves excessive joint flexibility predisposing to subluxations and pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports persistent muscle pain in multiple body areas; symptoms don't follow a consistent nerve or dermatomal pattern.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain sustained posture or repetitive activity; symptoms worsen with prolonged muscle engagement but improve with rest and movement variation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms developed insidiously over months to years; patient reports significant psychological stress and poor sleep quality; multiple specialists consulted with no structural diagnosis identified.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Widespread taut bands, multiple trigger points in different muscles, variable referred pain patterns.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Normal nerve conduction studies and imaging; symptoms don't follow dermatome distribution.

puzzles.explanation: Myofascial pain syndrome shows chronic, multi-regional presentation mimicking neuropathic pain but with trigger point findings on examination.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 78-year-old with limited mobility. Reports tender, painful area on lower back that has worsened over the past 3 weeks. Denies trauma. Area feels warm and looks red.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Spends 20+ hours daily in bed or wheelchair due to stroke. Cannot reposition independently. Pain worsens with pressure and improves only when weight is fully relieved from affected area.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Immobile for 4 months post-stroke. Poor oral intake, albumin low. Incontinent of urine. History of diabetes. Skin care and repositioning attempted but inconsistently maintained due to staffing limitations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Well-demarcated erythematous area 6 cm diameter over sacrum. Skin surface intact in centre but area feels boggy; shallow fluid-filled blister or very thin skin layer visible. Surrounding skin blanches with pressure; central area does not. Non-purulent serous drainage on dressing. No exposed subcutaneous tissue or bone. Temperature normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows fluid collection in dermis/subcutaneous tissue with intact skin barrier at some points and partial-thickness loss at others. No fascial involvement, no gas in tissues. Consistent with partial-thickness skin loss with shallow undermining.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced pressure injuries with extensive tissue involvement require multidisciplinary management including wound care, offloading, and infection control.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 16-year-old boy with progressive difficulty climbing stairs and rising from chairs over the past 2 years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Recently started using wheelchair for community mobility; reports shortness of breath on minimal exertion and occasional chest discomfort with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset age 12–13 with insidious progression. No trauma or viral illness preceding symptoms. Progressive decline despite physiotherapy and bracing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe proximal and generalized weakness, minimal movement in lower extremities, cardiac murmur, respiratory muscle weakness. Parents note similar pattern in maternal uncle (deceased at age 20).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram shows dilated cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction and EMG shows myopathic pattern. Elevated CK >40,000.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced Duchenne muscular dystrophy with severe disability, cardiac involvement, and respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged patient with ankle pain and swelling after a twisting injury during walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate uneven ground or lateral ankle weight-bearing; ankle becomes stiff and swollen with activity despite normal ROM in plantarflexion and dorsiflexion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 3 days ago. No prior ankle injuries or instability. No systemic disease or inflammatory history. No current medications affecting healing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lateral ankle tenderness,, anterior drawer test positive, limited range of motion with inversion, and plantarflexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays rule out fracture and ultrasound shows ligament. MRI demonstrates partial ligament tear with surrounding inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Grade 1-2 lateral ankle sprains cause pain and swelling but maintain functional mobility with appropriate management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient presents with posterior knee pain and vague 'heaviness' in the lower leg during prolonged sitting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms exacerbated by activities requiring sustained knee flexion and minimal acute instability but functional impairment noted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Remote or poorly recalled mechanism; patient notes gradual onset of calf swelling and tightness over weeks to months. Minimal initial trauma recall.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Posterior tibial sag at 90° knee flexion; posterior drawer test positive but less pronounced than expected for acute injury. Quadriceps atrophy and weakness with positive lag sign. Hip external rotators weak.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows PCL fibre disruption or avulsion from tibial insertion; secondary posterolateral knee OA and posterior capsular thickening. No ACL or meniscal involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic PCL insufficiency may present insidiously with posterior pain and weakness rather than acute instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Progressive lower back pain with bilateral leg symptoms and functional decline over recent weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Worsening despite rest, night prominent, and progressive functional decline.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent spinal pathology with constitutional symptoms (weight loss, fever). Acute worsening over past 5 days despite analgesia.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lower limb neurological exam: grade 4/5 bilateral hip flexors and ankle dorsiflexors. Saddle anaesthesia present. Diminished anal tone. Lower limb reflexes variable. Straight leg raise bilaterally limited by pain at 45 degrees.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows metastatic disease with epidural spinal cord compression at lumbar spine.

puzzles.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome is caused by metastatic disease requiring oncology consultation and emergency decompression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Adolescent athlete with anterior knee pain and localized swelling below the kneecap, worse with jumping and sprinting during a period of rapid growth.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to play sport comfortably; pain worsens with running, jumping, and ascending stairs. Symptoms plateau during periods of slower growth but flare with accelerated growth spurts.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Involved in basketball and volleyball for 2+ years; recently increased training intensity. Reports bilateral quadriceps and hamstring tightness. No prior knee injury or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Prominent tibial tuberosity with localized tenderness, knee extension against resistance reproduces, and palpable apophyseal prominence.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays reveal irregular fragmentation and calcification at the tibial tuberosity apophysis. MRI shows bone marrow edema and soft tissue inflammation localized to the apophyseal insertion without intra-articular pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Osgood-Schlatter disease is a self-limiting apophysitis common in growing adolescent athletes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports pain at the base of the great toe, worse with prolonged walking and footwear pressure.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens significantly with walking on hard surfaces and prolonged standing; patient avoids certain footwear due to discomfort at medial foot.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual development of deformity and family history common. Often begins in adolescence or early adulthood may worsen with pregnancy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible medial deviation of great toe and bony prominence at first metatarsal head. Bursa inflammation may be present possible second toe crowding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays show increased intermetatarsal angle, hallux valgus angle, joint degenerative changes possible, and assess first metatarsal position.

puzzles.explanation: Hallux valgus is lateral deviation of the great toe with medial first metatarsal prominence causing bunion deformity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old with acute onset knee pain and swelling after a fall from height landing on one leg. Pain is sharp and persistent; unable to bear weight comfortably on the affected side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk without significant pain and antalgic gait. Knee swells rapidly after activity. Pain worsens with weight-bearing and any attempt to straighten the leg fully. Sitting with knee flexed provides some relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy mechanism: fell 8 feet onto flexed knee. Injury occurred 3 weeks ago. History of osteoporosis (on calcium supplementation only, no bisphosphonates). Immediate swelling and ecchymosis. No prior knee surgery or instability episodes before this event.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant joint effusion present. Pain at joint line on palpation. McMurray's test negative. Lachman and anterior drawer tests negative. Valgus and varus stress tests stable. ROM limited by pain; flexion contracture of 15 degrees. Crepitus felt on passive extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT knee shows depressed fracture of lateral tibial plateau with 8mm step-off of articular surface. Comminution present in posterolateral corner. No soft tissue entrapment. Bone density reduced consistent with osteoporosis; minimal healing callus at 3 weeks post-injury.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic tibial plateau fracture sequelae often involve post-traumatic arthritis and ligamentous laxity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old man presents with radial-sided wrist pain and functional loss following a fall six months ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports pain and weakness with gripping and twisting activities; avoids weight-bearing through the wrist.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall from height was the inciting event. Patient reports persistent wrist stiffness with no history of inflammatory arthritis or prior hand surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked tenderness in the radial wrist anatomical space, restricted radial wrist deviation, and significantly diminished grip strength. Scaphoid compression test positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT imaging demonstrates scaphoid non-union with sclerotic fracture margins. MRI reveals avascular necrosis of the proximal pole.

puzzles.explanation: Scaphoid nonunion with possible avascular necrosis, chronic presentation from inadequate initial treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Office worker reports eye strain, dry eyes, and headache after prolonged screen time.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worsen with prolonged computer use; improve with reduced screen time and frequent breaks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Unable to maintain near vision comfort for extended periods; symptoms persist despite distance breaks, but resolve with overnight rest.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced tear break-up time, conjunctival injection, accommodation lag, dry eye findings.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Normal ophthalmologic imaging; dry eye testing confirms reduced tear production or quality.

puzzles.explanation: Computer vision syndrome shows occupational eye strain from prolonged screen use with dry eye component.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 67-year-old with hypertension reports sudden onset of heart pounding and shortness of breath. Symptoms began at rest during evening. No chest pain. Denies recent illness, trauma, or medication changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Episodes of palpitations occur unpredictably—both at rest and with minimal exertion. Patient reports feeling lightheaded. Symptoms do not consistently worsen or improve with body position, breathing pattern, or activity level.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset 2 days ago, sudden and persistent. Past medical history: hypertension (10 years), chronic kidney disease stage 3. No prior cardiac events, syncope, or arrhythmia. Currently on antihypertensive medication. No recent infections or fever.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Heart rate 110 bpm, irregularly irregular rhythm palpated at radial pulse. Blood pressure elevated at 156/94. Apex beat difficult to locate. Lung bases clear bilaterally. No peripheral edema. Capillary refill normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: 12-lead ECG: absent P waves, irregular RR intervals with irregularly irregular QRS complexes, fibrillatory baseline at 300-600 bpm. Troponin negative. Chest X-ray normal. Transthoracic echo shows normal ejection fraction but left atrial enlargement.

puzzles.explanation: Acute atrial fibrillation presentation with rapid ventricular response and hemodynamic compromise. ECG confirms diagnosis with characteristic findings. Hypertension and CKD are predisposing factors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Child presenting with heel pain that worsens with sports activity, particularly running and jumping. Pain localised to posterior heel.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate sustained running or jumping. Symptoms improve with activity reduction but return within days of resuming sport. No night pain or resting symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Occurs during growth spurts, typically 8-14 years old. High activity level, tight gastrocnemius-soleus complex.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness at calcaneal insertion of Achilles, pain with passive dorsiflexion. Tight calf muscles, pain with squeezing heel.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays may show fragmented apophysis, MRI shows apophyseal inflammation. Self-limited condition resolving with growth plate closure.

puzzles.explanation: Sever's disease is a self-limiting calcaneal apophysitis common in growing athletic children.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports persistent dizziness and imbalance 6 weeks after an acute viral illness. Initial severe spinning sensation has largely resolved, but positional symptoms and difficulty with spatial orientation remain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports feeling unsteady in crowded or visually complex environments. Gait on uneven surfaces is slow and cautious. Recovers well with rest and visual fixation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute viral illness 6 weeks ago with initial severe vertigo and nausea. Hearing decline noted; audiometry confirms high-frequency sensorineural loss. No recent head trauma or recurrent episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Dix-Hallpike now negative, Spontaneous nystagmus absent, Romberg test positive but improved from acute phase, Gait shows cautious pattern but not severely restricted, Dynamic gait testing shows deficits with head turns.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows mild residual labyrinthine enhancement with overall resolving inflammation. No central lesions or structural abnormality. Audiometry confirms high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss consistent with inner ear injury.

puzzles.explanation: Post-acute labyrinthitis requires vestibular rehabilitation emphasizing gait training, balance retraining with visual and proprioceptive compensation, habituation to remaining symptoms, and psychological support for chronic dizziness adjustment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old reports recurrent shoulder dislocation episodes with minimal provocation, recently increasing in frequency.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to throw or lift arm overhead without fear of dislocation. Certain arm positions (particularly abduction and external rotation) trigger immediate apprehension and guarding.

clues.label: History
clues.text: First dislocation occurred 5 years ago, initially requiring manual reduction at ED; now dislocates several times monthly with trivial movements. No significant trauma history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive apprehension test and relocation test. Positive sulcus sign. Generalized ligamentous laxity noted on examination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows labral damage with Hill-Sachs lesion, glenoid bone loss. Chronic instability changes.

puzzles.explanation: Recurrent anterior instability with established labral pathology and bone loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Road cyclist reports hand numbness affecting ring and little fingers after long rides.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms reproducible with sustained grip on drop bars; relief occurs with wrist extension or lateral deviation, and with padded gloves. Cannot sustain long rides without symptom escalation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive over 6 months with increased training volume and hours on bike. No trauma or prior hand/wrist injury. No systemic symptoms or neck pain. Symptoms worse with more aggressive riding positions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Tinel's test over ulnar nerve at wrist, weakness in intrinsic hand muscles, sensory loss in ulnar distribution.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show ulnar nerve slowing at wrist; ultrasound shows compression site.

puzzles.explanation: Cyclist's palsy shows ulnar nerve compression at wrist from prolonged cycling hand pressure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 30-year-old female presents with unilateral left-sided buttock pain that she points to with one finger over the posterior superior iliac spine.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: is worse with prolonged standing, walking uphill, and rolling over in bed. Relieved by sitting. Reports when stepping up onto a curb with the left leg.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 4 months postpartum. Vaginal delivery with prolonged second stage of labour. Reports her pelvis felt 'unstable' since delivery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Three or more SIJ provocation tests positive: compression test, thigh thrust (posterior shear), Gaenslen's test, and sacral thrust. Positive active straight leg raise (ASLR) test bilaterally. Lumbar spine examination unremarkable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays are normal. MRI shows mild bone marrow oedema at the left SIJ without erosions or sclerosis, consistent with inflammatory changes rather than structural pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Postpartum SIJ dysfunction is common due to ligamentous laxity from relaxin. Diagnosis requires 3+ positive provocation tests to achieve adequate specificity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old male tradesperson presents with anterior knee pain and swelling of two weeks duration. Symptoms began without a specific injury incident.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with kneeling on knees; cannot kneel for extended periods without significant discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms triggered by work activities involving sustained kneeling. No acute injury recalled; symptoms developed insidiously over several weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable swelling anterior to proximal tibia; tenderness localized deep to patellar tendon. No effusion in knee joint; passive knee flexion reproduces anterior pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates bursal enlargement with fluid collection.

puzzles.explanation: Infrapatellar bursitis occurs from repetitive kneeling in a kneeling position.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes × 12 years reports a non-healing wound on the sole of their foot that began 8 weeks ago. Patient notes drainage from the site and difficulty walking. No clear memory of initial injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear weight on affected foot. Significant functional limitation with ambulation and balance. Drainage soaks through dressings daily. Pain is minimal despite wound size and appearance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Wound onset unclear—patient discovered lesion rather than recalling trauma. HbA1c 9.2% at last visit. Known peripheral neuropathy for 3 years. Prior foot ulcer on opposite foot 5 years ago, healed with conservative care. No recent vascular interventions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Large ulcer (≥2 cm) on plantar surface with slough and exposed tissue. Surrounding erythema and induration present. Monofilament test: unable to sense 5.07g in forefoot and toes bilaterally. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses palpable but diminished. Ankle-brachial index 0.68.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Foot X-ray shows bone demineralization and loss of cortical definition at metatarsal heads. MRI reveals T2 signal hyperintensity in subcutaneous tissues and marrow edema. Probe-to-bone test positive. Bone culture grows methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus.

puzzles.explanation: Complicated diabetic foot ulcer with infection and vascular insufficiency requires urgent multispecialty management including infectious disease, vascular surgery, wound specialist, glycemic control, and preparation for potential amputation with rehabilitation planning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 34-year-old woman presents with widespread joint pain and fatigue. Reports dizziness when standing up quickly and has noticed her skin is unusually stretchy.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with sustained standing and aerobic activities. Fatigue out of proportion to exertion level. Symptoms worsen significantly with physical activity despite attempting graded exercise.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong history of joint sprains and dislocations. Multiple hospitalizations in childhood for unexplained bleeding. Family history of hypermobility and connective tissue problems. No recent trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Beighton score 7/9. Skin velvety and translucent with visible veining. Tachycardia (104 bpm) at rest, blood pressure drops 18 mmHg on standing. Joint palpation reveals generalized laxity without inflammation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Genetic testing confirms EDS; imaging shows degenerative changes despite young age.

puzzles.explanation: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is a complex systemic presentation with significant autonomic and multisystem involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old with severe initial COVID requiring ICU admission reports ongoing dyspnea and reduced exercise tolerance 6 months later.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot return to work due to dyspnea with stairs. Exercise intolerance marked. Persistent cough.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Severe COVID with pneumonia and 10 days mechanical ventilation. Post-discharge: progressive fatigue worsened by minimal exertion, night sweats, and brain fog. Gradual decline over 6 months despite apparent medical recovery. No prior cardiopulmonary disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Decreased respiratory muscle strength. Able to walk 200 meters before dyspnea onset. Oxygen saturation drops to 92% with exertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest CT shows resolved pulmonary edema but persistent ground-glass opacities suggesting ongoing inflammation. PFTs show reduced DLCO and exercise-induced desaturation.

puzzles.explanation: Severe Long COVID presentation following critical illness. Combines persistent respiratory impairment with ICU-acquired weakness and significant functional limitation requiring rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Cyclist with chronic pain at ischium; activity-dependent discomfort.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Worse during intervals and climbs; improved with cadence reduction and seat height adjustment.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She has a background of recurrent sacroiliac joint pain and recently increased her cycling training volume by 40% over eight weeks. No prior hamstring injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized ischial tenderness, positive Puranen-Orava test. Pain with resisted hip flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows hypoechoic swelling and vascularity within proximal hamstring tendon at ischial insertion; dynamic imaging reproduces pain with hip flexion against resistance.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic hamstring tendinopathy in cyclists often relates to bike setup and loading mechanics.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old male with progressive pain in hands and feet, associated with skin changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Morning stiffness lasting over 1 hour. Pain primarily in DIP joints of hands and forefoot. Symptoms improve with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Skin lesions preceded joint symptoms by approximately 1 year. No prior joint disease. Exacerbations correlate with high stress periods. Father has inflammatory arthritis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Psoriatic skin plaques on extensor surfaces. Nail pitting and onycholysis visible. Ray-like swelling of fingers (sausage digits).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows pencil-in-cup deformities and osteolysis. Asymmetric joint involvement. MRI shows inflammation and bone erosions.

puzzles.explanation: Psoriatic Arthritis is the classic presentation with visible skin manifestations preceding or accompanying joint disease with characteristic asymmetric pattern and bone changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with severe heel pain following a fall from standing height.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe pain with heel weight-bearing, massive swelling. Unable to ambulate.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy mechanism (3-metre fall onto hard surface). Immediate swelling and inability to weight-bear from time of injury. No significant comorbidities or prior heel pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe diffuse heel swelling with ecchymosis. Loss of normal heel contour. Positive bulge sign. Severe pain on passive ankle dorsiflexion and palpation of heel. Calf tenderness present; compartment syndrome suspected clinically.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT scan shows intra-articular comminution, loss of Bohler's angle. Subtalar involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Calcaneal fractures are severe injuries with significant morbidity and risk of chronic pain and dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with hand, numbness in ring, and little fingers progressing over months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform pinch grip or manipulate small objects. Difficulty with finger spreading and coordinated finger movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset over several months. Occupational: prolonged computer work with elbow in flexion. No acute trauma or significant injury history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Interosseous atrophy, weakness of finger abduction and adduction, sensory loss in ulnar distribution, Froment sign positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows slowing across elbow, ultrasound shows nerve thickening at cubital tunnel, and mRI shows nerve compression.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic ulnar nerve entrapment at cubital tunnel with progressive motor loss, intrinsic hand atrophy, and confirmed compression on imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young patient develops foot pain and limited ankle motion during adolescence.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: During walking and sports, patient cannot invert or evert the foot; ankle feels stiff and rigid rather than mobile.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset age 10-14, family history of foot problems, and progressive symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Special tests reveal severely restricted subtalar motion in isolation; ankle dorsi/plantarflexion preserved. Foot appears persistently flat with rigid midfoot. Heel varus present on weight-bearing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT scan shows talocalcaneal or talonavicular coalition and osseous or fibrocartilaginous bridge.

puzzles.explanation: Tarsal coalition is an abnormal bone or cartilage bridge between tarsal bones causing pain and motion limitation during growth.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Teenager reports mild back discomfort and notices uneven clothing fit.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient continues to participate fully in sports and physical education without restrictions or symptom exacerbation during activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Curve detected at age 10, slowly progressive over 3 years, and still growing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left lumbar scoliosis with rib hump, mild pain with spinal palpation, and flexible curve.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serial X-rays show Cobb angle progression from 22 to 35 degrees and immature skeletal age.

puzzles.explanation: Progressive idiopathic scoliosis in growing adolescents may require bracing to halt progression and prevent the need for surgery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with severe back pain unrelieved by position changes and nocturnal pain present.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain upright posture; symptoms persist despite rest and positional modifications.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fever and systemic malaise present. Risk factors include immunosuppression, recent infection, IV drug use, or recent spinal procedure.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Midline vertebral tenderness over affected segment. Possible lower extremity neurological deficit or myelopathic signs if cord compression present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows disc space narrowing, vertebral body involvement, and inflammatory signal changes with endplate involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Spondylodiscitis is a spinal infection requiring emergency recognition and treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 38-year-old right-handed office worker. Wrist pain with intermittent swelling. Onset gradual over past 2 years. No systemic symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with gripping and sustained typing. Clicking or clunking sensation during wrist movement. Symptoms worse with radial/ulnar deviation. Can tolerate light activities but heavy lifting limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall on outstretched hand 2 years ago. Initially treated conservatively with rest and splinting. Symptoms improved initially then plateaued; never fully resolved. No prior wrist injury. No inflammatory arthropathy history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive midcarpal clunk test with radial deviation. Grip strength reduced 20-30% compared to opposite side. Palpable clunking during passive wrist motion in midcarpal region. Pain on axial thumb loading. Wrist ROM mildly restricted in extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Wrist X-rays (AP, lateral, scaphoid views): normal carpal alignment, no fracture lines, no scaphoid waist gap, no SLAC pattern. MRI: partial tear of dorsal intercarpal and radiocarpal ligaments; preserved scaphoid integrity; mild synovitis without cartilage loss.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic wrist sprain with scapholunate instability and carpal malalignment from inadequate healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Healthcare or animal facility worker reports progressive respiratory symptoms at work.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms resolve on days off and weekends, recur upon return to workplace.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive sensitization over months to years and initial symptoms mild but worsening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Baseline forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV1) reduced; significant reversibility or fluctuation noted across shift timings; wheezing audible during work-shift samples.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Peak flow diary shows ≥20% variability between work days and non-work days; specific IgE elevated to workplace-relevant allergen; baseline spirometry shows obstructive pattern consistent with chronic exposure effect.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational asthma shows allergic sensitization occupational asthma with progressive airway obstruction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient woke unable to lift hand at wrist. Arm was in unusual compressed position during sleep. No preceding trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot extend wrist or fingers. Wrist held in flexion (droops downward). Has numbness over back of hand and thumb.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset after sleeping on arm in compressed position across armchair. No trauma, no prior episodes. No sensory or motor symptoms before sleep.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wrist drop present. Finger extension severely weak (grades 1-2). Elbow extension normal strength. Sensory loss confined to first web space and dorsal thumb. No other neurological deficits distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows conduction block at mid-humerus and ultrasound shows nerve compression. X-ray normal.

puzzles.explanation: Acute radial nerve compression palsy from prolonged pressure with classic wrist drop and sensory loss in superficial radial nerve distribution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports chest wall pain, described as sharp or aching. Chronic onset over weeks to months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Movements involving trunk rotation and deep breathing exacerbate pain; difficulty with overhead reaching and lifting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset following repeated heavy lifting 3 months ago. No acute trauma. Previous similar episodes resolved with rest. No systemic illness or referred symptoms from viscera.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Focal tenderness over lateral chest wall, between ribs 4–6. Pain reproduces with palpation and resisted trunk rotation. Normal neurological examination. Costochondral junctions non-tender.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates muscle oedema and minor scarring in external intercostal muscles at symptomatic level with no nerve root compression; confirms musculoskeletal origin without fracture or pneumothorax.

puzzles.explanation: Intercostal muscle strain is chronic with potential nerve involvement (intercostal neuralgia).

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old presenting with gradual-onset pain in the ball of the foot. Describes aching and burning sensation that worsens as the day progresses.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain aggravated by prolonged standing and walking, particularly in tight or narrow footwear. Symptoms ease with rest and wider-fitting shoes. No specific injury recalled.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 3 months ago without trauma. Patient reports a sedentary job but increased activity recently. No prior foot surgery or fracture. Pain has been gradually worsening despite activity modification and rest.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized tenderness over the 2nd and 3rd metatarsal heads. Reduced plantar intrinsic muscle strength bilaterally. Pain reproduced with compression across metatarsal heads (metatarsal squeeze test). ROM of great toe normal but plantarflexion-loaded positions uncomfortable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing anteroposterior foot radiographs show subtle narrowing of the 2nd-3rd metatarsophalangeal joint spaces and early plantar callus formation under those heads. MRI reveals chronic stress changes in the plantar plate with intact ligamentous structures. No fracture or arthropathy.

puzzles.explanation: Secondary metatarsalgia may result from biomechanical dysfunction of adjacent joints.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 20-month-old child. Parents report child walks on outer border of foot. Foot appears twisted inward and downward. No history of trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Child ambulates with weight-bearing on lateral foot surface. Difficulty achieving heel strike during gait. Appears to walk on toes and outer edge of sole.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Deformity present since birth. Parents received initial treatment recommendation in neonatal period but discontinued intervention at 6 months. No orthotic device used since treatment stopped.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Rigid forefoot adduction and inversion. Ankle plantarflexion present but limited dorsiflexion. Heel in varus position. Medial foot crease visible. Calf musculature atrophic. No associated pain or swelling.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing foot radiographs show talus deviated laterally, calcaneus in varus with increased pitch angle, navicular displaced medially. Metatarsals adducted relative to midfoot axis.

puzzles.explanation: Recurrent clubfoot deformity from inadequate treatment requires repeat intervention and compliance with dynamic splinting protocols.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young person with acute chest wall pain, worse with movement and breathing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by deep breathing, coughing, and direct pressure to the chest wall. She avoids lying on her left side and finds relief only with complete rest and analgesia.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset over days without preceding trauma. Multiple investigations performed to rule out cardiac and pulmonary causes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible swelling and erythema at the costochondral junctions. Reproducible tenderness on direct palpation of affected cartilage. No cardiac or respiratory findings.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows no rib fractures, malignancy, or cardiac pathology. Costochondral changes consistent with benign inflammatory swelling.

puzzles.explanation: Costochondritis is Tietze's syndrome with visible swelling requiring imaging confirmation to rule out serious pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient presents with persistent lateral ankle pain and feeling of ankle instability following an inversion sprain three months ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform lateral ankle proprioceptive tasks; difficulty with stairs and single-leg stance; gives way on direction changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Follows ankle inversion injury and persistent despite ankle rehabilitation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lateral hindfoot tenderness with deep palpation between talus and calcaneus; positive talar tilt and anterior drawer; pain with inversion stress; subtalar joint mobility reduced.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates oedema and inflammatory changes localized to subtalar coalition region with associated synovitis; weightbearing CT reveals subtle talocalcaneal rotation and reduced posterior facet alignment.

puzzles.explanation: Sinus tarsi syndrome results from inflammation and instability in the subtalar joint after ankle sprain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 32-year-old female with insidious onset polyarticular pain and swelling in hands, wrists, and knees over 6 months. Associated fatigue, low-grade fevers, and unintentional weight loss (3–4 kg). No known trauma or recent infection.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant morning stiffness lasting 1–2 hours. Difficulty gripping objects and performing fine motor tasks. Symptoms worse with sustained activity; some joints improve with rest, others remain persistently swollen. Reports worsening with sun exposure.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms fluctuate over weeks and months. Family history notable for autoimmune disease. No prior joint trauma, infection trigger, or occupational exposure. Currently taking no disease-modifying therapies. Reports recurrent mouth ulcers and occasional facial redness in sun-exposed areas.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Symmetric swelling and warmth at MCP and PIP joints bilaterally. Reduced grip strength (15 mmHg). No joint deformity or subluxation. Oral ulceration on hard palate noted. No nodules, effusions, or systemic lymphadenopathy palpated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain film radiographs of hands and wrists show no erosions, joint-space narrowing, or bone deformity. Positive ANA with anti-dsDNA antibodies and low complement levels (C3, C4). Negative rheumatoid factor. Urinalysis shows mild proteinuria and dysmorphic RBC casts.

puzzles.explanation: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus is the classic presentation in a young female with characteristic malar rash, non-erosive arthritis, and positive serology including ANA and dsDNA antibodies.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged female with sharp lateral hip pain after increasing walking distance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain reproducible with side-lying; aggravated by crossing legs while standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in running or gym activity; gluteal weakness noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Exquisite tenderness at trochanter, positive lateral hip pain test. Weak hip abduction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals gluteal medius tendinopathy with associated bursal inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Activity-related GTPS often involves functional weakness of hip stabilizers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old female office worker presents with pain in her left buttock that radiates down the back of her thigh. She has been treated for sciatica but her lumbar MRI was normal.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged sitting, crossing legs, and climbing stairs. Driving is uncomfortable. Pain eases with standing and walking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms present for 4 months. No low back pain. No trauma but increased running recently.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness on deep palpation reproducing buttock and leg symptoms. Positive FAIR test (flexion, adduction, internal rotation). Positive Pace sign.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI pelvis shows mild hypertrophy and oedema of the piriformis muscle with close relationship to the sciatic nerve. MR neurography suggests nerve irritation at that level.

puzzles.explanation: Piriformis syndrome (deep gluteal syndrome) causing extra-spinal sciatic symptoms with a normal lumbar spine, positive piriformis-specific provocation tests, and MRI showing the piriformis-sciatic nerve relationship.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older individual with persistent lateral hip pain, sharp or burning in character, limiting activity and function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged standing, walking on inclines, and lying on affected side. No significant morning stiffness.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic presentation; recent increase in activity or prolonged walking. No acute trauma or hip surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Trendelenburg sign; palpable tenderness over greater trochanter; pain with resisted hip abduction and external rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows thickened bursa with fluid; gluteus medius tendon appears hypoechoic with areas of tendinopathy but no full-thickness tear.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic GTPS often reflects underlying biomechanical issues and gait abnormalities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Athlete with acute posterior thigh pain during sprinting or kicking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear weight through affected leg; limping or favoring limb immediately after injury and unable to resume activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No significant prior symptoms and high-speed activity or overstretching mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible bruising in posterior thigh and palpable gap or indentation in muscle belly. Positive straight leg raise and reproduction with hamstring stretch.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI confirms muscle tear with retraction and fluid collection.

puzzles.explanation: Hamstring strain typically occurs during explosive sports movements, with ecchymosis and immediate functional loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with incidental finding of posterior knee mass and minimal or no symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Mass may feel tense or fuller with knee extension, may fluctuate with activity level, or patient notes transient swelling that resolves with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cyst present for extended period without change and no acute episodes. Underlying knee pathology may be minimal; often found incidentally on imaging ordered for other reasons.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable cyst without significant tenderness, primary knee examination largely normal, and size stable neurovascular intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI confirms simple cyst with no complex features. Primary knee joint may be normal or show minimal degenerative changes.

puzzles.explanation: Asymptomatic Baker's cysts are common incidental findings and may not require intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old with leg pain and occasional numbness. Symptoms started gradually 8 months ago and have been worsening over the past 2 months despite initial improvement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain radiates down the leg with certain movements. Bending forward sometimes helps, sometimes doesn't. Pain worsens with repetitive activity. Difficulty with stairs and prolonged sitting or standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Had lumbar surgery (disc herniation repair) 18 months ago. First 6 months were good with steady improvement. Then gradual return of leg symptoms without clear trigger. No new trauma or falls.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Spinal movement is stiff and limited, worse in some directions. Leg strength variable and inconsistent on repeat testing. Reflexes normal. Sensory changes don't follow a single clear nerve pattern. Nerve stretch tests produce leg pain but pattern is unpredictable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows post-surgical scar tissue at the operative level with the nerve root appearing tethered or bound to surrounding structures. No new disc herniation. No significant canal stenosis to account for current symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: Nerve root adhesion is a post-surgical complication with persistent radicular pain from scar formation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports heel pain that worsens after periods of rest, particularly noticeable with initial weight-bearing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate prolonged standing or running; symptoms improve transiently with calf stretching but recur with activity resumption.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset with increased running volume, associated with tight calves, and high-impact activities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Maximal tenderness along medial plantar fascia at heel insertion; pain reproduced with passive ankle plantarflexion and toe extension. Ankle dorsiflexion ROM limited to 5 degrees.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates plantar fascia thickness >4 mm at heel insertion with hypoechoic appearance consistent with inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Plantar fasciitis athlete shows typical running-related heel pain with fascial inflammation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 19-year-old female university student reports diffuse anterior knee pain that has gradually worsened over 2 months since starting running.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by going up and down stairs, squatting, and prolonged sitting with knees bent. Running downhill is worse than uphill.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No locking, giving way, or significant swelling. Increased her running volume by 40% over 3 weeks. Wears old running shoes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness on patellar facets with compression. Positive Clarke's test (patella grind). Increased dynamic valgus on single-leg squat.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray unremarkable. MRI shows no structural pathology — no cartilage defects, intact menisci and ligaments. Mild patellar lateral tilt on axial views.

puzzles.explanation: Classic patellofemoral pain in a young female runner with training error, proximal weakness, and characteristic 'movie sign' and stair pain. Imaging is typically unremarkable.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old presents with pain and swelling at the shoulder tip after direct blow.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with adduction across body (cross-body adduction); pain at top of shoulder.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mechanism was sudden collision; patient reports immediate onset. No prior shoulder injury or surgery. Unremarkable general health.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness at AC joint, positive AC joint compression test. Crossover test positive, assess grade of injury.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows AC joint displacement, assess for grade 1-3 injury. Comparison views helpful.

puzzles.explanation: Acute AC joint sprain from direct trauma with typical mechanism and clinical findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old with sudden severe elbow pain and visible deformity after a fall onto an outstretched arm during sport.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot flex or extend elbow; extreme difficulty locating elbow joint contour due to gross swelling and visible deformity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Immediate swelling within minutes. No prior elbow injury or instability. High-energy impact; patient did not attempt to relocate the elbow.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Elbow held in 45° flexion, resistant to passive movement. Posterolateral bony prominence palpable. Radial and ulnar pulses present; sensory intact in radial, ulnar, and median distributions. No crepitus.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Anteroposterior and lateral X-rays show posterior displacement of ulna and radius relative to humerus. Coronoid process and radial head intact. No associated fractures.

puzzles.explanation: Acute posterior elbow dislocation from typical fall mechanism requiring urgent reduction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Athlete reports sudden-onset pain in the groin region during a match, aggravated by explosive movements and change of direction.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with resisted leg crossing (adduction) and rapid acceleration; symptoms ease with rest but recur with return to sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute non-contact injury during acceleration or lateral movement; pain radiates along medial thigh.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Adductor tenderness, positive adductor squeeze test, pain with hip adduction against resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows muscle strain with hypoechoic area; MRI shows muscle fiber disruption and edema.

puzzles.explanation: Groin strain shows acute adductor muscle strain from kicking mechanism with positive squeeze test.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 62-year-old reports progressive finger deformity over several years. Affected fingers feel stiff and are adopting an unusual resting posture. No acute injury or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with precise hand tasks like buttoning or picking up small objects. Cannot fully extend affected fingers at the middle joint, though some flexion remains possible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Longstanding inflammatory arthritis diagnosed 8 years ago. Fingers affected one at a time over past 5 years; now involves thumb and two fingers. Gradual progression despite medical management.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: PIP joint held in fixed flexion (~90°), DIP joint in compensatory hyperextension. Active extension at PIP severely limited; passive extension also restricted. No focal trauma or skin breakdown noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand X-ray shows central slip tendon atrophy with PIP joint subluxation. MRI demonstrates central slip rupture at PIP level with intact lateral bands laterally displaced. Joint destruction consistent with chronic inflammatory disease.

puzzles.explanation: Secondary boutonniere deformity from chronic rheumatoid arthritis with progressive joint destruction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old woman presents 10 weeks after surgery for breast cancer. Reports progressive shoulder stiffness and reduced arm mobility. No acute trauma or fall. Pain is dull and restrictive rather than sharp.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to raise arm overhead or reach across body. Difficulty with fastening bra, combing hair, or lifting light objects. Symptoms worse with repetitive overhead movements. No neurological symptoms (numbness, tingling).

clues.label: History
clues.text: Underwent modified radical mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection. Surgical wound healed well. Did not receive adjuvant radiation. No significant shoulder pathology prior to surgery. Minimal physiotherapy since discharge.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shoulder internal rotation severely restricted (45° compared to 75° contralateral). External rotation mildly limited. Forward flexion reduced to 110°. No palpable mass or cord-like bands. Scapular dyskinesis noted. Strength 5/5 throughout. No sensory deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder ultrasound shows no rotator cuff tear, subacromial effusion, or biceps pathology. Axillary web syndrome not present on physical examination or imaging. MRI chest/axilla shows normal post-surgical changes only, no recurrence. Imaging findings support adhesive capsulitis secondary to surgical immobility.

puzzles.explanation: Post-mastectomy rehabilitation complicated by radiation therapy requires modified approaches to avoid skin damage, aggressive treatment of axillary cording, lymphedema management, and coordination with oncology team for optimal outcomes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old female violinist presents with diffuse arm pain, numbness in two of her fingers, and a sensation of heaviness in her right arm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are provoked by prolonged overhead activities and playing violin for more than 20 minutes. Reports occasional colour changes in her hand during cold weather.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lean build with postural forward head and rounded shoulders. History of whiplash 2 years ago. Symptoms worsening with increased rehearsal schedule over 12 months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Roos test (elevated arm stress test) at 90 seconds — reproduces symptoms and hand pallor. Positive Adson's test with diminished radial pulse. Tender anterior and middle scalenes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows a rudimentary cervical rib on the right. Doppler ultrasound demonstrates subclavian artery compression with arm abduction. Nerve conduction studies are normal.

puzzles.explanation: Neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome with vascular component, presenting with ulnar-distribution symptoms, provoked by overhead use, with cervical rib as a structural contributor.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 36-year-old reports persistent fatigue, dyspnea, and cognitive difficulties. Symptoms began 4 months ago after a mild acute COVID-19 infection.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Profound fatigue worsening with minimal exertion and lingering for days (post-exertional malaise). Dyspnea on walking. Difficulty concentrating at work.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Initially recovered from acute COVID-19 but failed to return to baseline function; decline occurred during apparent convalescence. Previously very active; now significantly limited. No other acute precipitant identified.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Vital signs and resting SpO2 normal. Formal cognitive testing shows measurable impairment (not subjective complaint). Heart rate response to minimal exertion assessed—check for abnormal chronotropic response.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows no acute consolidation or effusion. High-resolution CT of the chest demonstrates mild ground-glass opacities in the lower lobes bilaterally. Pulmonary function tests show a mild restrictive and diffusion-capacity defect.

puzzles.explanation: Typical Long COVID presentation with post-exertional malaise, cognitive dysfunction, and dyspnea persisting months after acute infection. Symptom variability and exclusion of organic pathology are characteristic.

clues.label: Complaint
