clues.text: Insidious onset without clear precipitant. No significant head injury history. No relevant medication changes. Symptoms unilateral at onset, now bilateral but asymmetric. Spouse notes patient moves less spontaneously and speaks more quietly.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Uniform resistance to passive movement throughout range, present in flexors and extensors equally. Movement initiation slow; rapid finger tapping reveals decrement in speed and amplitude. Posture slightly flexed. Gait narrow-based with reduced arm swing on one side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain normal; no structural lesions, infarcts, or hydrocephalus. Dopamine transporter SPECT scan shows bilateral reduction in striatal uptake, asymmetric, consistent with nigrostriatal denervation.

puzzles.explanation: Idiopathic Parkinson's disease with the classic triad of rest tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia, plus non-motor features (anosmia, REM sleep disorder, constipation) and asymmetric onset.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports hip pain and significant difficulty with lower limb weight-bearing and mobility. Onset was acute, following a surgical intervention several weeks ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot abduct the hip against gravity and demonstrates a compensatory limp. Lying flat and rolling in bed are particularly difficult. Sitting is limited to 30–45 minutes before pain worsens.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient underwent a major surgical procedure on the hip joint for advanced osteoarthritis. Recovery timeline is 6–12 weeks into rehabilitation. No prior hip pathology or surgery on the contralateral side. Comorbidities include mild hypertension, well controlled.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip abduction strength is 2/5 (poor, gravity-eliminated). Hip flexion ROM is 60°, abduction 20°. Pain is sharp and anterior with internal rotation. Glute med inhibition noted on palpation. No fever, wound healing normal. SLR positive for pain inhibition.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate appropriate prosthesis positioning and seating. Radiographs exclude mechanical complications.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Total Hip Replacement shows excellent long-term outcome at 18 months with nearly complete functional recovery and stable imaging findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A competitive athlete experiences sudden onset knee pain during rapid deceleration with a cutting maneuver in-sport. Reports immediate sensation of the knee 'giving way' and inability to continue activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports inability to perform plant and cut movements. Single-leg stance is unstable. Fear-avoidance behaviour limits weight-bearing activities, particularly on stairs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute injury during sport with non-contact mechanism—rapid direction change or pivoting on planted foot. The patient heard or felt an audible pop at moment of injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive Lachman test and anterior drawer test demonstrate anterior knee instability. Pivot shift test may be positive, indicating rotational instability.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates complete anterior cruciate ligament tear with characteristic signal discontinuity. MRI characterises associated injuries including meniscal tears and bone bruising patterns.

puzzles.explanation: ACL Grade 2 shows partial ligament tear with positive Lachman and drawer tests, preserved but damaged fibers on MRI with classic mechanism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports progressive numbness and tingling in the feet, gradually worsening over months. Some difficulty with fine motor control in the hands noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty walking on uneven surfaces and balance impairment, particularly when eyes are closed. Trips on stairs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Diabetes diagnosed 15 years ago, poorly controlled. Recent fatigue and weight loss. No prior neurological symptoms. Family history of autoimmune disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, sensory loss in stocking-glove distribution is demonstrated. Reflexes are diminished or absent. Weakness may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography demonstrates reduced amplitudes of compound muscle action potentials with normal conduction velocities, consistent with axonal degeneration. Nerve conduction study shows length-dependent pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Distal symmetric sensory diabetic polyneuropathy with typical distribution, functional impairment, and increased ulceration risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old warehouse worker presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the hand that worsens at night and affects sleep quality. The symptoms began insidiously 3 months ago without preceding trauma. Pain radiates from the elbow down to the ring and little fingers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports difficulty gripping tools at work and has dropped several items in the past month. Prolonged elbow flexion during computer work exacerbates symptoms, while extending the arm and shaking the hand provides temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient works assembly lines requiring repetitive elbow flexion. Past medical history includes rheumatoid arthritis controlled on medication. The patient denies neck pain or shoulder involvement. Symptoms are worse in the morning after sleeping with the elbow bent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tinel's sign is positive at the medial elbow. Froment's sign demonstrates weakness of thumb adduction. Intrinsic hand muscle atrophy is visible between thumb and index finger. Two-point discrimination is diminished in the ulnar distribution compared to the contralateral side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging shows enlargement and hypoechoic appearance of the ulnar nerve at the medial elbow, consistent with nerve swelling. The nerve measures 0.7 cm in cross-sectional area at the compression site, well above normal limits of 0.4 cm.

puzzles.explanation: Cubital tunnel syndrome secondary to osteoarthritis with prominent motor findings and muscle atrophy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An adolescent athlete reports anterior knee pain that worsens with physical activity, particularly jumping and running sports.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot kneel without discomfort, and pain is reproducibly worse with activities involving forceful knee extension or explosive movements. Sitting with knee bent for prolonged periods is tolerated.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The adolescent is typically 11-18 years old during growth spurt. Symptoms correlate with periods of rapid growth. Sport involvement is common.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, swelling and tenderness are present at the tibial tuberosity. Pain is reproduced with knee extension against resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may show tibial tuberosity enlargement and fragmentation. MRI demonstrates tibial tuberosity inflammation and soft tissue involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Osgood-Schlatter disease is a traction apophysitis of the tibial tuberosity common in adolescents during growth spurts with high sporting loads involving running and jumping.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports severe muscle pain and weakness developing during or immediately after an intense workout. Severe global malaise and marked fatigue noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to continue or repeat the activity that triggered symptoms. Severe global malaise and fatigue limit all physical activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Triggered by unaccustomed intense exercise (e.g., new training program, military bootcamp, extreme heat event). Patient has no prior history of similar episodes or known muscle disorders.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe muscle tenderness and induration palpable in exercised muscle groups. Calf or quad swelling noted. Neurological examination intact. Urine dipstick positive for blood/myoglobin with absence of RBCs on microscopy.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serum creatine kinase >5000 IU/L (often >10,000). Urine myoglobin present. Serum creatinine elevated with oliguria, consistent with acute tubular necrosis from myoglobin precipitation.

puzzles.explanation: Exertional rhabdomyolysis shows muscle breakdown with severe CK elevation, myoglobinuria, and risk of acute kidney injury from intense exertion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with migraine headache history presents with episodes of vertigo that occur with or separate from headache. Vertigo episodes are recurrent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports significant balance impairment during episodes, particularly difficulty with gait and head movements. Nausea and photophobia are prominent accompanying features.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Migraines began in early adulthood. Vertigo episodes last minutes to hours. Triggers include stress, certain foods, and hormonal changes. No prior head trauma or hearing loss. Patient has tried migraine prophylaxis with variable response.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positional nystagmus or gaze-evoked nystagmus may be present. Balance testing shows abnormalities. Neurological examination is otherwise normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain excludes other causes of vertigo. MRI is normal in vestibular migraine.

puzzles.explanation: Vestibular Migraine presents as vertigo-predominant migraine without accompanying headache, showing the vestibular symptoms without the typical headache component.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete presents with pain in the groin region or lower abdominal region. Pain develops with athletic activity and resolves with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running, kicking, or twisting movements. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training intensity or volume coincides with symptom onset. Sport involving rapid acceleration and deceleration is typical.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited in the groin region and lower abdomen. Pain is reproduced with resisted hip adduction and internal rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates adductor muscle strain or rectus femoris tear near the pubic symphysis. MRI characterises muscle injury and inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Athletic pubalgia represents a functional defect in the posterior inguinal wall without overt hernia, difficult to diagnose.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old recreational athlete presents with medial-sided knee pain and mechanical symptoms. Pain is moderate intensity and worsened by certain movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with stairs, squatting, and kneeling due to joint line discomfort. Walking with an antalgic gait. Pain worsens with deep knee flexion and rotational activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of twisting injury while playing football 3 weeks ago with subsequent surgical intervention. Moderate pain interfering with sport and daily activities. No prior knee injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mild joint effusion on examination. Quadriceps strength 4/5, VMO atrophy evident. Joint line tenderness medially reproduced. Knee flexion range 95 degrees with terminal extension lag 5 degrees.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows meniscal resection with intact ACL, PCL, and collateral ligaments. No acute chondral lesions identified. Joint space preserved. No loose body.

puzzles.explanation: Post-meniscectomy rehabilitation requires early quadriceps activation and strengthening, proprioceptive retraining, restoration of confidence in knee stability, and gradual progression to sport-specific activities while preventing secondary overload injuries.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old presents with unilateral lower back pain. Onset gradual over several months. No trauma or specific incident. Pain is mechanical in nature, varies with activity and position.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with standing and walking, especially over longer distances. Bending backward aggravates symptoms significantly. Patient reports relief when sitting or flexing forward. Difficulty with stairs and prolonged upright posture.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic intermittent symptoms with episodic flares over past 2 years. No radicular pain or leg weakness. Recent increase in gardening and yard work preceded current exacerbation. Previous episodes resolved with rest and activity modification. No significant prior spinal trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Extension and ipsilateral rotation of the lumbar spine reproduce local pain. Flexion and contralateral rotation do not provoke symptoms. Unilateral tenderness elicited on deep palpation over the posterior aspect of the spine at L4-L5 level. Straight leg raise negative. Normal lower extremity strength, sensation, and reflexes. No neurological deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows facet joint hypertrophy and osteophyte formation at L4-L5 on one side. Mild degenerative changes within the facet joint including cartilage loss and capsular thickening. No central canal stenosis, no neural foraminal compromise, no disc herniation. Findings correlate with clinical examination and pain reproduction.

puzzles.explanation: Facet joint syndrome presents as a degenerative condition in an older patient with chronic pain and stiffness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An adolescent reports progressive upper back pain that worsens with activity and prolonged sitting. Pain is mechanical in nature.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty maintaining upright posture for prolonged periods. Appearance shows exaggerated rounding of the upper back.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began in early adolescence with gradual worsening over 1–2 years. Family history of similar spinal deformity is noted. No prior significant back injury or trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, fixed thoracic kyphosis is evident that does not correct with forward bending. Back pain is elicited with extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate thoracic vertebral body wedging and Schmorl's nodes characteristic of Scheuermann's disease. Kyphosis is measured.

puzzles.explanation: Scheuermann's disease is a resolved condition with residual postural deformity and minimal ongoing pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old former construction worker. Gradual onset of shortness of breath over 3 years, initially only with stairs but now present at rest. Persistent dry cough. No recent infection or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk more than 50 metres without stopping to catch breath. Oxygen saturation drops with minimal exertion. Difficulty sleeping flat; requires multiple pillows. No relief with rest or positional changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Worked 40 years in dusty environments (stone cutting, demolition). Symptoms began years after occupational exposure ended. Non-smoker. No recent illness, infections, or medication changes. Progressive despite lifestyle modifications.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Fine crackles heard throughout lung bases on auscultation, worse on inspiration. Reduced air entry bilaterally. Finger clubbing present. Normal heart sounds. No oedema. Oxygen saturation 88% at rest, drops to 80% on minimal activity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest shows bilateral reticular opacities and ground-glass infiltrates in lower lung lobes with honeycombing pattern. Pulmonary function tests reveal restrictive pattern: FVC reduced, FEV1/FVC ratio elevated, DLCO significantly reduced.

puzzles.explanation: Acute exacerbation of IPF presentation, which carries poor prognosis. Respiratory failure triggered by viral infection, representing clinical deterioration despite antifibrotic therapy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old female presents with difficulty controlling bladder and bowel function that has progressed over 8 months. She reports urgency and occasional incontinence during exercise, coughing, or sneezing. There is also pain with sexual intercourse.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient has modified her routine to avoid jumping or running due to urine leakage. She performs Kegel exercises but reports poor muscle control. Prolonged sitting worsens pelvic discomfort, while lying down relieves symptoms. She avoids tampon use due to discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She had two vaginal deliveries 15 and 10 years ago with episiotomy noted during second delivery. She reports a history of chronic constipation. Menstrual cycles are regular without hormonal contraceptive use. She denies previous pelvic surgery beyond childbirth.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Internal palpation reveals weak muscle contraction during requested pelvic floor squeeze, graded 2/5. A cough stress test produces leakage before pelvic floor contraction occurs. Tender trigger points are noted in the pelvic floor muscles bilaterally during assessment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound demonstrates descent of the bladder base beyond 5 cm on Valsalva maneuver, indicating hypermobility. Pelvic floor thickness measures 0.6 cm on endovaginal ultrasound, below the normal range of 0.8-1.2 cm, confirming atrophy and dysfunction.

puzzles.explanation: Pelvic Floor Dysfunction shows classic stress incontinence from weakness in a multiparous menopausal female with poor muscular support.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports pain, stiffness, and difficulty with movement following thermal injury. Onset was acute; now 6 weeks post-injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot reach across midline or overhead. Difficulty with fine hand movements and walking due to lower limb tightness. Functional independence in ADLs is severely compromised.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sustained deep partial thickness and full thickness burns to upper and lower limbs 6 weeks ago. Initial acute treatment completed; now progressing through scar maturation phase. No prior burns or significant medical history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible hypertrophic scarring over dorsal hands and anterior shins with blanching erythema. Elbow flexion limited to 100° and ankle dorsiflexion to 0° due to soft tissue tightness. No sensory loss; strength intact but guarded by pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows thickened dermis with heterogeneous echogenicity consistent with hypertrophic scar. No evidence of underlying tissue compromise or active infection.

puzzles.explanation: Full-thickness burn rehabilitation requires careful graft protection during early phases, progressive range of motion, hypertrophic scar management, pain control strategies, and cautious progression to weight-bearing and ambulation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient presents with acute onset shoulder pain and visible swelling following a fall. Pain is reproduced with shoulder movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to abduct or elevate the arm. Arm splinting by patient. Unable to perform overhead activities or carry objects.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall directly onto the point of the shoulder during contact sport. Heard/felt a crack at time of injury. No prior shoulder pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable step deformity or prominence at the clavicle. Horizontal adduction (cross-body) test elicits focal pain. Scapular dyskinesis evident. No neurological deficit distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate clavicle fracture with characterisation of displacement and comminution. Imaging excludes neurovascular injury.

puzzles.explanation: Low-energy clavicle fracture in elderly patient with osteoporosis from minor trauma.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old construction supervisor presents with severe lower back pain that began suddenly while lifting a heavy load 2 weeks ago. The pain radiates down the left leg into the foot with associated numbness in the foot. Sitting and bending forward intensely worsen the pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot sit for more than 15 minutes without severe radiating pain. Lying flat with the left leg elevated provides mild relief. Bending forward to pick up objects triggers sharp shooting pain down the left leg. Light walking is tolerated better than sitting or standing still.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 10-year history of intermittent lower back pain managed with NSAIDs and physical therapy. He reports smoking one pack daily and spends 8 hours per day in a vehicle for work. No prior episodes of leg pain. Family history includes degenerative disc disease in his father.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Straight leg raise test is positive on the left at 35 degrees with reproduction of radiating pain. Ankle dorsiflexion weakness is demonstrated as 4/5 on the left. Sensory testing shows decreased sensation in the L5 dermatome on the left foot. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are intact bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar spine reveals a large posterolateral disc herniation at L4-L5 with significant compression of the left L5 nerve root. The herniated disc material is noted to be sequestered, and there is associated left-sided foraminal stenosis at the level of involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar disc herniation is an atypical presentation in a young athlete with acute trauma during sport and initially misleading clinical presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old racquetball player presents with wrist pain on the ulnar side that began gradually during a tournament 6 weeks ago. Pain is located on the medial aspect of the wrist just above the wrist crease and worsens with gripping activities. There is associated clicking sensation in the wrist.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Racquet sports and activities requiring power grip aggravate the pain significantly. Twisting motions of the wrist, particularly combined with ulnar deviation, provoke sharp pain. The patient reports weakness when attempting to lift objects with the affected hand. Rest and ice provide temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has played racquetball competitively for 15 years with no prior wrist injuries. He reports recent increase in training intensity. No systemic inflammatory history. The patient is right-hand dominant with left wrist affected. He denies previous wrist fractures or dislocations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The ulnar fovea sign is positive with palpable laxity of the ulnocarpal ligaments. Positive ulnar fovea sign test reproduces the patient's clicking. The ulnar meniscus load test provokes pain with axial compression and ulnar deviation combined. Grip strength is reduced 25% on the affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the wrist shows a tear of the triangular fibrocartilage with associated fluid signal extending into the tear. There is mild signal change in the lunate bone consistent with early chondral injury. The ulnar styloid process is intact without fracture or edema.

puzzles.explanation: TFCC tear from acute twisting mechanism with ulnar-sided pain and instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old fell onto outstretched hand while skateboarding. Now reports sharp pain in the lateral elbow with significant swelling over the past 2 hours. Pain is constant, 7/10, and radiates slightly into the forearm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to fully straighten the elbow. Rotating the forearm (both directions) triggers sharp pain. Gripping is weak and painful. Lifting objects above shoulder height is impossible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct impact to the lateral elbow during fall. No prior elbow injuries. Swelling appeared within minutes. Patient denies any numbness, tingling, or neck symptoms. No loss of consciousness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized tenderness in the groove lateral to the olecranon. Elbow range slightly restricted in extension. Pain with pronation and supination, especially near midrange. Posterior interosseous nerve testing intact. No neurovascular compromise. Elbow effusion present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs (AP, lateral, radial head view) reveal a non-displaced fracture through the radial head articular surface. No associated fractures noted. CT confirms simple undisplaced pattern without comminution.

puzzles.explanation: Radial head fracture from FOOSH injury with loss of forearm rotation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old tripped on stairs and caught themselves with outstretched arm. Now reports acute wrist pain, immediate onset swelling, and inability to move wrist. No numbness or tingling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot grip, pinch, or bear weight through affected arm. Wrist locked in slightly flexed position. Pain severe with any attempt at wrist extension or rotation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct fall 2 hours ago onto extended wrist. No previous wrist injuries. No osteoporosis history. No loss of consciousness. Pain progressively worsening; swelling now visible from hand to forearm.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wrist grossly swollen with ecchymosis developing. Bony tenderness over distal forearm. Wrist held in protective flexion. Mild loss of radial-sided hand contour. Fingers pink, warm, capillary refill normal. Sensation intact. No crepitus on palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Posteroanterior and lateral wrist radiographs show transverse fracture through distal radius metaphysis with dorsal displacement and posterior angulation of the distal fragment. Radial height loss noted. No ulnar fracture. Intact radiocarpal joint space.

puzzles.explanation: Classic Colles fracture in elderly osteoporotic patient from fall with typical 'fork' deformity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old male reports burning pain and numbness on the outer thigh that began 3 months ago and is progressively worsening. The symptoms are localized to the lateral thigh and do not extend below the knee. He describes a constant burning sensation with occasional electric shock sensations.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged standing and walking exacerbate the burning pain and numbness significantly. Wearing tight waistbands, belts, or sitting with legs crossed worsens the symptoms dramatically. Lying down with the hip flexed provides relief. The patient has reduced his walking distance from 30 minutes to 5 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 40-pound weight gain over the past 18 months and recently switched to wearing tighter fitting work pants due to his occupation. He denies lumbar pain or lower extremity radiculopathy. Recent history includes a fall onto his hip, though skin over the affected area is intact.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sensation is diminished to light touch and pinprick over the lateral thigh in the L2-L3 dermatome distribution. Straight leg raise test is negative. Hip flexion and extension strength are normal 5/5. Tapping over the inguinal ligament just medial to the anterior superior iliac spine reproduces the burning sensation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the pelvis and hip shows no mass or structural lesion at the inguinal ligament. The appearance is normal without focal nerve enlargement. Imaging confirms the absence of lumbar radiculopathy or hip joint pathology, supporting a diagnosis of nerve entrapment rather than central or referred pain.

puzzles.explanation: Post-surgical meralgia paraesthetica from iatrogenic nerve compression during hip surgery with isolated sensory distribution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old presents with acute onset chest wall pain following a fall onto a hard surface. Pain is sharp, localised to one side, and significantly worse when taking a deep breath or coughing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports severe difficulty with coughing and unable to lie on the affected side. Pain worsens with trunk twisting and forward bending, but is relieved somewhat when patient splints chest with pillow during cough.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct impact to left lateral chest wall 3 days ago during fall at home. Pain onset was immediate. No previous chest injury. Patient reports pain gradually improving but still significant. No dyspnoea, cough, or haemoptysis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localised point tenderness elicited over lateral ribs 5-7 on left. Pain reproduced with compression across chest wall (anteroposterior squeeze). Normal breath sounds bilaterally. No crepitus, swelling, or subcutaneous emphysema. Normal cardiovascular examination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest shows linear fracture through left 6th rib with minimal displacement. No pneumothorax, haemothorax, pulmonary contusion, or injury to mediastinal structures. Fracture margins are sharp without callus formation.

puzzles.explanation: Rib fracture has chronic sequelae with persistent pain despite imaging healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with repetitive occupational activity presents with pain in the proximal dorsal forearm at the second dorsal compartment. Pain is worse with gripping and resisted wrist extension.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by repetitive wrist extension and resisted finger extension. Patient reports visible swelling in the proximal forearm and pain with activities like rowing, rock climbing, or racquet sports.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms onset insidious over weeks. Patient is a rower, rock climber, or tennis player. No acute trauma, prior forearm injury, or inflammatory history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the proximal dorsal forearm where extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis cross over extensor carpi radialis and extensor digitorum communis. Crepitus or fine cracking sensation may be appreciated with movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates tenosynovitis and fluid at the intersection of the second and third dorsal compartments. MRI shows peritendinous edema and tendon sheath inflammation at the crossing point.

puzzles.explanation: Intersection syndrome from intense gripping activities with characteristic location and crepitus finding.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Infant presents with weakness and flaccidity of one arm noticed immediately after delivery. Caregiver reports the arm hangs limp at the side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Infant unable to lift the affected arm against gravity. No protective movement or grasp response on affected side. Cries asymmetrically during distress.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Delivery complicated by shoulder dystocia requiring significant traction. Weakness noted in the delivery room. Parents report arm has remained weak over first 2 weeks with no improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Arm held in adduction and internal rotation (waiter's tip posture). Weakness predominantly in shoulder abduction and external rotation. Biceps and deltoid strength severely reduced. Wrist and finger movements relatively preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography documents denervation changes in affected muscles. MRI may show nerve root avulsion if severe.

puzzles.explanation: Erb's palsy is injury to the upper brachial plexus (C5-C6) from birth trauma, resulting in shoulder and arm weakness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old presents with sudden onset of weakness and numbness on one side of the body. Noticed while at rest this morning. No trauma or recent illness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk or bear weight on affected leg. Difficulty with fine motor control and coordination on affected side. Unable to lift leg against gravity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began acutely 2 hours ago with no warning. Long-standing hypertension (poorly controlled) and 30-year smoking history. No prior episodes of weakness or numbness. Currently on no antiplatelet therapy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lower extremity strength 3/5 on affected side; upper extremity strength 5/5 bilaterally. Hyperreflexia and extensor plantar response on affected side. Mild facial droop absent. Mild confusion noted; language intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT head non-contrast: no hemorrhage. MRI with diffusion imaging shows acute infarct in medial frontal lobe and anterior limb of internal capsule on one side. Perfusion deficit matches arterial distribution pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Acute ACA stroke with lower extremity-predominant weakness, personality disturbance, and medial hemisphere involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged patient presents with central wrist pain and progressive loss of grip strength. Pain develops insidiously in the wrist, often after prior wrist trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform sustained gripping (e.g., opening jars, holding tools). Fine motor tasks like writing become progressively difficult. Symptoms worsen with repeated wrist loading and ulnar-sided activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual, progressive onset over weeks to months. Patient reports past wrist trauma (fracture or ligament injury) years prior, often with disrupted vascularity. Symptoms do not improve with rest alone and show insidious progression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness elicited over the lunate (dorsal wrist midline). Wrist extension and radial deviation are restricted. Positive lunate press test (pain with axial loading over lunate). Grip strength markedly reduced compared to contralateral side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate lunate flattening, sclerosis, fragmentation, and eventual collapse. MRI shows lunate necrosis with signal changes and loss of normal marrow intensity. Sequential imaging documents progressive disease stages.

puzzles.explanation: Kienbock's disease with classic progressive presentation and imaging findings of avascular necrosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old software developer presents with numbness and tingling in the hand that worsened over 6 weeks. Symptoms are triggered by prolonged desk work. Pain is present in the forearm and hand.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty with fine motor tasks and weakness in hand pinching. Resisted forearm movement in one direction reproduces forearm pain. Rest provides temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient works 8-10 hours daily at a desk with forearm resting on a padded armrest in pronation. He denies neck pain or shoulder involvement. Symptoms began insidiously without trauma. No history of elbow fracture or subluxation. The patient is right-hand dominant with symptoms on the right forearm.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weakness demonstrated in thumb-down pronation grip (4/5 strength). Tinel's sign is positive in the proximal forearm between two muscle bodies. Median nerve distribution sensory testing shows mild diminishment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the forearm demonstrates hypoechoic swelling of the median nerve as it passes between the two heads of the pronator teres muscle. Ultrasound shows nerve compression without mass effect or fracture. Cross-sectional area of the median nerve at this level is increased compared to the contralateral side.

puzzles.explanation: Pronator syndrome with median nerve compression at pronator teres origin, distinguished from CTS by location.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old male office worker presents with episodic unilateral headaches located in the temporal and occipital regions bilaterally asymmetrical. Symptoms correlate temporally with neck stiffness and worsen throughout the day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Headache consistently provokes with prolonged sitting and neck extension movements. Relief noted when lying supine or with ice application. Certain neck postures reproducibly trigger pain within minutes. Neck stretching and movement paradoxically worsens the headache initially.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Three-year history of intermittent symptoms, initially triggered by a motor vehicle accident with whiplash mechanism. Progressive worsening associated with sedentary work environment. Reports no visual changes, hearing loss, or vestibular symptoms. Stress management minimally affects pain pattern.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cervical range of motion significantly restricted in extension (40 degrees; normal >50 degrees) and right rotation (55 degrees; normal >80 degrees). Extension immediately reproduces familiar occipital-temporal headache. Positive flexion-rotation test with pain reproduction. Cervical paraspinal muscles palpably tight and tender.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine X-rays demonstrate loss of cervical lordosis with subtle C4-C5 disc bulging. MRI shows C4-C5 and C5-C6 uncovertebral joint arthrosis with mild cervical spondylosis. Facet joint changes at C3-C4 level on the right correlate with restricted ipsilateral rotation and headache reproduction.

puzzles.explanation: Cervicogenic headache is the classic mechanical presentation with clear cervical-headache relationship and mechanical provocation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old administrative worker reports hand pain and tingling that began several weeks ago. Symptoms are worse at night and with gripping. No recent trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with fine motor tasks and sustained grip. Symptoms improve with rest and wrist splinting at night. Keyboard work aggravates tingling in thumb, index, and middle fingers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Open carpal tunnel release surgery performed 8 weeks ago for diagnosed median nerve compression. Initial post-operative improvement for 4 weeks, then gradual return of numbness and pain. No infection or wound complications reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Median nerve distribution paresthesias reproduced with sustained wrist flexion. Two-point discrimination reduced in radial three digits. Scar is well-healed and mobile. Grip strength 80% of contralateral side. Phalen's test positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Repeat nerve conduction studies show recurrent prolonged distal motor latency and reduced sensory conduction velocity across carpal tunnel. Ultrasound reveals thickened flexor retinaculum with minimal scar tissue. MRI excludes mass or hematoma.

puzzles.explanation: Severe or chronic carpal tunnel cases may have prolonged recovery. This presentation requires aggressive scar management, sensory reeducation, and extended strengthening to achieve functional recovery of thenar muscles.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete presents with acute anterior thigh pain that developed during activity. Pain is localised to the anterior thigh.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running, kicking, or activities requiring hip flexion and knee extension. The patient demonstrates functional limitation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset during or immediately after athletic activity. The patient may have inadequate warm-up or prior thigh injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the rectus femoris. Resisted knee extension or hip flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption or hematoma within the rectus femoris. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic rectus femoris strain may develop from repetitive sport movements and prior injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young to middle-aged adult presents with acute unilateral facial weakness and drooping that developed over hours to 48 hours. The patient reports inability to wrinkle forehead, close eye, or bare teeth on the affected side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional impairment includes inability to close the eye completely on the affected side (lagophthalmos), difficulty with oral hygiene, and loss of facial symmetry during smiling. Water escapes from the mouth during drinking due to lip weakness and drooling may occur.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset was acute, reaching maximum deficit within 72 hours. Recent upper respiratory tract infection, herpes zoster, or Lyme disease exposure may precede weakness. No prior facial weakness episodes and no significant neurological history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, complete unilateral lower facial weakness is evident, with forehead involvement (distinguishing from central causes). Incomplete eye closure on affected side with Bell's sign present. Tear production may be reduced. Hyperacusis and altered taste on anterior two-thirds of tongue are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography demonstrates acute denervation changes and motor unit recruitment abnormalities consistent with peripheral facial nerve injury. Head CT or MRI is unremarkable, ruling out central brainstem lesions, stroke, space-occupying masses, or other structural pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Classic idiopathic Bell's palsy with acute unilateral facial nerve paralysis affecting forehead and orbicularis oris, suggesting upper motor neuron involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old driver. Onset of neck pain and headache 2 hours after a motor vehicle collision. No loss of consciousness. Pain radiates to shoulders and upper back.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot turn head beyond 30 degrees without sharp pain. Difficulty looking over shoulder while driving. Symptoms worse with sustained postures; brief relief with movement. No radiation past elbow.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Rear-impact collision at low to moderate speed. No direct blow to head. Immediate post-impact onset within minutes to 2 hours. No prior neck problems. No loss of consciousness or concussion symptoms reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cervical ROM limited in all planes, especially rotation (30°) and extension. Palpable muscle guarding and tenderness in upper trapezius and cervical paraspinals. Normal grip strength, reflexes normal, no dermatomal sensory loss or motor deficits. No upper limb neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine radiographs (AP, lateral, odontoid) show loss of normal cervical lordosis and straightening. No fractures, subluxations, or listhesis. MRI cervical spine shows increased signal in cervical musculature and anterior longitudinal ligament on T2 sequences; no disc herniation or cord signal changes.

puzzles.explanation: Whiplash associated disorder is acute post-traumatic injury from classic motor vehicle mechanism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young baseball pitcher presents with medial elbow pain. The pain develops during throwing activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by throwing, particularly with high-velocity throwing. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with throwing sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Young athlete engaged in overhead throwing sports. Symptoms develop with increased throwing volume or intensity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, medial elbow tenderness is present over the medial epicondyle. Valgus stress test reproduces pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may demonstrate medial epicondyle avulsion or fragmentation. MRI characterises soft tissue involvement and ligamentous injury.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic Little League elbow may progress to collateral ligament injury requiring surgical intervention and extended rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 62-year-old reports sudden loss of limb function with inability to perform activities of daily living. Experiences pain in the region where the limb was, sensation of limb still being present, and significant psychological distress about functional capacity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk independently, transfer from bed to chair, or bear weight. Experiences shooting pain and burning sensations in the absent limb, particularly with attempted movement. Fatigues quickly with minimal exertion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute limb loss 3 weeks ago from traumatic crush injury with vascular compromise requiring surgical intervention. No prior limb loss history. Currently on pain management and anti-inflammatory medications. Lives with family, motivated for functional recovery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Residual limb shows surgical scarring, moderate edema, and early tissue remodeling. Palpable bone prominence at surgical site. Intact sensation and skin integrity. Phantom sensations reproduced with residual limb movement. Trunk strength intact, upper extremities normal. Balance impaired in sitting without support.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs of residual limb show stable bone healing at amputation site with early callus formation. No signs of infection or nonunion. Soft tissue ultrasound documents residual edema and muscle atrophy progressing as expected for 3-week post-operative timeline. No imaging contraindication for prosthetic fitting initiation.

puzzles.explanation: Post-amputation rehabilitation requires holistic management of phantom pain, stump conditioning and shaping, upper extremity strengthening for prosthetic ambulation, and psychological support during adjustment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged adult presents with neck pain that radiates into one arm. The pain has developed gradually or acutely following minor trauma or activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is reproducibly triggered by certain neck movements, particularly extension and ipsilateral rotation. The patient reports functional limitation with arm activities and difficulty maintaining posture.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of neck pain may be present. Symptoms began spontaneously or after minor trauma. The patient may have prior history of cervical spine problems. Symptoms are activity-dependent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, neck movement is limited, particularly extension and ipsilateral side-bending. Upper extremity weakness is demonstrated in a dermatomal distribution. Diminished reflexes may be present. Spurling's test reproduces radicular pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of cervical spine demonstrates disc herniation at the affected level with nerve root compression and narrowing of the neural foramen. The herniated material displaces neural structures.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical disc herniation is the classic presentation with acute nerve root compression and clear imaging correlation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged adult presents with progressive weakness in the hands affecting fine motor tasks. The patient reports difficulty with activities including writing and buttoning clothes over the past several months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional decline is evident with impaired grip strength, difficulty with coordinated movements, and progressive inability to perform occupational tasks. The patient demonstrates a characteristic pattern of weakness that spreads to involve multiple limb regions over time.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began focally in one region and gradually spread to involve other body areas. The patient reports no sensory changes, preserved cognition, and no significant trauma or infection history. Progressive nature over 6-12 months noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, visible muscle fasciculations are present in affected regions, with measurable atrophy. Hyperreflexia is noted in hyperexcitable muscle groups. Cranial nerve examination may reveal mild tongue atrophy or fasciculations. Sensory examination is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography (EMG) demonstrates acute denervation with fibrillations and positive sharp waves in affected muscles, consistent with anterior horn cell loss. MRI of brain and spine excludes structural lesions or spinal cord compression.

puzzles.explanation: Sporadic ALS with spinal-onset presentation showing upper and lower motor neuron signs, fasciculations, and denervation on EMG.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 56-year-old accountant presents with progressive lower back pain and leg weakness that worsens with extended periods of standing or walking. The patient reports a forward-leaning posture is more comfortable than standing upright. Symptoms began 18 months ago and have gradually deteriorated.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking forward uphill is tolerated better than walking backward or on flat surfaces. Prolonged standing without support causes severe pain and cramping in both legs. Sitting down or leaning forward significantly relieves symptoms within minutes. The patient can walk further when bent forward, using a shopping cart for support.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has had a 20-year history of intermittent lower back pain managed conservatively. No history of prior spinal surgery. Recent fall from a ladder with direct blow to the lumbar region occurred 18 months ago, coinciding with symptom onset. He denies bowel or bladder changes. Family history of osteoporosis is noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Forward spinal flexion reproduces pain, but lumbar extension is limited and painful. Straight leg raise testing is negative. Neurological examination reveals normal strength and reflexes bilaterally. Palpation over the spinous process at L4-L5 elicits tenderness. Standing extension loading test increases symptoms.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain film X-rays show anterior slipping of L4 on L5 measuring approximately 15-20% of the vertebral body width, with associated degenerative changes. MRI confirms anterior spondylolisthesis at L4-L5 with canal narrowing and bilateral foraminal stenosis. No instability on flexion-extension radiographs. Disc signal is mildly decreased at the slipped level.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar spondylolisthesis is the classic case with clear mechanical symptoms and definitive imaging showing vertebral slip.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with knee injury presents with pain on the lateral knee. The injury occurred with varus stress mechanism or direct contact to medial knee.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation is present with difficulty with walking and activities causing knee pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute injury mechanism documented. The patient may have prior ankle or knee injury history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, lateral knee tenderness is present. Varus stress test demonstrates excessive varus motion indicating ligamentous injury.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates lateral collateral ligament tear with characterisation of tear severity and associated injuries.

puzzles.explanation: LCL injuries are less common than MCL but can occur with inversion and varus mechanisms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old woman reports progressive pain and swelling in her hand joints, particularly affecting the distal interphalangeal joints, with morning stiffness lasting 20 minutes. Pain is worse in the dominant hand and has progressed over several years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Fine motor activities such as writing, sewing, and opening jars are significantly limited by hand pain and weakness. Carrying objects with a firm grip worsens symptoms. Hand elevation and ice provide temporary relief; prolonged use causes pain flare-ups.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has had repetitive hand use occupationally (seamstress) for 40 years and experienced a distal interphalangeal joint injury at age 35. She has no systemic rheumatologic symptoms, fevers, or rashes. Family history significant for osteoarthritis in the paternal line.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable osteophytes (Heberden nodes) at the distal interphalangeal joints of fingers 2-5 bilaterally. Hand grip strength reduced to 18 kg on right (age-expected 25-30 kg). Finger ROM limited at distal joints, with crepitus appreciated. No synovitis or joint warmth.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing primarily at distal interphalangeal joints with prominent marginal osteophytes and subchondral bone sclerosis. No evidence of erosions, periostitis, or soft tissue calcifications. Metacarpophalangeal joints spared.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoarthritis is localized to one or two joints with activity-related pain pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with ankle trauma presents with acute severe pain and swelling. The injury occurred from a high-energy mechanism such as fall from height or axial loading.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe functional limitation is present with complete inability to weightbear. The ankle is held in guarding and the patient requires immediate immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy trauma documented with direct axial load to the ankle joint. Associated injuries to ipsilateral leg, spine, or pelvis may be present.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ankle demonstrates severe deformity with gross swelling preventing palpation of bony landmarks. Skin integrity must be assessed for open fracture. Neurovascular examination documents dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses; sensation and motor function in foot assessed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate pilon fracture with comminution of the tibial plafond and intra-articular extension. CT imaging characterises the fracture pattern with 3D reconstruction essential for surgical planning.

puzzles.explanation: Pilon fractures are devastating injuries with high complication rates requiring urgent surgical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old woman presents with acute-onset paralysis of the right leg that began suddenly during an emotionally stressful period at work 4 weeks ago. She is unable to walk independently and uses a wheelchair for mobility. She reports no sensory symptoms below the waist.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She demonstrates inability to bear weight on the right lower extremity, yet exhibits inconsistent functional use of the limb during natural activities and when unaware of observation. Her gait pattern when attempting to walk does not fit typical neurological disease patterns. She shows dramatic relief of symptoms during emotionally supportive interactions but sudden worsening during stressful encounters.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She experienced significant workplace conflict and interpersonal stress coinciding with symptom onset. She has a history of anxiety symptoms and perfectionist personality traits. She has seen multiple neurologists without definitive diagnosis of organic neurological disease. Prior psychiatric history includes adjustment disorder 3 years ago treated with psychotherapy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, she demonstrates give-way weakness of right hip and knee with sudden collapse of effort when testing lower extremity strength. Hoover sign is positive indicating functional deficit rather than true paralysis. Gait when mobilizing without attention shows relatively preserved motor function compared to formal strength testing. Sensory examination is completely normal throughout. Reflexes are symmetric and normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spine MRI including cervical, thoracic, and lumbar regions is entirely normal without spinal cord lesions, compression, or structural abnormality. Brain MRI is normal with no white matter abnormalities, acute stroke, or demyelinating lesions. Routine laboratory studies including CBC and comprehensive metabolic panel are normal. Comprehensive neurophysiological testing is entirely normal without evidence of neuromuscular disease.

puzzles.explanation: Functional neurological disorder with non-organic motor signs, inconsistent findings on examination, and clear psychosocial precipitant.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with running activity presents with progressive metatarsal pain. Pain develops in the ball of the foot.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running and impact activities. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with weightbearing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training volume or intensity coincides with symptom onset. Female athletes with amenorrhea are at increased risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, focal tenderness is present over the affected metatarsal. Pain is reproduced with single-leg hopping.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may show callus formation at chronic stage. MRI demonstrates bone stress with periosteal reaction and bone marrow edema.

puzzles.explanation: Metatarsal stress fracture shows classic stress fracture from repetitive impact in runners or marchers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old female reports jaw pain localized to the joint in front of the ear that has progressively worsened over 4 months. She describes clicking or popping sounds with opening the mouth and occasional jaw locking that interferes with eating. Pain is dull and achy, worsening toward the end of the day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Chewing tough foods and prolonged talking exacerbate pain and clicking significantly. The patient reports difficulty eating harder foods and now avoids them. Yawning reproduces the locking sensation. Resting the jaw and applying ice provide moderate relief. She clenches her teeth during stressful situations.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient reports a high-stress occupation with recent significant life changes. She works in a fast-paced environment and admits to daytime jaw clenching and nighttime teeth grinding. Dental history includes recent orthodontic treatment completed 2 years ago. No history of jaw trauma or prior TMJ disorders.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpation over the temporomandibular joint bilaterally reveals tenderness on the affected side. Mouth opening measures 38 mm with deviation toward the right side. Clicking is audible during opening and closing of the mouth. Palpation of the masseter and temporalis muscles reveals significant myofascial trigger points with referred pain pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows displacement of the articular disc anteriorly with possible reduction during opening movements, indicating disc displacement with reduction. Mild joint space narrowing is present. Signal intensity changes in the joint fluid suggest mild inflammatory changes. No evidence of osteoarthritis or significant degenerative changes on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: TMJ disorder presents as chronic degenerative TMJ osteoarthritis with limited conservative response and structural joint changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old fell backward onto outstretched hand. Acute onset severe wrist pain, swelling, and inability to move wrist. No numbness or tingling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot flex or extend wrist. Hand hangs passively. Grip strength absent. Pain with any attempt at forearm rotation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy mechanism: fell from standing height onto palm with wrist in flexion. Immediate swelling. No prior wrist injury or arthritis. No neurovascular symptoms at time of injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wrist held in slight flexion. Prominent swelling over volar (palmar) aspect of distal forearm. Bony tenderness over distal radius. No crepitus. Radial pulse intact, capillary refill normal, sensation intact to light touch.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Wrist radiographs: distal radius fracture with volar angulation of distal fragment (reversed deformity). Fracture line transverse across distal metaphysis. No intra-articular extension.

puzzles.explanation: Smith's fracture with volar displacement from fall on flexed wrist.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A recreational runner presents with pain and stiffness at the back of the lower leg. Pain is localised to the Achilles region and develops with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is specifically triggered by running and activities involving plantarflexion. The patient experiences morning stiffness that improves with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training intensity or volume coincides with symptom onset. The patient may have prior history of Achilles pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable along the Achilles tendon. Crepitus may be appreciated with tendon movement. Resisted plantarflexion reproduces pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates Achilles tendon thickening, hypoechoic changes, or partial tear. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation confirming tendinopathy.

puzzles.explanation: Achilles tendinopathy develops from cumulative microtrauma and is common in runners and athletes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old former tennis player presents with progressively worsening shoulder pain for 8 months, particularly when lifting the arm overhead. Pain is located over the top of the shoulder and radiates down the lateral arm. Sudden acute flare-ups occur with minor overhead movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Overhead reaching activities, particularly reaching behind the head, provoke sharp pain and weakness. Carrying groceries with the affected arm is no longer possible due to pain and weakness. Lying on the affected shoulder is uncomfortable. Rest and ice provide temporary relief, but symptoms return quickly with activity resumption.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient played competitive tennis for 30 years, retiring 10 years ago. She reports a fall on the outstretched hand 8 months ago that preceded current symptoms. Past medical history includes hypertension controlled with medication and mild osteoarthritis in other joints. She denies previous shoulder surgery or dislocations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Abduction of the arm reproduces sharp pain, particularly between 60-120 degrees. Supraspinatus strength testing shows weakness with painful arc. Positive Hawkins test and Neer's impingement sign are elicited. Infraspinatus strength is 5/5. Palpation reveals tenderness over the supraspinatus insertion on the humeral head.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain film X-ray of the shoulder demonstrates a focal rounded density over the supraspinatus tendon insertion area. CT imaging shows a calcification measuring 12 mm with a heterogeneous appearance located within the supraspinatus tendon. The calcification is located 8 mm lateral to the humeral head articular surface margin.

puzzles.explanation: Acute calcification of supraspinatus with severe inflammatory response.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old male presents with acute onset inability to move his affected leg after undergoing hip replacement surgery. He cannot walk and cannot lift his leg off the bed. The anterior thigh is noticeably swollen and tender.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform active hip flexion or knee extension. Sensory loss noted over the anterior thigh and medial lower leg in dermatomal pattern. Non-weight bearing and requires assistance with all mobility.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred intraoperatively during total hip arthroplasty when the femoral nerve was stretched or contused during retraction for surgical access. Post-operative imaging revealed significant hematoma in the psoas and iliacus region. No prior neurological deficits. The patient underwent urgent hematoma evacuation 24 hours after surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion strength 0/5 with no visible iliopsoas contraction. Knee extension absent. Patellar reflex absent on affected side. Sensory loss to light touch in L2-L4 distribution. Complete paralysis of quadriceps and hip adductors.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the hip and proximal femur shows large hematoma in the iliopsoas and psoas major extending along the femoral nerve course. Nerve signal is interrupted at the level of the hematoma. Post-hematoma evacuation imaging shows partial reduction in swelling but persistent nerve compression. Nerve edema is visible extending distally.

puzzles.explanation: Post-surgical femoral nerve injury with acute quadriceps paralysis, loss of knee extension, and imaging evidence of nerve compression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old retired educator experiences chronic heel and arch discomfort extending across the sole of the foot. Morning pain is severe, necessitating 15-20 minutes of slow movement before tolerance improves. This chronic presentation affecting weight-bearing structures requires differentiation from multiple structural and systemic conditions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: New or increased walking beyond 2 kilometers triggers escalation of pain for the following 24-48 hours. Elevated heels provide some relief. Running is not tolerated; even brisk walking produces discomfort within 10 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 18 months ago following increased daily walking activity post-retirement. Patient has type 2 diabetes controlled by metformin with BMI 29. Previously experienced plantar fasciitis 10 years ago that resolved with conservative treatment within 6 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, pain is reproduced with palpation along the medial longitudinal arch. Windlass test produces marked pain with dorsiflexion of the hallux. Ankle plantarflexion range is normal; however, dorsiflexion is limited to 8 degrees bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals chronic thickening of the plantar fascia measuring 5.1 mm at the insertion point with heterogeneous signal intensity. Small calcaneal heel spur is visible. Marrow signal intensity at the calcaneal tuberosity is mildly altered, consistent with chronic inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Plantar fasciitis athlete shows chronic refractory heel pain with calcification and significant functional limitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old diabetic woman reports gradual progressive weakness in her right leg over the past 3 months without preceding injury. She describes the leg feeling heavy and numb.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She reports severe difficulty with stairs and standing from a chair. Her walking tolerance has dropped significantly. She notes night-time thigh pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 15-year history of poorly controlled Type 2 diabetes with recent HbA1c of 9.2%. She reports unintentional weight loss of 12 pounds over the past 3 months. No history of leg trauma or prior neurological disorders. Recent imaging ruled out lumbar pathology. Vascular history is significant for peripheral vascular disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion strength is 2/5 with minimal antigravity power. Knee extension is similarly weak. Patellar reflex is diminished on the affected side. Sensory loss follows L2-L4 distribution.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar plexus and proximal femur shows signal abnormality within the psoas and iliacus muscles consistent with denervation edema. The femoral nerve appears enlarged proximally with T2 hyperintense signal consistent with inflammation. No focal mass or compressive lesion. Findings are consistent with diabetic femoral neuropathy.

puzzles.explanation: Diabetic lumbosacral radiculoplexopathy affecting femoral nerve with proximal leg weakness and motor loss in femoral distribution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 14-year-old competitive athlete. Localized pain and swelling below the knee that appeared gradually over 2–3 months. Pain is activity-related and does not wake them at night. No history of direct trauma or fall.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens significantly with jumping, sprinting, and cutting movements. Symptoms ease with rest. Difficulty returning to full training intensity. Pain is reproducible and consistent with activity level.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Rapid growth phase (tall for age). Recent increase in training volume and intensity over past 3 months. Previous episodes of anterior knee discomfort that resolved with rest. No systemic illness, fever, or constitutional symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Focal bony prominence and tenderness over the anterior tibial tubercle. Pain elicited with resisted knee extension and single-leg squat. Mild local swelling; skin warm and non-erythematous. Knee range of motion preserved. No effusion or ligamentous laxity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lateral knee radiographs show fragmentation and irregularity of the tibial tubercle apophysis with surrounding soft tissue swelling. No fracture line or displaced fragment. Findings consistent with apophyseal traction injury.

puzzles.explanation: Residual OSD effects may persist after apophyseal closure due to bony irregularities and scar tissue.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports persistent fatigue and shortness of breath with minimal exertion. Symptoms began after a prolonged period off work due to acute illness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot climb stairs without stopping for breath and becomes exhausted after light household tasks. Walking >100m triggers symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prior illness, hospitalization, or prolonged immobility is documented. The patient has been inactive for weeks to months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Heart rate and blood pressure rise disproportionately with minimal activity. Resting heart rate elevated at 95 bpm. Six-minute walk test shows marked reduction in distance with dyspnoea at low workload.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cardiopulmonary exercise testing shows VO2 max 40% below age-predicted values. Echocardiography and pulmonary function tests are normal; no structural cardiac or lung disease detected.

puzzles.explanation: Post-illness deconditioning involves reduced exercise tolerance and function after immobilization, reversible with rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient presents with recurrent episodes of wheezing, dyspnoea, and chest tightness. Symptoms are variable and intermittent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports breathlessness and chest tightness triggered by exertion, cold air exposure, or allergen contact. Audible breathing sounds present during episodes. Symptoms resolve within minutes to hours of rest or symptom cessation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Unable to sustain aerobic activities without symptom onset. Nocturnal coughing occasionally reported. Symptoms worsen during high pollen seasons. No fever or recent infection preceding onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Auscultation reveals bilateral wheeze during or immediately after exertion. Respiratory rate elevated during symptoms. Accessory muscle use may be evident. Examination normal between episodes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spirometry demonstrates obstructive pattern with FEV1/FVC ratio reduced. Bronchial hyperresponsiveness confirmed on challenge testing. Significant reversibility documented after bronchodilator administration.

puzzles.explanation: Classic pediatric asthma with allergen triggers, family history, and reversible airway obstruction confirmed by spirometry. Typical presentation responsive to treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete presents with pain in the groin region or lower abdominal region. Pain develops with athletic activity and resolves with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running, kicking, or twisting movements. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training intensity or volume coincides with symptom onset. Sport involving rapid acceleration and deceleration is typical.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resisted hip adduction reproduces pain. Palpation over adductor insertion at pubis is tender. Pain localised to medial groin with positive squeeze test (resisted hip adduction in supine).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates adductor muscle strain or rectus femoris tear near the pubic symphysis with hypoechoic changes. MRI characterises muscle injury, inflammation, and pubic bone involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Athletic pubalgia requires high index of suspicion and may require surgical evaluation after failed conservative care.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 25-year-old patient presents with recurrent shoulder pain and a sense of the shoulder 'slipping' or feeling unstable. Symptoms are triggered by overhead activities and throwing movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform overhead press or throwing activities without fear of shoulder giving way. Symptoms occur in multiple arm positions, not just one.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient often has generalized ligamentous laxity. Symptoms develop with activity or may occur spontaneously.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Apprehension sign positive in multiple planes. Sulcus sign present (inferior instability). Relocation test positive. Laxity testing reveals increased humeral head translation in anteroinferior and posteroinferior directions.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates anteroinferior and posteroinferior labral tears with enlarged capsule. Humeral head subluxation evident in multiple positions on dynamic sequences.

puzzles.explanation: Multidirectional instability secondary to connective tissue disorder with severe manifestations.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 56-year-old recreational jogger presents with progressive pain and swelling on the medial ankle that began insidiously 8 months ago without preceding acute injury. The foot is progressively losing its arch and turning outward. Walking on uneven surfaces has become increasingly difficult, and the patient describes a sensation of instability.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Running is no longer tolerated; the patient can only walk short distances before ankle pain and swelling worsen. Walking on uneven terrain aggravates symptoms more than flat surfaces. Prolonged standing increases pain and swelling significantly. The patient reports the foot 'giving way' occasionally with walking. Rest, ice, and elevation provide only temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 20-year history of sporadic ankle sprains, with no specific traumatic event preceding current symptoms. He runs 4 times per week on concrete surfaces. No surgical history. Past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He reports progressive loss of endurance over the past year.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant foot pronation is observed during gait, with increased medial longitudinal arch collapse. A positive single-leg stance test shows loss of balance on the affected foot. Resisted inversion strength is 4/5 on the affected side. Palpation reveals swelling and tenderness along the posterior tibial tendon insertion. Navicular drop test measures 15 mm compared to 5 mm on the unaffected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the ankle shows thickening and partial-thickness tear of the posterior tibial tendon at the medial malleolar region, measuring 8 mm in width. Surrounding soft tissue edema is present. The spring ligament shows partial disruption. Hindfoot alignment demonstrates 8-degree valgus deformity with talonavicular uncoverage on coronal imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced Stage 2 PTTD often requires surgical consideration for symptomatic management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 25-year-old with lifelong history of recurrent chest infections and persistent productive cough. Reports dyspnea with exertion and chronic fatigue. No significant smoking history.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk more than one flight of stairs without stopping to catch breath. Daily cough productive of copious, thick sputum. Difficulty with sustained aerobic activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began in infancy with failure to thrive and recurrent pneumonias. Frequent admissions for respiratory exacerbations throughout childhood. History of loose, greasy stools and poor growth despite adequate nutrition in early years. No family history of genetic disorders.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Barrel-shaped chest noted. Clubbing of fingernails and toenails present. Decreased breath sounds bilaterally with scattered crackles throughout lung fields. Oxygen saturation drops to 88% with mild exertion. Weight and BMI below expected for age.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest shows bronchiectasis with bronchial wall thickening, bronchial dilation greater than accompanying vessels, and patchy bronchial wall opacification. Pulmonary function testing demonstrates both obstructive and restrictive patterns with reduced FEV1 (45% predicted) and FVC.

puzzles.explanation: Classic early-onset CF with systemic manifestations including malabsorption, bronchiectasis, and multiorgan involvement. Definitive diagnosis via sweat chloride test.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 8-year-old child. Parents report slowness and awkwardness with movement since early childhood. Stiffness in legs, more noticeable on one side. Difficulty with fine motor tasks like buttoning shirts. No recent change in symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walks independently but with a characteristic pattern: one leg tends to stay extended and turns inward; walks on tiptoes on that side. Difficulty running and climbing stairs. Balance problems when turning quickly. Trips over own feet frequently.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms present since infancy; parents noticed stiffness and asymmetrical movement by 6 months old. Born after complicated labour; required resuscitation at birth. Early developmental milestones (sitting, walking) delayed by 3-4 months. No progressive worsening over past 5 years. No family history of neuromuscular disease.

clues.label: Examination
