clues.text: Unable to tolerate prolonged upright posture (meetings, shopping). Symptoms improve when supine or sitting. Previously active but now mostly sedentary.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began abruptly 3 months after viral illness. No prior cardiovascular or autonomic symptoms. Lives in warm climate. Family history of syncope.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Supine HR 68, BP 110/70. Standing 2 minutes: HR 118, BP 108/68. No presyncope relief with squatting. Normal cardiac auscultation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Tilt table test: HR rises from 68 to 102 bpm within 3 minutes upright, sustained throughout 10-minute test. Systolic BP drops <10 mmHg. Plasma norepinephrine abnormally elevated supine (>600 pg/mL). Echocardiogram and Holter monitor normal.

puzzles.explanation: Severe POTS presentation with autonomic hyperactivity and possible mast cell activation. Marked disability despite therapeutic interventions indicates complex autonomic dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old athlete with recurrent episodes of lateral ankle instability and a catching or popping sensation with activity over the past 8 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate cutting and pivoting movements in sport. Pain and instability worse with rapid ankle plantarflexion and inversion, such as during change of direction drills.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury followed an inversion ankle sprain 8 months ago. At the time of the sprain, imaging suggested peroneal tendon involvement. Episodes of instability have been intermittent but recurring with specific movements.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable subluxation of peroneal tendons posterior to lateral malleolus with ankle position changes. Positive peroneal tendon subluxation test. Pain with resisted eversion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound and dynamic MRI show peroneal tendons subluxing posterior to lateral malleolus with ankle plantarflexion and inversion. Superior peroneal retinaculum laxity noted.

puzzles.explanation: Peroneal tendon subluxation causes dynamic instability from loss of retinacular support.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old with long-standing smoking history. Reports gradual onset shortness of breath that has worsened over several years. Persistent cough, mostly dry. Denies acute trauma or recent illness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with stairs and walking distances that used to be manageable. Symptoms worse with exertion, better with rest. Unable to keep up with daily household tasks. No relief from position changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: 40+ year smoking history. Symptoms began insidiously 5-10 years ago, progressive decline. Multiple exacerbations requiring treatment over past 2-3 years. Tried inhalers with minimal sustained benefit. No acute precipitant for current decline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced breath sounds throughout lung fields. Prolonged expiratory phase noted. Use of neck and shoulder muscles during breathing. Barrel-shaped chest. Pursed-lip breathing pattern observed. SpO2 drops with minimal activity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spirometry: FEV1/FVC ratio <70%, FEV1 significantly reduced (severity varies). Chest X-ray: hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms, increased anteroposterior diameter. ABG: hypoxemia at baseline, elevated CO2 in advanced cases.

puzzles.explanation: Non-smoking COPD presentation from biomass exposure (common in developing countries). Demonstrates that smoking is not the only etiology of airway obstruction and emphysema.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with involuntary loss of urine during specific activities. Onset gradual over several years with progressive functional limitation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot exercise or engage in high-impact activities. Leakage occurs predictably with coughing, sneezing, heavy lifting, and jumping. Avoids social situations due to unpredictable episodes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple vaginal deliveries. No history of neurological or spinal cord injury. Symptoms correlate with physical exertion, not urgency. Improves when lying down.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weak pelvic floor on assessment. Stress test positive. Bladder neck hypermobile. No urgency incontinence component.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Urodynamics confirms stress incontinence. Sphincter incompetence documented. Normal detrusor function without overactivity.

puzzles.explanation: Stress Urinary Incontinence shows mixed incontinence (stress + urge) with overactive bladder component and elevated post-void residual in an older multiparous woman.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 19-year-old presents 2 weeks post-incident with persistent headaches, difficulty concentrating, and feeling 'off balance'. Reports memory lapses for recent events.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cognitive difficulties including memory problems and concentration issues. Headaches daily with light and sound sensitivity. Dizziness and balance difficulty. Functional limitation with school work.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Witnessed loss of consciousness at scene lasting several hours. Post-traumatic amnesia extends beyond the period of unconsciousness. ICU monitoring initiated due to concern for intracranial injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mild cognitive deficits noted on basic testing. Photophobia and phonophobia present. Balance testing shows mild deficit. Headache reproducible with movement. Mood irritability noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Initial CT brain showed small epidural hematoma (managed conservatively). Follow-up CT normal. MRI shows diffuse axonal injury. Neuropsych testing shows mild cognitive deficits.

puzzles.explanation: Moderate traumatic brain injury with loss of consciousness, subdural hematoma, and diffuse axonal injury secondary to high-velocity motor vehicle collision.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old man, 4 weeks post-rotator cuff repair, begins physiotherapy. Arm immobilized with sling. Pain controlled on medication.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe restriction in arm use. Cannot elevate arm overhead or use arm for functional tasks. Pain increases sharply beyond approximately 70 degrees of flexion or 30 degrees of external rotation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute shoulder injury from fall. Imaging confirmed full-thickness rotator cuff tear. Recent surgical repair with protected postoperative protocol. Currently in early mobilization phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive ROM: abduction 65°, external rotation 30°, flexion 70°. Significant scapulohumeral rhythm guarding and pain-inhibited movement. No neurovascular deficits. Pain 3/10 at rest, 5/10 with passive ROM attempts.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows successful tendon repair with intact suture fixation. Minimal retraction. Post-operative edema present. No re-tear on imaging. Surgical integrity maintained.

puzzles.explanation: Post-rotator cuff repair requires strict immobilization and protection during early healing phases, followed by cautious progression of passive then active-assisted movements, scapular stabilization training, and eventual strengthening to restore function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete reports sudden-onset knee pain and instability during a pivot-and-plant movement. The knee 'gave way' during activity and has not been the same since.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform directional changes or cutting movements without fear of knee buckling. Weight-bearing in slight knee flexion with trunk rotation reproduces apprehension.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient is a contact sport athlete with a family history of knee instability. This is the first episode of acute instability; no prior dislocations or subluxations reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive apprehension test indicates fear of redislocation. Medial restraint testing reveals ligamentous insufficiency. Quadriceps strength is diminished. Swelling and joint effusion are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate a patella that is reduced in anatomical position with possible osteochondral findings. MRI shows acute ligamentous disruption of the medial patellofemoral ligament. Imaging confirms acute patellar dislocation.

puzzles.explanation: Acute patellar dislocation is traumatic and usually reduces spontaneously but leaves significant effusion and apprehension.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman living independently presents to the emergency department following a fall from standing height at home, reporting severe right hip and groin pain with inability to weight-bear.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She was unable to stand or take any steps after the fall and required an ambulance. Prior to the fall she was ambulatory with a walking stick indoors, and her mobility had been declining over the preceding months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of osteoporosis with declining bone density despite supplementation. Two previous falls in the last year without fracture, but this fall occurred from standing height only. On warfarin for atrial fibrillation, creating perioperative management complexity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The right lower limb is shortened and externally rotated at rest. There is marked tenderness over the greater trochanteric and proximal thigh region. Any attempt at active hip movement produces severe pain, and a positive log-roll test is noted with axial loading.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic AP X-ray demonstrates a fracture line below the femoral neck in the region between the greater and lesser trochanters. The fracture is classified as an unstable 4-part intertrochanteric pattern, and orthopaedic referral for dynamic hip screw fixation is arranged.

puzzles.explanation: Intertrochanteric fracture complications from severe osteoporosis may lead to loss of fixation or delayed healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old man reports persistent morning stiffness and aching in the shoulders and hips over the past 6 months. Symptoms gradually worsened before seeking care.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to overhead reach or comb hair due to shoulder pain and stiffness. Hip stiffness limits rising from chair and walking distance. Symptoms worst in early morning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent-onset bilateral shoulder and hip pain in a patient with elevated inflammatory markers. Minimal prior joint disease history. Rapid symptom progression over 2–3 months before presentation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral shoulder abduction and external rotation limited to 120° and 45° respectively. Hip flexion and internal rotation restricted. Proximal strength 4+/5 but pain-limited. No focal joint swelling or warmth. Grip strength normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ESR 52 mm/hr, CRP elevated at 8.2 mg/dL. No evidence of temporal artery tenderness or jaw claudication. Temporal artery ultrasound normal, excluding concurrent giant cell arteritis.

puzzles.explanation: Polymyalgia Rheumatica is complicated by Giant Cell Arteritis (temporal arteritis) with vascular manifestations including headache, jaw claudication, and visual symptoms requiring urgent intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old recreational runner with a 3-month history of posterior ankle pain that worsens during training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is worse with downhill running and explosive plantarflexion activities. Activities requiring repetitive push-off are increasingly restricted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual progressive symptoms with running. No specific traumatic event. Previous ankle sprain history. Tried relative rest with minimal improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Posterior ankle tenderness with palpation. Pain with passive plantarflexion and inversion positioning. Flexor hallucis longus palpable and tender. Pain reproduced with plantarflexion resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates posterior ankle impingement with flexor hallucis longus involvement. Soft tissue inflammation in posterior compartment. No bony pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Symptomatic os trigonum syndrome causes chronic posterior ankle pain unresponsive to conservative treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old woman presents with progressive swelling of the right arm and hand over the past 2 years. Gradual onset, no acute trauma. Reports heaviness and difficulty with fitting of rings and bracelets.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to wear normal-fitting sleeves. Describes affected arm as persistently heavy. Increased difficulty with overhead activities and fine motor tasks of the hand.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Right breast cancer with axillary dissection 3 years prior. No recurrent cancer. Gradual progressive swelling over 2-year period.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Right arm swelling with pitting edema initially, now non-pitting and indurated. Circumference difference 4 cm at midarm. Skin thickened and fibrosed. Reduced shoulder ROM secondary to edema.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates impaired lymphatic drainage in right upper extremity. Ultrasound shows increased soft tissue thickness in affected arm. Diameter discrepancy documented.

puzzles.explanation: Secondary lymphedema post-cancer surgery, most common cause in developed countries. Arm circumference difference and fibrotic changes confirm diagnosis. Management focuses on compression and exercise.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old competitive gymnast reports wrist pain with apparatus training. Pain worsens with hand weight-bearing and wrist extension.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform landings or apparatus dismounts. Grip-based training also provokes pain. Symptoms worse after high-volume training days.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 4 weeks ago during intensive off-season conditioning. No acute injury event. Increased training volume preceding onset. No prior wrist injury or treatment.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pinpoint tenderness over distal radial physeal region. Wrist ROM full in flexion/extension and radial/ulnar deviation. No significant effusion. Grip strength intact. Pain reproduced with loaded wrist extension but not isolated passive motion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Wrist X-rays show widened distal radial growth plate with possible irregularity. MRI shows inflammatory changes at growth plate. Physis intact without displacement.

puzzles.explanation: Growth plate fractures require careful management to prevent premature closure and subsequent limb length or alignment complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old woman in nursing home. One week post-operatively from hip surgery. Staff noticed localized area of skin discoloration over sacrum. Patient reports the area feels tender but denies significant pain. No open wounds or drainage noted by patient.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Bedbound and immobilized during post-operative recovery. Unable to reposition independently. Relies entirely on staff for turning and positioning changes. Spends prolonged periods in supine and semi-recumbent positions. Mobility expected to remain limited for several more weeks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hip fracture with surgical repair 7 days ago. No prior skin breakdown or tissue damage documented. Patient compliant with turning schedule when staff available. Nutritional intake adequate. No diabetes, vascular disease, or other comorbidities affecting skin integrity. Incontinence: occasional, managed with pads.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized area of persistent redness over sacral prominence. Redness does NOT blanch (lighten) when skin is pressed with fingertip. Skin surface completely intact—no blisters, cracks, or open areas. Underlying tissue feels firm and slightly swollen. Skin temperature normal. Sensation preserved in area. No signs of infection.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Bedside assessment with light blanching test performed. Prolonged capillary refill time in affected area when pressure applied and released. No ultrasound or advanced imaging needed. Clinical findings consistent with early tissue response to sustained mechanical pressure without yet breaching the skin barrier.

puzzles.explanation: Early pressure injury recognition allows intervention with repositioning, pressure relief, and skin care to prevent wound progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete experienced acute knee pain and instability during a twisting pivot-and-plant maneuver in sport. The knee gave way suddenly, and they felt something shift in the knee before it felt 'normal' again moments later.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform cutting or pivoting movements in sport. Apprehension and fear of the knee giving way limit weight-bearing during lateral movements and weight shifts. Patient reports needing to modify or avoid multi-directional agility tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute patellar dislocation occurred during athletic activity with a pivot-and-plant mechanism. Underlying anatomical predisposition such as increased Q-angle or ligamentous laxity may be present. This is the initial episode of dislocation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Apprehension test reproduces fear of redislocation. Lachman and anterior drawer tests are normal, ruling out ACL injury. Patellar tracking is abnormal with lateral glide preference. Moderate joint effusion present. VMO strength noticeably diminished compared to VL.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals acute tear of the medial patellofemoral ligament with bone marrow edema. Small osteochondral fragment visible on lateral femoral condyle. No evidence of other knee ligament injuries or meniscal pathology. Patella is concentrically reduced.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic recurrent patellar dislocation often involves underlying anatomical factors and systemic hypermobility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old female ballet dancer presents with a clicking sensation around the hip that started 12 months ago. Initially painless, it has recently become painful with activity and is affecting her training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The snap occurs reproducibly during hip flexion-extension and lateral movement patterns typical of her dance routine, with sharp brief pain that worsens with rehearsal intensity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She has no history of hip trauma or prior hip pathology. The onset coincided with a significant increase in her dance training hours twelve months ago. She notes the sound is audible to others in the room during warm-up exercises.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive hip flexion-extension reproduces the snap palpable over the anterior-lateral hip. Ober's test is positive. Hip ROM is full with no restrictions. Pain is elicited with resisted hip flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the hip are normal with no bony abnormality. Dynamic ultrasound imaging confirms snapping of the iliotibial band over the greater trochanter during the provocative movement, with no underlying bursitis or labral pathology identified.

puzzles.explanation: Posterior snapping from ligamentum teres is less common but may coexist with hip instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old woman. Gradual onset over 6 months of persistent dry sensation in eyes and mouth. Reports constant thirst despite adequate fluid intake and frequent need to drink water with meals.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty reading and working on screens for extended periods. Trouble eating dry or hard foods—requires liquids to swallow bread, crackers, nuts. Dry mouth worsens toward evening and with speaking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious progressive onset without trauma or infection trigger. Associated with fatigue and mild symmetric joint aching in hands. No prior autoimmune diagnoses. Symptoms worsening over months despite conservative care.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Schirmer test: <5 mm tear production bilaterally in 5 minutes. Oral mucosa appears pale and sticky; reduced salivary pooling noted. Slit lamp examination shows punctate epithelial keratopathy. No parotid enlargement palpable. Mild synovitis in MCP joints.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Anti-SSA/Ro and anti-SSB/La antibodies positive. Rheumatoid factor and ANA elevated. Labial salivary gland biopsy shows focal lymphocytic infiltration with focus score >1.

puzzles.explanation: Sjögren's syndrome requires multidisciplinary approach including symptom management, salivary and lacrimal gland support, management of systemic manifestations, monitoring for complications like lymphoma, and fatigue management strategies.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old painter reports lateral elbow pain that started 6 weeks ago. Pain is worse with work activities but he cannot pinpoint a specific injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to work at full capacity. Pain limits ability to perform sustained gripping and any forceful wrist movements. Symptoms worsen as the day progresses with continued activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms started without a specific injury event. Patient has continued working despite pain. No prior elbow injuries or treatment. No systemic illness or medications relevant to joint pain. Pain does not wake him at night.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lateral elbow tenderness at epicondyle. Painful resisted wrist extension. Mill's test positive with pain reproduction. Grip strength reduced bilaterally more on affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound common extensor origin shows tendinopathic changes with heterogeneity. Mild surrounding edema. No complete rupture. Lateral collateral ligament intact on assessment.

puzzles.explanation: Lateral epicondylitis in an occupational context (not tennis-related), highlighting that repetitive gripping and wrist extension in manual workers is the most common cause.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old construction worker. Sudden onset severe pain in elbow region after direct traumatic blow to arm. Rapid swelling. Arm held protectively and immobile. Patient reports feeling 'something shifted' in the joint at moment of injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Complete loss of elbow flexion and extension. Unable to move arm at elbow joint in any direction. Severe pain prevents active motion. Arm hangs in rigid, semi-flexed posture. Unable to lift, carry, or support any weight through affected limb.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy mechanism: caught between machinery with significant compressive force. Immediate onset; no prior elbow pathology or instability. Patient transported to ED within 30 minutes. No attempted self-reduction. First major joint injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Elbow held in fixed position with visible asymmetry compared to contralateral side. Palpable abnormal bony prominence. Unable to achieve passive flexion or extension without severe pain. Neurovascular exam: distal pulses present, capillary refill normal, sensation intact in radial and ulnar distributions. No focal neurological deficit detected.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs (AP and lateral views) show ulna and radius displaced posterolaterally relative to distal humerus. Olecranon process no longer articulating with humeral trochlea. Associated fracture lines visible at olecranon and coronoid process. CT confirms displacement direction and associated bone fragments. Post-reduction imaging shows anatomic reduction achieved.

puzzles.explanation: Recurrent/chronic elbow instability after initial dislocation in young athlete with functional deficits.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 81-year-old woman reports progressive difficulty with stairs and rising from a chair over the past 18 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to carry groceries or perform household tasks. Requires assistance with dressing and bathing. Lives with family due to safety concerns.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong relatively low physical activity. Post-menopausal with inadequate nutrition. Recent decline in function over 1-2 years. Multiple fall history from weakness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked generalized weakness bilaterally. Slow gait with small steps. Hip and knee strength severely reduced 2-3/5. Grip strength markedly diminished. Visible wasting.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA and bioelectrical impedance analysis show appendicular lean mass index <5.4 kg/m² (female threshold for sarcopenia). Muscle quality severely diminished.

puzzles.explanation: Severe sarcopenia in advanced age creates profound functional loss and vulnerability to complications from minimal stress.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman with progressive groin and medial thigh pain reports numbness and weakness. Pain radiates along medial thigh. Progressive symptoms over 2 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports functional limitation with hip flexion past 90 degrees and internal rotation. Pain worsens with prolonged walking. No difficulty with abduction or external rotation. Able to perform most daily activities but avoids stairs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No preceding trauma or injury. Patient denies recent hip surgery or groin procedures. No history of pelvic mass or malignancy. Symptoms began insidiously while doing yoga with deep hip flexion and adduction positions. No improvement with rest or NSAIDs over 2 months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip adductor (gracilis, adductor longus) strength 4/5 bilaterally, weaker on right. Hip flexion strength intact. Palpable tenderness at groin with hip flexion and internal rotation. Hip abduction strength normal. No gluteal involvement. Femoral nerve examination normal. Adductor reflex diminished on right.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows nerve compression at obturator foramen with signal change in obturator nerve. Possible anatomic variation or hypertrophied obturator externus. No mass. Compression correlates with clinical distribution of sensory loss on medial thigh below knee.

puzzles.explanation: Post-surgical obturator nerve injury with selective hip adductor weakness and medial thigh sensory loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 31-year-old with acute wrist pain and swelling after fall. Wrist held protectively. Unable to bear weight through hand or perform fine motor tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with radial/ulnar deviation and gripping. Swelling visible within 30 minutes. Bruising emerging over dorsal wrist.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall occurred during sport. Patient recalled immediate sharp pain but continued activity briefly before realising severity. No prior wrist injury. Wearing standard shoes, no medical comorbidities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wrist swelling and ecchymosis present throughout dorsal wrist. Wrist motion limited by pain in all planes. Neurovascular intact distally. Scaphoid and lunate palpable but no isolated step-off tenderness. Ligamentous laxity testing inconclusive due to pain and swelling.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays negative for fracture. MRI shows soft tissue injury between scaphoid and lunate with surrounding edema and fluid. No skeletal involvement. Ligamentous structures intact. Consistent with moderate wrist sprain.

puzzles.explanation: Acute wrist sprain with scapholunate ligament injury from typical FOOSH mechanism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 32-year-old athlete reports sudden onset groin pain during sport. Sharp quality, acute functional loss.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain provoked specifically with hip adduction and hip flexion/external rotation activities. Difficulty with side-stepping and diagonal movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior groin injuries. Recent increase in training volume over past 3 weeks. Flexible hips but limited adductor flexibility. Warm-up completed before injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness along medial thigh in longitudinal distribution. Pain on resisted hip adduction (30° hip flexion). Adductor bulk palpable but no obvious discontinuity. Antalgic gait with hip adduction avoidance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows muscle fiber disruption with surrounding inflammation. Hematoma forming. Adductor muscle involvement confirmed. No complete rupture.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic adductor strain develops gradually from overuse or inadequate flexibility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old man with chronic dizziness for 10 months reports constant swaying sensation. Balance difficulty with movement. Anxiety about symptoms prominent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Avoids crowded environments and driving. Symptoms worse when focusing on visual targets or when walking in open spaces. Social withdrawal due to fear of symptom onset in public.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mild head injury with brief dizziness 10 months prior. Dizziness persisted after physical recovery. Progressive anxiety worsened symptoms. Multiple specialist evaluations unrevealing. Hyperawareness of bodily sensations and catastrophizing about movements.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Postural sway present but mild on standing. Dizziness reproducible with rapid head turns and gaze shifts. Romberg test normal; tandem stance mild instability. Hyperventilation provokes dizziness and lightheadedness. Nystagmus absent. Dix–Hallpike and head roll tests negative. Fear-avoidance behaviour observed during testing. Symptoms intensify with attention to balance task.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Caloric testing normal, head impulse test normal. Videonystagmography within normal limits. MRI head and internal auditory canals normal. No evidence of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, vestibular neuritis, or central pathology. Diagnosis supported by persistent symptoms despite normal investigations and psychological overlay.

puzzles.explanation: Persistent Postural-Perceptual Dizziness shows primary PPPD without documented prior vestibular event, with prominent anxiety and visual/postural triggers with completely normal objective testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 15-year-old basketball player with knee pain during intensive training. Onset gradual over past 4-6 weeks, worse with jumping and running.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate plyometric training or high-impact sports. Symptoms worsen with repetitive loading. Some relief with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Intensive training program with frequent jumping and running. Rapid growth during adolescent spurt. Inadequate recovery between training sessions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized tenderness over medial aspect of proximal tibia. Pain reproduction on single-leg hop test. No effusion. Knee ligament stability tests normal. Full ROM.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Widened growth plate with increased T2 signal on MRI at proximal tibial physis. No metaphyseal fracture line or avulsion. Physis remains open and intact.

puzzles.explanation: Growth plate injury complications include premature closure causing limb length and angular deformities requiring corrective surgery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 29-year-old rugby player injured their ankle during a tackle. Reports pain above the ankle joint, worse with pivoting and weight-bearing movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear weight comfortably or change direction quickly. Pain reproduced with external rotation movements of the ankle.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 3 days ago. Patient reports immediate swelling and severe pain, preventing return to sport. No prior ankle injuries. No physiotherapy yet attempted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Syndesmotic tenderness above ankle joint, between tibia and fibula. Pain with external rotation stress test positive. Squeeze test positive (pain with compression of tibia/fibula).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ankle X-rays with weight-bearing show widened tibiofibular space suggesting syndesmotic disruption. Stress radiographs demonstrate increased talar rotation. CT further characterizes injury.

puzzles.explanation: High ankle sprains involve the syndesmotic ligaments and carry risk of chronic pain and instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old male boxer presents with acute hand pain and swelling after a punch during a match.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot make a closed fist. Inability to clench hand without sharp pain over the knuckle area.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 2 hours ago during training. Player felt immediate sharp pain and heard/felt a crack. No prior hand injuries or fracture history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Fifth metacarpal head swelling, ecchymosis, and angulation. Pain with palpation over fracture site. Prominence of knuckle reduced (depressed fracture appearance).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand X-rays show fifth metacarpal neck fracture with apex dorsal angulation (classic 'boxer's fracture'). Displacement and angulation measured on X-rays.

puzzles.explanation: Boxer's fracture shows fifth metacarpal neck fracture from punching mechanism with typical angulation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old recreational tennis player presents with posterior elbow pain that worsens with forceful arm movements. Pain began 4 weeks ago and has been gradually worsening.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform overhead throwing or serving motions. Pushing and pressing movements (like bench press or wall push-ups) reproduce pain. Training intensity significantly reduced.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Increased tennis training volume focusing on serving technique. Repetitive overhead serving causing triceps overload. Progressive symptoms over 4 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked tenderness over triceps insertion at olecranon. Resisted elbow extension reproduces sharp pain. Triceps strength 4/5 due to pain inhibition. Possible palpable swelling or thickening at insertion site.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows triceps tendon thickening at insertion with heterogeneous echotexture. Possible small tear or chronic inflammation. Olecrannon bursa may show fluid.

puzzles.explanation: Triceps tendinopathy from repetitive extension and throwing activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old woman with progressive low back pain and neurogenic claudication over 2 years. Pain radiates to buttock and upper thigh. Unable to walk beyond 100 metres before requiring rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged standing and walking downhill. Significant relief with sitting or forward bending. Difficulty with stairs and sustained postures. Unable to work as retail supervisor due to prolonged standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prior non-operative management failed (6 months physiotherapy and analgesia). Imaging showed L4-L5 disc degeneration with facet arthropathy and central stenosis with intermittent neurogenic claudication. Symptoms progressively worsened over 2 years despite conservative care. Underwent lumbar fusion 6 weeks ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Current post-operative presentation: Lumbar ROM: flexion 20°, extension 10°, lateral flexion 15° bilaterally. Straight leg raise 70° bilaterally, pain-free. Hip and knee ROM full and pain-free. Neurological examination normal. Incision well-healed, no signs of infection. Pain 2-3/10 at rest, 4-5/10 with movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-ray (6 weeks): Fusion hardware properly positioned across L4-L5. Lateral view shows appropriate restoration of disc height and sagittal alignment. No evidence of hardware loosening, malposition, or breakage. Early bony bridging between vertebral bodies visible on CT.

puzzles.explanation: Post-lumbar fusion rehabilitation requires long-term graduated progression, emphasis on core stabilization without spinal motion, modification of activity patterns, and realistic expectations for return to function given the extensive surgical trauma and fusion site healing requirements.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 32-year-old manufacturing worker. Onset of cough and chest tightness over past 8 weeks. Denies fever, recent illness, or smoking history. No prior respiratory diagnosis. Reports general fatigue and occasional wheezing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms noticeably worse during work shifts; improve significantly on days off and during vacation week. Difficulty with sustained exertion at work. Chest feels tight mid-shift. Symptoms resolve within 24–48 hours away from workplace.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Works on assembly line with exposure to chemical aerosols and dust. Started current role 6 months ago. Symptoms appeared after 2 months on job. History of seasonal allergies. No prior lung disease, no significant sick leave before this year.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Peak expiratory flow (PEF) readings show >20% variability between work days and rest days. Spirometry demonstrates reversible airflow obstruction (FEV1/FVC 0.65 improving to 0.78 post-bronchodilator). Chest clear to auscultation. No signs of cardiac disease.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Methacholine bronchial challenge test positive (PC20 <8 mg/mL). Specific IgE testing identifies sensitization to workplace chemical exposure. Chest X-ray normal; no infiltrates, hyperinflation, or structural abnormality.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational asthma shows work-triggered bronchoconstriction from occupational sensitization or irritant exposure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old otherwise healthy adult presents with sudden onset severe vertigo upon waking this morning. No hearing loss reported. Recent upper respiratory viral illness 5 days prior to symptom onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vertigo is constant and disabling, with dizziness exacerbated by head movements and eye closure. Patient experiences significant nausea with vomiting. Symptoms slightly improve with bed rest and head immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior episodes of vertigo. Patient reports gradual return to upright posture tolerance over 48 hours. No medication changes. Works in office environment with minimal physical demands.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Head impulse test shows positive catch-up saccade indicating unilateral vestibular deficit. Dix-Hallpike maneuver negative excluding BPPV. Caloric testing shows reduced response in affected ear (50% unilateral paresis). Romberg test positive with falls toward affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI inner ear structures normal, no T2/FLAIR signal abnormality in IAC. No enhancement of CN VIII. Auditory evoked responses normal. Videonystagmography shows reduced caloric response ipsilateral to positive head impulse test.

puzzles.explanation: Vestibular Neuritis shows subacute recovery phase with persistent unilateral vestibular deficits, oscillopsia, and ongoing rehabilitation needs.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old dancer reports acute onset anterior thigh pain during rapid knee flexion and hip extension movement. Sharp discomfort with immediate functional loss and swelling over anterior thigh.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain reproduced with resisted knee flexion and combined hip extension. Unable to perform high kicks or explosive lunges. Weight-bearing through leg possible but significantly limited. Difficulty with stairs and sustained standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sudden onset during ballistic warm-up movement. No prior thigh injury. Symptoms started acutely without preceding warning signs. Inadequate dynamic stretching prior to activity. Immediate pain and loss of load tolerance.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness localised to rectus femoris muscle belly, mid-anterior thigh. Pain with resisted knee flexion and combined hip extension. Possible small myofascial defect palpable on direct compression. Rectus femoris strength 4/5 secondary to pain. Hip flexor strength intact. Swelling and bruising visible over anterior thigh.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows partial muscle fiber disruption of rectus femoris with surrounding edema and intramuscular fluid collection. No complete muscle rupture or retraction. Strain injury with acute inflammation and minimal haematoma.

puzzles.explanation: Acute adductor strains occur with explosive side-to-side or rotational movements, particularly in sports.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old motorcyclist presents 3 weeks after a fall with shoulder dislocation. Reports progressive weakness in upper arm and shoulder with associated pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Weakness predominantly affects shoulder abduction and external rotation with inability to lift arm against gravity. Patient reports altered sensation in lateral shoulder and thumb region.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Post-injury imaging demonstrated edema and hemorrhage in brachial plexus region. No prior neurological history. Patient reports symptom progression over 3 weeks with no improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination shows Erb's palsy pattern: waiter's tip position of arm with internal rotation, adduction, and elbow extension. Supraspinatus/infraspinatus atrophy visible. Upper limb reflexes (biceps, brachioradialis) diminished or absent on affected side. Horner syndrome absent (excluding lower trunk).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG/NCS shows acute denervation potentials in biceps, supraspinatus, and deltoid muscles with slowed conduction velocities. Motor amplitudes reduced in axillary and suprascapular nerves. MRI demonstrates brachial plexus edema and hemorrhage at level of upper trunk with partial continuity.

puzzles.explanation: Severe brachial plexus injury from high-energy trauma with persistent deficits.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old office worker reports progressive clumsiness of both hands over 6 months. Difficulty with fine motor tasks like buttoning and writing. Also notes unsteady gait and increased stumbling. Wife reports patient walking more cautiously and using wider stance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Hands show more functional loss than expected from strength alone—dropping objects, trouble manipulating small items. Gait worsens on stairs and uneven ground. Difficulty with rapid hand movements and coordination. Arm swing reduced bilaterally. Symptoms progress throughout day with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Decades of intermittent neck stiffness and occasional pain. Motor vehicle accident 5 years ago—no fracture at that time. Symptoms began insidiously 6 months ago without clear trigger. Progressive course over this period. Smoker, age 58. No recent trauma or acute event.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hyperreflexia in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Positive Hoffmann's sign bilaterally. Gait shows spasticity and imbalance. Fine finger coordination impaired—difficulty with rapid alternating movements. Mild weakness in intrinsic hand muscles. Sensory testing reveals loss of vibration sense in feet. Neck extension reproduces lower limb symptoms slightly.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine demonstrates multilevel degenerative changes with osteophytes at C5-6 and C6-7. Spinal canal narrowed to 10mm at most compressed level. T2-weighted imaging shows central cord signal hyperintensity indicating myelopathic edema. No acute fracture or instability. Cord appears compressed between osteophytes and posterior ligament.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical myelopathy is chronic and insidious with subtle early signs that progress to significant dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 34-year-old woman with 4 days of progressive weakness starting in the legs. Reports gastroenteritis with diarrhea about 10 days ago, which seemed to resolve. Now weakness is worsening and spreading upward.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk independently. Difficulty speaking clearly and chewing. Has noticed mild shortness of breath with exertion. Weakness symmetric on both sides of body.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior neurological or autoimmune disease. Vaccination history unremarkable. Symptoms appeared suddenly 4 days after GI illness resolved, and have progressed daily without improvement. No similar presentations in family.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral lower extremity weakness symmetric. Absent reflexes throughout. Ascending pattern with hip and knee involvement. Upper extremity strength testing begins to show weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CSF shows elevated protein with minimal cells (albuminocytologic dissociation). EMG shows demyelination pattern. Nerve conduction studies show conduction blocks. Respiratory function monitored.

puzzles.explanation: Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy with classic ascending paralysis, areflexia, and respiratory compromise in post-infectious setting.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 65-year-old man of Northern European ancestry presents with progressive difficulty extending his hand. He first noticed a firm lump in his palm 2 years ago and has increasingly struggled to straighten his fingers, particularly the ring and little fingers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot easily perform activities requiring open palm positioning—difficulty with writing, typing, and personal hygiene. Grip strength subjectively reduced but pain minimal.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Family history of similar hand contractures in father and uncle. Northern European ancestry (Swedish descent). Age-related presentation typical for Dupuytren disease. No significant trauma or repetitive hand use occupation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination reveals firm palmar nodules along flexor surface. Palpable longitudinal cords extending from palm into ring and little finger. Ring finger MCP joint flexion contracture 45 degrees, PIP involvement minimal. Table top test fails with inability to place palm flat on surface. Skin dimpling and pit formation noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates hypoechoic fascial cords extending from palm into ring and little finger rays with MCP joint involvement. No evidence of nerve or vessel entrapment. Severity graded moderate.

puzzles.explanation: Progressive Dupuytren's contracture in middle-aged male with family history and progressive functional loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old recreational tennis player presents with anterior shoulder pain for 3 weeks. Pain began during overhead serve motion. Reports occasional clicking sensation in shoulder during reaching.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sharp anterior shoulder pain with overhead activities, serving, and throwing motions. Pain exacerbated by resisted elbow flexion and supination. Discomfort improves with rest and ice. Patient experiences pain at night when lying on affected shoulder.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Intensive tennis participation (4 times weekly) over the past 12 months. Gradual symptom onset rather than traumatic incident. No previous shoulder surgery. Age 52 with no prior upper limb pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Screening examination revealed weakness and poor control of rotator cuff musculature. Speed's test positive with anterior shoulder pain. Palpable tenderness over biceps groove. Subacromial space appears narrowed clinically.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates biceps tendon thickening (5mm) with hypoechoic signal and increased vascularity on Doppler imaging consistent with tendinopathy. Subacromial space narrowed suggesting associated subacromial impingement. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration of proximal biceps tendon without full thickness tear.

puzzles.explanation: Biceps tendinopathy with underlying instability as dynamic stabilizer is compromised.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged office worker presents with hand paresthesias and numbness. Symptoms are worse at night and disturb sleep. Fine motor tasks and gripping activities feel more difficult than usual.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptom pattern is distinctly nocturnal—worst at night and upon waking. Shaking or flicking the hand provides transient relief. Daytime symptoms correlate with repetitive hand-intensive work but are far less bothersome than night symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began insidiously 3–4 months ago during a period of increased keyboard and administrative work. Patient denies preceding trauma or acute injury. Similar symptoms reported in contralateral hand but milder. No prior episodes of paresthesias or neurological symptoms elsewhere.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, Tinel's sign is positive over the median nerve at the wrist. Phalen's test reproduces symptoms after 60 seconds. Two-point discrimination is reduced in median-innervated fingers. Grip strength is diminished.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyographic testing demonstrates median nerve conduction slowing across the wrist with fibrillation potentials. Nerve conduction studies confirm focal demyelination at the carpal tunnel. The diagnosis is established by neurophysiological testing.

puzzles.explanation: Work-related carpal tunnel syndrome with clear occupational trigger and ergonomic factor.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old with 2-week history of progressive shortness of breath. Chest feels heavy. Exercise tolerance declining. No fever or cough.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Dyspnea worsens with exertion and lying on left side. Able to walk short distances on flat ground but limited by breathlessness. Positional component—more comfortable sitting upright.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Completed chemotherapy 4 weeks prior for known malignancy. Gradual symptom onset over 2 weeks despite medical management. No recent infection, trauma, or heart failure exacerbation reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Dullness to percussion at bilateral lung bases, more pronounced on left. Reduced breath sounds and tactile fremitus at bases. Oxygen saturation 88% on room air at rest. Egophony present over left base.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray: blunting of left costophrenic angle with meniscus sign. CT confirms moderate left-sided collection (4 cm) and smaller right-sided collection. Pleural fluid aspiration shows exudative characteristics with malignant cells on cytology.

puzzles.explanation: Malignant pleural effusion from primary lung cancer. Often rapid accumulation, high suspicion given cancer history, and positive cytology confirms malignancy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old woman awakens with sudden onset facial droop on right side and cannot close right eye properly. No prior neurological history. Onset occurred overnight without prodrome.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Right lower facial weakness greater than upper face. Complete eye closure impossible on right. Water drips from affected side during drinking. Difficulty with oral intake and speech distortion. Pain in right ear preceding facial weakness by 24 hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Right-sided facial weakness affecting ability to smile, close eye, and maintain oral competence. Water dribbles from mouth during drinking. Difficulty articulating consonants. Note: ear pain preceded weakness onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Complete inability to wrinkle right forehead and close right eye. Asymmetric smile with right lip droop. Light touch sensation normal across all trigeminal divisions. Hearing normal bilaterally. Gag reflex intact. No visible vesicles in external auditory canal, post-auricular area, or palate.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of brain and internal acoustic meatus shows enhancement of right facial nerve within temporal bone but excludes mass, stroke, or other structural lesion. No evidence of parotid gland pathology. Electromyography shows denervation in right facial muscles consistent with acute nerve injury.

puzzles.explanation: Secondary facial nerve palsy from mastoiditis and otitis media with gradual onset, severe otalgia, and imaging evidence of bone involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old recreational athlete presents following acute knee injury during sports. The patient reports sudden-onset knee pain and immediate functional loss. The knee gave way and could not bear weight.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to return to pivoting/cutting sports. Attempts to change direction cause sudden instability and knee collapse. Straight-line walking is tolerated but rotational control is absent.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute injury occurred during a deceleration-plant-and-cut maneuver in sports. Mechanism involved combined flexion, external tibial rotation, and valgus stress. This acute ACL injury requires surgical consultation and management.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Lachman test indicates anterior tibial translation. Positive pivot shift test demonstrates rotational instability. Mild effusion is present. Pain limits examination, but clinical signs are consistent with ACL rupture.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates disruption of the anterior cruciate ligament with discontinuity of fibers. Bone bruising is visualized at the expected locations. Associated meniscal or collateral ligament injuries may be present. Imaging confirms complete ACL tear.

puzzles.explanation: ACL Grade 2 presents as chronic post-injury instability with associated muscle weakness and secondary degenerative changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old woman presents with progressive height loss over the past year and chronic mid-back pain. Recent fall onto outstretched wrist resulted in fracture.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform overhead activities or carry loads due to thoracic pain and kyphosis. Unable to tolerate prolonged standing. Reports increasing fear of falling and declining balance confidence.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Menopause 15 years ago without hormone replacement therapy. History of poor calcium intake and limited outdoor activity. Mother had vertebral fractures in her 70s. Recent corticosteroid use for respiratory condition (6 months, moderate dose).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Thoracic kyphosis 45 degrees measured via digital inclinometry. Spinal palpation tenderness at T7-8 with palpable step-off. Grip strength 18 kg bilaterally. Functional reach test 6 inches. No focal neurological deficit. Pain with spinal extension worse than flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan shows T-score of -2.8 at lumbar spine and -2.5 at femoral neck (osteoporotic range). Vertebral fracture assessment shows compression fracture at T7 (15% height loss). Biochemical markers show elevated P1NP and CTX-1 indicating high bone turnover.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoporosis presents with symptomatic multiple fractures and progressive deformity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 35-year-old man presents with acute onset severe pain in the buttock and posterior thigh following a fall with penetrating trauma. Reports inability to move foot normally and describes dragging sensation in lower leg when walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to lift foot off ground or point toes downward. Difficulty walking with characteristic high-stepping gait to clear the affected limb. Pain and weakness localized to buttock, posterior thigh, and lower leg on one side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 2 days ago from deep laceration to buttock sustained during fall on broken glass. Immediate onset of neurological symptoms at time of injury. No prior nerve problems or chronic conditions. Wound managed but patient reports progressive weakness despite pain control.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Motor deficit: foot drop present, unable to dorsiflex ankle or extend great toe. Sensation reduced in lateral lower leg and dorsum of foot in consistent dermatomal pattern. Tinel sign positive over buttock wound site. Ankle reflex diminished on affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of posterior thigh shows focal nerve swelling and disruption at gluteal region corresponding to wound location. Nerve conduction studies demonstrate prolonged distal motor latencies and reduced compound muscle action potentials in tibial and peroneal divisions only, with normal proximal responses.

puzzles.explanation: Iatrogenic sciatic nerve injury from hip injection with acute onset, motor loss in sciatic distribution, and imaging evidence of nerve trauma.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old man presents 6 weeks after hand surgery for a finger contracture. Reports excellent initial progress with restored movement, but over the past week has noticed increasing tightness returning and range of motion declining.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to fully flatten hand and extend fingers completely. PIP joint of ring finger particularly stiff despite good recovery in early weeks. Recent increased hand use with therapy and work contributing to symptom progression.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Original contracture measured 50 degrees pre-operatively. Surgical plan included complete fascial excision. Increased activity with physical therapy and return to work commenced 4-5 weeks post-op, coinciding with symptom return.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Range of motion measurement shows MCP extension improved to 10 degrees flexion contracture (from pre-op 45 degrees) but PIP extension limitation developing. Scar tissue palpable along surgical site with firmness increasing. Minimal swelling and erythema. No signs of infection or wound complications.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound at 6 weeks post-op shows intact fasciectomy with resected tissue planes visualized. No fluid collection or hematoma. However, early recurrent disease process evident with hypoechoic nodules reforming at operative margins and PIP joint region. Scar tissue shows intermediate echogenicity consistent with early proliferative phase.

puzzles.explanation: Post-fasciotomy rehabilitation requires intensive range of motion work, night-time splinting to prevent recurrence, scar management, edema control, and progressive strengthening while protecting surgical sites and skin grafts.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old office worker presents with acute onset severe shoulder pain yesterday during routine desk work. Sharp anterior shoulder pain unrelated to specific trauma. Pain awakens patient at night with inability to rest arm comfortably.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe limitation with shoulder abduction and internal rotation; reaching across body markedly painful. Pain prevents active motion through guarding; passive range of motion not yet assessed. Discomfort worse with lying on affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No preceding trauma or overuse history. Office work involves minimal overhead demands. Pain intensity markedly disproportionate to mechanism (routine desk task). Similar acute presentations often self-resolve over weeks despite severity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe tenderness over supraspinatus tendon insertion. Passive range of motion better preserved than active motion. Supraspinatus strength testing limited by pain rather than true weakness. Dugas test negative (shoulder can still adduct). Hawkins test markedly positive reproducing sharp pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder X-ray (AP and scapular Y views) demonstrates dense calcium deposit within supraspinatus tendon measuring 8x6mm. Calcium appears radiodense with well-defined borders. Associated soft tissue swelling visible around deposit. MRI shows T2 hypointense deposit with surrounding fluid signal indicating active inflammation and resorption phase.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic calcific tendinopathy with asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic calcium deposits.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old man with 12-year diabetes history presents with progressive symmetrical distal sensory symptoms — numbness and burning pain in feet over the past 2 years, affecting sleep quality.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports loss of protective sensation in feet — recently slipped in shower because unable to sense hot water temperature. Symptoms worse at night, interfering with sleep. No upper limb involvement noted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Poor glycemic control with HbA1c 8.5% throughout disease course. No prior neuropathy screening or intervention. Sedentary lifestyle and obesity (BMI 32). Recent addition of statin medication 6 months ago. Multiple vascular risk factors including hypertension and smoking history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Diminished vibratory sensation at great toe (tuning fork 128Hz lost after 3 seconds versus normal 10+ seconds). Monofilament testing shows loss of protective sensation in both feet using 10g monofilament. Ankle reflexes absent bilaterally. Mild weakness in ankle dorsiflexion 4/5. Proprioception decreased in lower extremities.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction study shows reduced amplitudes in sural sensory response bilaterally with prolonged distal latencies. Motor conduction of peroneal nerve shows reduced velocities (38 m/s). EMG demonstrates chronic denervation changes in foot intrinsic muscles. MRI excludes other spinal causes. Fasting glucose 185 mg/dL.

puzzles.explanation: Painful diabetic neuropathy with prominent neuropathic pain symptoms (burning, allodynia) and confirmed small-fiber involvement on biopsy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 5-month postpartum woman reports persistent midline abdominal wall separation and weakness. Functional limitation with daily activities. Concerned about long-term cosmetic outcome.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 5-month postpartum woman reports persistent abdominal weakness and difficulty with functional activities. Notices visible bulging along midline when exerting herself.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cannot return to pre-pregnancy exercise intensity. Reports pain and bulging with coughing, sneezing, and straining. Difficulty maintaining upright posture for prolonged periods.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible abdominal bulging with contraction. Palpable separation 3.5 fingers wide at umbilicus. Reduced abdominal wall tension. Rectus contraction shows lateral bulging. No signs of hernia.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates diastasis rectus measuring 4.2 cm interrectal distance with thin linea alba. No fascial dehiscence. Cross-sectional imaging confirms bilateral muscle separation.

puzzles.explanation: Diastasis Rectus Abdominis shows persistent separation in multiparous woman one year postpartum with associated postural dysfunction and core weakness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old recreational runner presents with buttock pain for 8 weeks that worsens during runs over 5km. Pain localized to lower buttock region near ischial tuberosity. Recent increase in training volume and intensity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worse during running, especially hills and sprinting. Sitting on hard surfaces increases discomfort (describing difficulty with office chair use). Pain radiates to posterior thigh but does not extend below knee. Morning stiffness in buttock lasting 10-15 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over 8 weeks correlating with increased running mileage (40 to 55 miles per week). No acute traumatic event. Prior history of lower back tightness and hip flexor tightness. Weak gluteal activation noted. Running on cambered surfaces recently.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness over ischial tuberosity region with pain on hamstring contraction against resistance. Positive modified Puranen-Orava test (buttock pain with hip flexion and knee extension). Pain with straight leg raise 70 degrees. Slump test negative (radicular component excluded). Hip internal rotation limited compared to external rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows increased T2 signal in proximal hamstring tendons at ischial attachment with tendon thickening (8mm versus normal 5mm). No full-thickness tear visualized. Associated inflammation around ischial tuberosity. Ultrasound confirms hypoechoic tendon with loss of normal fibrillar pattern and increased vascularity on Doppler imaging at attachment point.

puzzles.explanation: High hamstring tendinopathy can involve the musculotendinous junction, often related to sprint training.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Dyspnea and fatigue

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Worse with exertion and lying flat; improves with sitting upright

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hypertension × 15 years, poorly controlled; recent MI 6 months ago

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: JVD, crackles at lung bases, S3 gallop, peripheral edema, hepatomegaly

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ejection fraction 28%, dilated ventricles, pulmonary edema on CXR

puzzles.explanation: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), a distinct entity from systolic HF. Normal or near-normal EF with diastolic dysfunction is key finding.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 75-year-old osteoporotic woman falls from standing height onto outstretched arm. Immediate severe shoulder pain and inability to move arm. Right shoulder swelling and deformity noted at scene.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot attempt any active shoulder movement. Rapid swelling extends into the upper arm over minutes. Pain severity out of proportion to typical soft tissue injury alone. Severe guarding prevents passive range of motion testing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known osteoporosis with prior fragility fractures. Low-energy mechanism (ground-level fall). No prior shoulder surgery. Immediate symptom onset with no prior warning or prodrome.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shoulder deformity with loss of normal contour. Crepitus palpable at fracture site. Axillary nerve testing shows sensory loss to lateral shoulder and difficulty with deltoid contraction (0/5). Radial pulse intact and capillary refill normal. Sensation intact in radial and ulnar distributions but diminished axillary.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows 4-part proximal humerus fracture with displacement. Greater tuberosity fragment displaced 1.2cm. Humeral head retropulsion present. CT scan confirms comminution with axillary nerve involvement. Angiography normal excluding vascular injury but confirms soft tissue trauma with hematoma.

puzzles.explanation: Complex proximal humerus fracture from high-energy trauma requiring surgical planning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old woman presents with diffuse pain for 5 years affecting multiple body regions. Pain described as burning and aching with atypical features including disproportionate severity and non-mechanical quality.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports light touch causes significant pain (allodynia). Sleep severely disrupted with non-restorative pattern. Symptoms worsen with stress and weather changes. Concurrent memory and concentration difficulties reported.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual insidious onset 5 years ago following minor neck strain. No significant trauma or incident. Multiple negative investigations over years. History of anxiety and depression. Recent major life stressors and significant emotional distress. Pain catastrophizing evident in interview.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination shows widespread tender points (11+ of 18 tender points positive). Allodynia confirmed to light touch across multiple body areas. No neurological deficits, no synovitis, no muscle atrophy. Objective findings notably disproportionate to severity of pain complaints.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Laboratory studies all normal including inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, and thyroid function. MRI cervical and lumbar spine normal without disc herniation or stenosis. Sleep study shows non-restorative sleep pattern (reduced deep sleep stages). Quantitative sensory testing shows lowered pain thresholds throughout body (wind-up amplification confirmed).

puzzles.explanation: Central sensitisation presents as post-viral central sensitization with post-exertional malaise, systemic symptoms, and neuroinflammatory findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old man presenting 8 weeks after major surgery. Reports pain at surgical site, unusual cramping and pressure sensations in the affected area, and significant difficulty with movement and positioning. Expresses frustration about loss of independence and uncertainty about recovery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to move independently from bed to chair or perform transfers without substantial assistance. Severe loss of balance and stability when upright or seated without upper extremity support. Reluctant to participate in prescribed exercises or mobility training. Reports fear of falling and moving the surgical area.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing Type 2 diabetes with poor glycemic control and chronic nerve damage from diabetes. Underwent emergency surgery 8 weeks ago for severe infection that did not respond to conservative treatment. Lives alone with limited family involvement in rehabilitation. Sedentary lifestyle prior to surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Surgical site shows firm scar without drainage, erythema, or dehiscence. Significant swelling in surgical region requiring compression garment. Hip muscles on affected side measure 3+/5 strength. Trunk stability fair; requires hand support for sitting balance. Transfer assessment shows moderate dependence. Mood assessment indicates depression (PHQ-9 = 18). Sensation intact in residual region.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiograph of surgical site shows solid bone fusion at operative level without prominent bony overgrowth. Ultrasound confirms normal scar tissue maturation and appropriate tissue planes. Vascular study shows adequate blood perfusion to surgical site (ankle-brachial index 0.7). Rehabilitation imaging assessment clears patient for prosthetic fitting with microprocessor-assisted device recommended for gait safety.

puzzles.explanation: This complex presentation requires interdisciplinary management including pain modulation strategies, contracture prevention and treatment, psychological support for depression and anxiety, and collaborative prosthetic rehabilitation planning.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old collegiate soccer player reports acute anterior knee pain during game when planting foot during pivot maneuver. Severe pain with sensation of something tearing in anterior knee. Immediate swelling and loss of function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe anterior knee pain with any attempt at active knee extension. Unable to perform straight leg raise. Rapid swelling developed within 30 minutes of injury. Guarding of entire knee joint.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Young athletic male with high-demand sport (soccer). Acute traumatic mechanism during cutting maneuver. No prior knee surgery or injury. Recent growth spurt (18 years old) with some residual cartilage sensitivity. Full participation in sport up to injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Complete loss of active knee extension strength (0/5 against gravity). Gross anterior knee swelling with visible hematoma localised to tibial tubercle region. Severe point tenderness over tuberosity. Knee effusion present. Quadriceps muscle palpable but unable to generate contraction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray (AP, lateral, and Merchant views) shows avulsion of tibial tuberosity fragment with 1.5cm displacement. CT confirms fracture extends into articular surface of tibial plateau. MRI shows complete disruption of patellar tendon at bone insertion with severe edema. Associated bone marrow edema in proximal tibia. Soft tissue trauma with hematoma extensive.

puzzles.explanation: Postoperative tuberosity avulsion management focuses on restoring knee extension strength.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old man presents with deep buttock pain with walking for 3 months. Pain described as sharp and localized deep in buttock. Difficulty with prolonged ambulation (limited walking distance to 500 meters).

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Deep buttock pain worse with walking, particularly during hip adduction phase of gait. Pain relieved by sitting and lying down. Difficulty transitioning from sitting to standing. Pain not radiating down leg (no sciatica pattern). Groin pain sometimes accompanying buttock discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual insidious onset over 3 months. No significant trauma reported. Occupational sitting 8 hours daily. Hip tightness with limitation in internal rotation. History of groin strain 2 years prior to current symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pain with hip adduction across midline against resistance. Ischiofemoral stretch test reproduces deep buttock discomfort. Limited hip internal rotation (25 degrees versus 45 degrees opposite side). Tenderness with deep palpation in infrapiriformis region. Hamstring muscle tenderness present. Gait antalgic with reduced weight bearing phase.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI pelvis shows narrowing of ischiofemoral space measuring 18mm (normal >24mm) with adjacent quadratus femoris muscle edema. Hamstring origin tendinopathy visualized at ischial tuberosity. Gadolinium-enhanced imaging confirms inflammation in deep hip musculature. No femoroacetabular impingement or labral pathology identified.

puzzles.explanation: Ischiofemoral impingement is a rare but significant cause of deep posterior hip pain, often overlooked initially.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 7-year-old boy. Parents report he seems clumsy and accident-prone compared to classmates. Struggles with tasks other children his age do easily. No significant medical history, no recent trauma or illness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with handwriting and drawing — letters poorly formed, inconsistent spacing, takes longer than peers. Also struggles with self-care: buttoning shirts, tying shoelaces. PE class is a challenge: cannot skip or hop on one leg reliably, running appears awkward.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Difficulties noticed since early preschool and have persisted despite normal development otherwise. No significant delays in speech or cognitive ability. Full-term uncomplicated birth. No history of seizures, head injury, or neurological illness. Skills plateau rather than improve with practice.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tandem stance: loses balance in <10 seconds. Single-leg hop: poor control and coordination. Gait: slightly asymmetric arm swing, reduced dynamic stability. Nine-hole peg test: 2.5 minutes (normal ~1 minute for age). Pencil grip awkward and fatigues quickly. Standard neurological exam: normal strength, reflexes, sensation throughout. No focal weakness or spasticity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Movement Assessment Battery for Children: scores <5th percentile in both gross and fine motor domains. Brain MRI: normal, no structural lesions or white matter abnormality. Cognitive and language testing: within normal limits. Hearing normal. Pattern indicates generalized motor control deficit without neurological or structural explanation.

puzzles.explanation: DCD is a neurodevelopmental disorder of motor planning and coordination without neurological lesion, affecting academic and social activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old woman on long-term corticosteroid therapy for rheumatoid arthritis falls from standing height onto her left side. Acute severe left hip pain with inability to bear weight. Left leg held in external rotation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe pain with any hip movement. Cannot transfer bed-to-chair or ambulate. Left leg adopts external rotation and flexion posture. Marked swelling in proximal thigh with visible deformity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prolonged corticosteroid use (15 years) for autoimmune disease causing osteoporosis. Low-energy mechanism (standing fall) concerning for pathological fracture. History of previous right hip fracture 8 years ago. Female postmenopausal age 62. History of insufficient vitamin D.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip shortened 2cm compared to contralateral side. Leg externally rotated and flexed. Severe tenderness over greater trochanteric region and proximal femur. Hip abduction strength impossible to test due to pain. Groin pain with passive internal rotation. Neurovascular examination intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows transverse subtrochanteric fracture with lateral comminution. Fracture line extends from medial aspect at junction of femoral neck to lateral cortex. Medial cortex disruption with 30-degree valgus angulation. CT confirms comminuted fracture pattern with significant soft tissue trauma. DEXA T-score -3.5 confirming osteoporosis.

puzzles.explanation: Subtrochanteric fractures are challenging to fix due to high stress and poor bone quality in osteoporotic bone.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 60-year-old patient with a 10-year history of intermittent neck pain now reports progressive tingling and weakness in both arms over the past 6 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot work at a desk for more than 30 minutes without symptom escalation. Activities requiring overhead reaching and downward gaze (reading, cooking) trigger acute worsening. Symptoms do not improve with rest alone.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient reports morning stiffness lasting 45 minutes. No prior trauma or neck injury. No imaging performed previously. Symptoms gradually worsened despite two courses of physiotherapy (6 months apart). Intermittent episodes of grip weakness and difficulty with fine motor tasks (buttoning, writing).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Neck flexion limited to 30°. Bilateral hand intrinsic weakness (grip strength 3+/5 bilaterally). Brisk reflexes in upper extremities (3+). Positive Lhermitte sign (electric shock down spine with neck flexion). Reduced finger dexterity noted.

clues.label: Imaging
