clues.text: Onset correlates with a particularly demanding construction project involving repetitive lifting and twisting in awkward postures. Prior lumbar strain 2 years prior resolved with conservative care. No acute trauma or fall. Patient admits to minimal exercise outside of work.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness along thoracolumbar fascia region bilaterally (flanks at L4-5 level). Pain with trunk rotation against resistance. Reduced lumbar rotation in both directions (limited by pain). Palpable muscle guarding in quadratus lumborum region. Single leg stance provokes lateral back pain. Neurologically intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine X-rays normal excluding fracture or structural abnormality. MRI shows fascial thickening and edema in thoracolumbar fascia region. Quadratus lumborum muscle shows edema and signal changes consistent with myofascial injury. No nerve compression or disc pathology. Ultrasound confirms fascial thickening with echo changes.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracolumbar fascia pain is a complex regional pain from fascia and muscular structures.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old woman with 6-week history of gradual-onset joint pain and swelling affecting both hands. Symptoms worse in the morning, improving somewhat with activity. Reports fatigue and general malaise.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty gripping objects, dressing, and fine motor tasks. Pain and swelling predominantly in hands bilaterally. Functional impact worse after prolonged rest or overnight inactivity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No preceding injury or trauma. Family history significant for autoimmune thyroid disease and lupus. Recent mild weight loss. No improvement with rest or non-steroidal anti-inflammatories. Patient previously well with no medical comorbidities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Symmetric swelling and warmth of metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Grip strength bilaterally reduced (14 kg versus baseline 30 kg). Rheumatoid nodules palpable on elbows. Positive squeeze test of metacarpophalangeal joints reproducing pain. Reduced hand function with difficulty making fist.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Rheumatoid factor positive (titer 1:1280). Anti-CCP positive (256 IU/mL). ESR elevated at 52 mm/hr, CRP 18 mg/dL. Hand X-rays show early periarticular osteopenia with marginal erosions. Ultrasound demonstrates synovial hypertrophy with power Doppler activity in affected joints. Complete blood count shows mild anemia (Hb 10.8).

puzzles.explanation: Rheumatoid Arthritis shows more aggressive disease with extra-articular manifestations in an older male, including nodules and pulmonary involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 38-year-old woman with 8 months of pain during sexual activity. Postmenopausal for 1 year. Significant relationship impact and mood disturbance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain occurs only with penetration attempt, preventing intercourse. No pain with non-penetrative contact or daily activities. Pain severity has worsened progressively over 8 months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset 8 months ago without clear inciting event. Post-menopausal status 1 year prior contributing to tissue changes. No prior pelvic trauma or surgery. Stress and relationship factors contributing. History of anxiety and depression. Recent hormonal changes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pelvic floor examination shows hypertonic pelvic floor muscles with trigger points palpable. Tenderness on introital palpation. Pelvic floor muscle spasm evident during examination. Levator ani muscles tight bilaterally. Vulvar erythema present in introital region. Speculum examination normal excluding other causes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Transvaginal ultrasound shows increased echogenicity and thickening of pelvic floor muscles. No endometriosis, adenomyosis, or structural lesions. Findings consistent with myofascial hypertrophy and spasm.

puzzles.explanation: Dyspareunia without obvious structural cause likely involves pelvic floor dysfunction, myofascial restrictions, or central sensitization. Treatment requires pelvic floor physical therapy, trigger point release, education, and pain neuroscience approaches.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old woman presenting with persistent pain in multiple body regions (back, neck, shoulders, hips, knees). Onset 4 years ago following a mechanical low back strain. Pain described as constant with variable intensity throughout the day. Associated with poor sleep quality and daytime fatigue.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant functional limitation: difficulty prolonged sitting or standing, unable to return to prior work duties, reduced tolerance for household tasks. Symptoms do not clearly follow expected dermatomal or myotomal patterns. Minimal relief with rest or positional changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain began after low back strain but did not improve as expected over months. Has sought care from multiple providers (orthopedics, neurology, rheumatology). Reports severe sleep disruption with unrefreshing sleep pattern. Denies recent illness, fever, weight loss, or new joint swelling. No significant past medical history of autoimmune or neurological disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Widespread hyperalgesia to light touch across trunk and limbs. Tender points reproducible at multiple non-articular sites. Range of motion preserved in spine with inconsistent restriction to effort. Neurological examination normal (strength 5/5 throughout, sensation intact, reflexes normal). No evidence of joint swelling, inflammation, or systemic findings.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI spine, pelvis unremarkable—no disc herniation, canal stenosis, or structural abnormality to explain widespread pain distribution. Laboratory studies normal: inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP), metabolic panel, thyroid function, rheumatologic screen (ANA, RF) all negative. Quantitative sensory testing shows reduced pain thresholds and wind-up phenomenon consistent with altered pain processing.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic pain syndrome shows persistent pain beyond tissue healing with psychological factors and complex pain behaviors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old female athlete presents with insidious-onset pain in the forefoot. Pain worsens with weight-bearing activities over the past 2 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate impact activities and sports. Swelling visible over the forefoot. Antalgic gait with avoidance of push-off phase during walking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Athlete in repetitive jumping/running sport. No acute injury. Symptoms gradually worsening despite activity modification and rest. No prior treatment sought yet.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness directly over second metatarsal head (dorsal aspect). Swelling and prominence of metatarsal head region. Pain with passive dorsiflexion of second metatarsophalangeal joint. Limited metatarsophalangeal joint motion secondary to pain. Gait antalgic with reduced stance phase on affected foot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows flattening and fragmentation of second metatarsal head consistent with Freiberg's infraction (osteonecrosis). Dorsal hypertrophic changes present. MRI shows osteochondral lesion with bone marrow edema. T1 signal hypointensity within metatarsal head indicating necrosis. Associated cartilage irregularity.

puzzles.explanation: Freiberg's disease involves avascular necrosis of a metatarsal head, typically the second.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 22-year-old male. Sudden onset severe shoulder pain during contact sport. Arm feels unstable and 'out of place.' Unable to use arm. Significant anxiety about the injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Complete loss of shoulder function immediately after injury. Cannot lift arm or move it away from body. Pain severe with any attempted movement. Arm held motionless against torso.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during tackle—arm forcibly driven backward and outward. No prior shoulder problems, no instability history, no connective tissue disorders. First traumatic shoulder injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shoulder visibly squared-off with fullness anteriorly. Humeral head palpable anteriorly below clavicle. Arm locked in internal rotation and adduction. Unable to externally rotate or abduct. Axillary nerve sensation intact. Rotator cuff strength untestable due to guarding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray (AP, scapular Y, axillary views) shows humeral head displaced anteriorly relative to glenoid fossa. Post-reduction imaging reveals associated glenoid rim fracture and possible labral avulsion. MRI confirms rotator cuff intact but demonstrates labral pathology consistent with acute traumatic event.

puzzles.explanation: Shoulder dislocation presents as acute anterior glenohumeral dislocation from traumatic mechanism with structural damage evident on imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old overhead athlete reports intermittent shoulder pain with a sensation of the shoulder 'giving way' during throwing. Symptoms are vague but reproducible with certain movements in sport.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate cocking phase of throw without apprehension. Unable to perform weighted activities (e.g., push-ups, bench press) without feeling shoulder is unstable. Symptoms worse in positions of shoulder abduction and external rotation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Overhead athlete with progressive symptoms over past 2 seasons correlating with increased throwing volume. No discrete traumatic injury. Denies prior shoulder surgery or known hypermobility syndrome. Reports sense of fatigue in shoulder during extended practice.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive sulcus sign (inferior shoulder subluxation with traction). Positive apprehension and relocation tests. Laxity demonstrated with inferior translation in dependent position (drawer sign). Scapular dyskinesis evident during active motion. Reduced internal rotation relative to external rotation (reverse position). Strength 4+/5 indicating dynamic stabilizer weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows enlarged rotator cuff interval with increased capsular volume. Posterior labral fraying (SLAP pathology) associated lesion. Excessive glenohumeral translation documented on imaging. No discrete labral detachment (rules out Bankart). Ultrasound demonstrates increased capsular volume and laxity on dynamic maneuvers.

puzzles.explanation: Multidirectional instability with generalized ligamentous laxity in young patient.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Neonate presents with upper limb weakness and loss of function noted shortly after birth. Parents report decreased movement and apparent pain or discomfort with arm handling during the first few days of life.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Infant demonstrates severe limitation in shoulder movement. The arm is held in a characteristic resting posture with reduced abduction and external rotation. Upper limb protective positioning is evident; infant resists passive shoulder movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Delivery was complicated by shoulder dystocia requiring manual traction to deliver the infant. Neonatal exam performed within 24 hours of birth noted the motor deficit. No prior upper limb trauma or congenital anomalies.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination: biceps and deltoid strength grossly weak (0-1/5). Moro reflex is absent on affected side; lower limb Moro intact. Passive shoulder abduction and external rotation severely limited. No claw hand or wrist drop observed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brachial plexus reveals avulsion of the C5-C6 nerve roots from the spinal cord. The upper trunk demonstrates signal abnormality and discontinuity. Nerve root avulsion is confirmed with imaging findings consistent with preganglionic injury.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic Erb's palsy with incomplete recovery requires assessment for nerve regeneration potential and consideration of surgical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 30-year-old athlete reports recurrent episodes of ankle giving way, particularly during cutting or pivoting movements. The instability started after a previous ankle injury several months ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk confidently on uneven terrain without fear of ankle giving way. Struggles with quick directional changes and single-leg activities. Normal weight-bearing walking is mostly stable, but sudden movements trigger instability.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic ligamentous laxity follows previous ankle sprains and incomplete rehabilitation. Proprioceptive deficits persist despite symptom resolution. Multiple recurrent inversion episodes have established chronic instability.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive anterior drawer test indicates significant tibiotalar translation. Inversion stress testing demonstrates hypermobility. Proprioceptive testing reveals impaired balance. Ankle proprioception is diminished.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates chronic lateral ligament attenuation with scar tissue. Anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL) shows partial thickening and signal changes consistent with healed but weakened ligament. No osteochondral lesion or syndesmotic involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic ankle instability results from incomplete healing and proprioceptive deficits after previous sprains.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young child presents with developmental delay in motor milestones and abnormal movement patterns. Muscle tone abnormalities and stiffness restrict normal motor function. The condition is nonprogressive but becomes more apparent with age and development.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Child walks on toes with knees bent and legs crossing in front of each other. Hip adduction is pronounced bilaterally. Unable to achieve normal heel-strike during ambulation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Birth complicated by prolonged labour and low Apgar scores. Motor difficulties were apparent within first weeks of life. Parents note the pattern of movement dysfunction has not worsened or improved significantly over years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked spasticity in bilateral lower extremities with hyperreflexia (3+/5) and sustained ankle clonus. Hip flexors and adductors tight bilaterally. Scissor gait evident. Upper extremities demonstrate normal tone and strength. No weakness in antigravity muscles; tone abnormality is primary finding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brain demonstrates periventricular leukomalacia with signal changes in white matter around the ventricles. Ventricular dilation may be present secondary to white matter loss. The imaging findings correlate with the pattern of spastic diplegia observed clinically.

puzzles.explanation: Cerebral palsy is a non-progressive motor disorder from perinatal brain injury, typically presenting with spasticity and developmental delays.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old reports persistent low mood, fatigue, and difficulty concentrating over past 8 months. Describes feeling unmotivated and withdrawn from usual activities. No recent major life stressors or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot sustain focus at work or engage socially. Avoids previously enjoyed hobbies. Noticed mood temporarily lifts during physical activity—symptoms return within hours of stopping. No improvement with rest alone.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset 8 months ago. Previously active and engaged. No psychiatric history. Attempted one antidepressant (sertraline) for 6 weeks with minimal effect. Started attending gym 4× weekly 10 weeks ago; reported sustained mood improvement correlating with exercise frequency.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting heart rate decreased from 82 to 68 bpm over 10 weeks. VO₂ max improved 15% on repeat cardiopulmonary testing. PHQ-9 score reduced from 18 to 7. No neurological deficits. Cardiovascular examination normal. Sleep log shows consistent 7–8 hours nightly.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: TSH, B12, and metabolic panel normal. No anaemia or thyroid dysfunction. Brain imaging not indicated. Psychiatric evaluation excluded bipolar disorder and major depressive disorder requiring pharmacotherapy. Mood trajectory correlates precisely with exercise adherence and fitness gains; no other interventions changed.

puzzles.explanation: Exercise-induced mood improvement shows positive psychological response to regular physical activity in depressed patient.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged woman with diabetes presenting with shoulder pain. Onset gradual over recent weeks. Pain present at rest and with activity, disrupting sleep and daily routines.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to reach overhead or behind back. All shoulder movements restricted. Patient reports difficulty with dressing, grooming, and sleeping on affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Risk factors include middle age, female gender, and poorly controlled diabetes mellitus. The condition often follows minor shoulder trauma or immobilization. Onset is typically gradual with progressive loss of motion over weeks to months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive external rotation severely limited (near 0 degrees). Scapulohumeral rhythm abnormal with early scapular elevation. Discomfort at end-range in capsular pattern. No weakness detected.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the shoulder are typically normal without bony abnormality. MR arthrography demonstrates thickening and enhancement of the joint capsule. The rotator cuff interval is tightened with loss of the normal axillary pouch distensibility.

puzzles.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis presents with insidious onset of global shoulder stiffness in a capsular pattern, strong association with diabetes and thyroid disease.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of urinary urgency and frequency that significantly impacts her quality of life. She experiences a persistent urge to urinate 12-14 times daily and 3-4 times nightly, even when the bladder contains minimal urine.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient has modified daily activities to stay near bathroom facilities. Social activities and travel are significantly limited. Sleep is disrupted by nocturia with 3-4 nighttime voids.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began insidiously without identified trigger or prior urinary tract infection. No history of pelvic surgery, trauma, or radiation. Denies use of diuretics or caffeine excess. No diabetes or neurological conditions reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Vital signs normal; abdominal examination reveals no masses or tenderness. Pelvic floor muscle assessment shows normal strength but evidence of increased tone and difficulty with relaxation. Neurological examination of lower extremities and perineal sensation are intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Bladder ultrasound performed post-void demonstrates normal post-void residual volume of 25 mL. Renal ultrasound is unremarkable with no hydronephrosis. Urinalysis is completely normal with negative culture. Cystoscopy reveals no structural abnormalities, malignancy, or interstitial cystitis features.

puzzles.explanation: Overactive Bladder shows neurogenic OAB secondary to CNS disease (stroke, neurological decline) with impaired detrusor function and elevated post-void residual.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young athlete reports anterior knee pain that worsens with jumping and cutting activities. Pain is activity-dependent, onset gradual over weeks to months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain during landing from jumps and deceleration activities. Single-leg stance and stairs provoke symptoms. Pain often resolves during activity but returns severely after sport, with stiffness the next morning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset follows increased training volume or intensity, particularly jumping-based sports. Weakness in hip or core muscles may contribute to abnormal loading patterns. Previous patellar or knee pathology may predispose to tendinopathy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is localized to the patellar tendon insertion at the tibial tubercle. Single leg squats demonstrate poor motor control and knee valgus. Pain occurs with resisted knee extension testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging reveals thickened patellar tendon with mixed echogenicity and increased vascularity on Doppler. MRI demonstrates signal changes within the tendon substance. Imaging findings correlate with symptom duration and severity.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic patellar tendinopathy results from cumulative microtrauma and incomplete healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old male presents with acute-onset weakness and numbness in the shoulder region following a severe fall with direct shoulder impact.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot initiate any movements requiring shoulder abduction. Passive range of motion is preserved. Weakness is the primary functional limitation rather than pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct trauma from fall onto shoulder from standing height. No prior neurological symptoms, shoulder pathology, or recurrent dislocations. First neurological symptoms noted immediately post-injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, deltoid muscle is atrophic and significantly weakened with shoulder abduction strength 2/5. Sensation is diminished over the lateral shoulder in the regimental badge distribution. Axillary nerve traction test is positive. Rotator cuff strength is intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder X-rays rule out fracture or dislocation. MRI shows no rotator cuff tear but reveals signal changes in the axillary nerve course with surrounding soft tissue edema. Electromyography demonstrates denervation potentials in deltoid and axillary nerve conduction slowing.

puzzles.explanation: Post-dislocation axillary nerve injury with deltoid paralysis and sensory loss in axillary nerve distribution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 62-year-old with severe shoulder pain and marked loss of arm mobility. Symptoms acute in onset 6 weeks ago. Pain constant, worse with any attempted movement. No significant relief with rest or NSAIDs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot lift arm away from body or reach overhead. Dependent on others for dressing, grooming, and personal care. Unable to sleep on affected side. Pain reproduced with both active and passive motion attempts.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute symptom onset 6 weeks ago coincided with a major orthopedic intervention for chronic joint disease. Pre-intervention ROM was already severely restricted. Recovery trajectory slower than expected. Patient reports feeling apprehensive with passive stretching attempts.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive forward flexion 60° with pain and guarding. Passive external rotation severely limited to 20°. End-range passive motion elicits apprehension. Shoulder musculature visibly atrophic. No active rotator cuff strength testing performed due to pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs demonstrate secure metallic component fixation with no evidence of loosening, migration, or subsidence. No heterotopic ossification. Soft tissue swelling consistent with expected post-operative inflammation. Articular surfaces show appropriate positioning.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Total Shoulder Replacement shows mid-term recovery (4 months) with good pain control but persistent stiffness and weakness requiring intensive strengthening.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A woman in her 60s reports sensation of heaviness and pressure in the pelvis that worsens as the day progresses. She also notices increased urinary frequency and occasional urgency.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports she must avoid prolonged standing and cannot perform heavy lifting without significant symptom exacerbation. Symptoms improve within minutes of lying flat.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Parity and vaginal deliveries are significant risk factors for pelvic floor dysfunction. Chronic straining from constipation or chronic cough may contribute. Advancing age and menopause worsen pelvic floor laxity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On pelvic examination, anterior vaginal wall bulging is evident with Valsalva maneuver. The bladder prolapse may be visualized or palpated during straining. Grade of prolapse is classified from 1-4 based on extent of descent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dynamic pelvic MRI shows anterior compartment prolapse with bladder descent of >2 cm below pubic symphysis during straining, consistent with grade 2-3 cystocele. No posterior compartment involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Cystocele management requires pelvic floor strengthening, pessary fitting for conservative management, weight reduction, smoking cessation, and consideration of surgical repair (anterior colporrhaphy) if conservative measures fail and functional limitation significant.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 64-year-old reports acute onset hip pain with severe functional limitation. Pain is constant and worsens with movement. Patient describes inability to perform basic self-care and mobility tasks. Onset was sudden, approximately 1 week ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain significantly worse with hip flexion beyond 60 degrees and adduction across midline. Patient unable to weight bear independently and requires walker. Dressing, toileting, and bed transfers severely restricted. Symptoms not relieved by rest or positioning adjustments.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began acutely 5-7 days ago without trauma. Patient reports preceding chronic hip pain and stiffness that had progressively worsened over months. No recent falls, direct injury, or acute inflammatory symptoms. Prior conservative management attempted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion limited to 45 degrees, abduction to 15 degrees, internal rotation severely restricted. Marked muscle guarding around hip. Trendelenburg sign positive. Straight leg raise weak and painful. Surgical scar present on anterior hip with appropriate healing, minimal drainage or erythema. No calf swelling, warmth, or signs of DVT.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs show metallic femoral component and acetabular cup in anatomically appropriate position. No lucencies, loosening, or dislocation evident. CT may show component alignment within normal parameters. Clinical presentation and imaging timeline consistent with expected early post-operative phase and protective muscle guarding response.

puzzles.explanation: THA rehabilitation requires strict hip precautions in the acute phase, progressive hip abductor strengthening to prevent Trendelenburg gait, hip flexor stretching, and functional mobility training to optimize long-term prosthetic function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-week postpartum woman reports visible abdominal bulging at the midline and generalized abdominal weakness. Concerned about functional recovery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform sit-ups or roll bed-side without support. Difficulty carrying infant. Symptoms worsen with straining or Valsalva maneuver but improve with abdominal bracing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Uncomplicated vaginal delivery 6 weeks prior. Weight gained 16 kg during pregnancy. Return to exercise initiated without postpartum rehabilitation guidance. No diastasis history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable gap of 3 fingers width between rectus muscles at midline above umbilicus, reducing to 2 fingers below umbilicus. Rectus muscles separate further with abdominal contraction. Intact fascia, no hernia.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound confirms diastasis rectus abdominis with interrectal distance 3.5 cm. Linea alba thickness normal. No ventral hernia present. Dynamic ultrasound shows muscle separation increase with contraction.

puzzles.explanation: Diastasis Rectus Abdominis shows postpartum separation in early recovery period following single uncomplicated vaginal delivery with visible bulging and palpable gap.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A weightlifter reports sudden sharp pain in the anterior chest during a heavy bench press. Immediate weakness and inability to continue lifting. Notices visible change in chest appearance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot initiate or perform any pushing movements. Unable to perform horizontal adduction against gravity. Pain increases with any attempt to adduct the arm across the body.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Rupture typically occurs during heavy weightlifting with eccentric loading during bench press. The distal tendon attachment at the humerus is the most common rupture site. Steroids or chronic tendon pathology may predispose to rupture.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Strength testing confirms profound weakness in horizontal adduction and internal rotation. Resisted adduction produces no contraction. Palpation along the anterior chest reveals a palpable gap or indentation where muscle continuity is lost. Asymmetry with the contralateral side is striking.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the chest reveals discontinuity of the pectoralis major tendon near its humeral insertion. Muscle retraction and edema are evident at the rupture site. Imaging confirms the site and extent of rupture for surgical planning.

puzzles.explanation: Pectoralis major rupture from maximal weight lifting effort with acute onset.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports knee pain with intermittent mechanical symptoms. Pain is localized around the knee joint. Swelling develops gradually, particularly worse after prolonged weight-bearing activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to perform rotational movements of the knee under load. Squatting and deep flexion with rotation are significantly limited. Occasional episodes of knee 'giving way' or sudden inability to extend fully.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tear may follow a specific injury with twisting mechanism or develop insidiously in degenerative knees. Previous meniscal pathology or knee injuries increase risk. Degenerative tears are common in middle-aged and older patients.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Joint line palpation reproduces localized tenderness. Knee effusion present. Range of motion limited in flexion, particularly with rotation. Special tests for meniscal irritation are positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the knee demonstrates meniscal tear in the medial or lateral meniscus with classification of tear pattern. T2 signal intensity within the meniscal substance indicates tear. Imaging localizes the tear and guides treatment decisions.

puzzles.explanation: A medial meniscal bucket-handle tear presents with mechanical locking, joint line tenderness, delayed effusion (distinguishing from ACL), and positive McMurray's test.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old man reports progressive difficulty perceiving sound over past 5 years. Describes sounds as muffled and unclear. Wife reports he frequently asks for repetition and turns up television volume significantly. No ear pain, drainage, or recent trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Struggling to follow conversations in noisy environments (restaurants, group settings). Difficulty with phone calls. Avoids social situations due to communication frustration. Finds higher-pitched sounds particularly hard to distinguish. Performance worsens with background noise.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset without specific injury or infection. 45-year occupational exposure to loud machinery (machinist) without consistent hearing protection. No ototoxic medications, ear surgery, or recent infection. Father had similar age-related hearing difficulties. 40 pack-year smoking history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weber test lateralizes to right (better hearing side). Rinne test: air conduction audible longer than bone conduction bilaterally, but gap present. Otoscopy shows cerumen impaction; removed bilaterally. Post-removal, sound perception unchanged. Otoscopic findings otherwise normal. Speech discrimination reduced at 68%.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Audiometry: bilateral sensorineural loss predominantly above 2000 Hz; air-bone gaps 15-25 dB across all frequencies. Bone conduction thresholds elevated at 4000-8000 Hz (classic pattern). Tympanometry normal (Type A). Acoustic reflexes intact bilaterally. Results consistent with noise exposure and age-related contribution.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related hearing loss (presbycusis) involves progressive sensorineural hearing loss, particularly in high frequencies, affecting communication.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete reports insidious onset of pain in the lower abdomen and groin region over several weeks. Pain is activity-related and worsens with sport participation. No acute injury or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is provoked by hip adduction and flexion under load. Crossing legs, rapid directional changes, and forceful kicking reproduce symptoms. Symptoms settle with activity modification but recur with return to sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Condition occurs in athletes with repetitive hip adduction and flexion activities. Asymmetric hip strength and flexibility contribute to excessive shear at the symphysis. Previous hip or groin injuries increase risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness is elicited over the pubic symphysis, worse with direct palpation. Pain is reproduced with resisted hip adduction in supine, and with combined flexion-adduction against resistance. Pain is NOT reproduced with hip abduction or external rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate symphyseal joint changes including widening, sclerosis, and bone resorption. Bone scintigraphy shows increased uptake at the pubic symphysis. MRI reveals edema and inflammatory changes at the symphyseal joint.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic osteitis pubis often involves pelvic instability and may be associated with lumbar or hip dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 76-year-old woman presents with progressive weakness and functional decline over 2 years. Difficulty rising from chair without armrest support. Walking slower and shorter distances than previously. Recent fall from trip on uneven pavement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Reduced grip strength (12 kg versus 22 kg in contralateral hand). Difficulty with stairs and walking long distances. Loss of independence with heavy household tasks. Slow gait velocity (0.7 m/sec versus normal 1.4 m/sec). Difficulty getting out of bed and toilet independently.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 76 with sedentary lifestyle and reduced physical activity. Inadequate protein intake (40g daily versus recommended 1.0-1.2 g/kg body weight). Loss of appetite with reduced food intake. Multiple comorbidities including osteoarthritis and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease limiting activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible muscle wasting in upper extremities and thighs. Reduced grip strength 12 kg (abnormal cutoff <16 kg for women). Slow gait speed 0.7 m/sec (abnormal <1.0 m/sec). Reduced lower extremity strength particularly hip flexors and knee extensors (4/5). Difficulty rising from chair in <12 seconds (requires 18 seconds).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA scan shows normal bone mineral density ruling out primary osteoporosis. Body composition analysis demonstrates fat mass normal but lean muscle mass significantly reduced at 35% total body weight (normal >40% in women age 75). No focal vertebral fractures or lytic lesions present.

puzzles.explanation: Sarcopenic obesity combines muscle loss with excess adiposity, masking underlying sarcopenia and significantly impairing function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 62-year-old former construction worker. Progressive breathlessness on exertion over 18 months. Persistent dry cough. Reports reduced energy and feels generally deconditioned.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk more than 100 metres without needing to stop and catch breath. Climbing stairs leaves patient significantly winded. Dyspnea out of proportion to activity level. Fatigue limits all sustained activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual insidious onset without acute illness trigger. 40-year history of occupational dust exposure (construction/masonry). Never smoker. No recent infections. Progressive decline over past 18 months despite rest and conservative management.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Fine crackles heard at lung bases bilaterally on auscultation, predominantly end-inspiratory. Clubbing of fingernails present. Reduced chest expansion symmetrically. Mild cyanosis noted. Oxygen saturation drops with mild exertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest: bilateral reticular patterns with traction bronchiectasis in basilar distribution, consistent with usual interstitial pneumonia pattern. Pulmonary function tests: restrictive pattern with FVC 55% predicted, DLCO significantly reduced at 35% predicted.

puzzles.explanation: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis with classic presentation of progressive dyspnea, dry cough, clubbing, and UIP pattern on HRCT. Reduced DLCO is characteristic.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old female cyclist with chronic anterior knee pain reports increasing difficulty with cycling distances. Pain worsens with climbing hills and longer rides. Progressive functional decline.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with squatting and descending stairs. Pain on deep knee bend. Unable to perform single-leg activities. Activity limitations more pronounced during eccentric loading tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cycling hobby with recent increase in distance and intensity. No acute injury. Gradual progressive onset. Multiple attempted conservative interventions with limited benefit.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive J-sign patellar tracking. VMO strength weak. Patellar irritation with movement. Pain with loaded knee extension. Tight hip muscles evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows normal articular cartilage and no structural pathology. No meniscal involvement. Mild synovial effusion present. Femoral anteversion noted on imaging. No evidence of ligamentous injury or osteochondral lesion.

puzzles.explanation: Runner's knee shows chronic patellofemoral pain with significant biomechanical dysfunction and functional limitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old reports acute sharp chest wall pain that worsens with deep breathing. Onset was sudden, 2 days ago. Pain is focal and constant but exacerbated by movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to lie on affected side. Reaching across body and bending forward are particularly limited. Difficulty with overhead activities and sustained postures.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Strain typically follows direct trauma, coughing, or sudden twisting movement. Heavy lifting or forceful physical exertion may precipitate the injury. Previous chest wall strain increases recurrence risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, tenderness is elicited along the intercostal muscles. Reproducing pain with intercostal space compression confirms muscular origin. Restricted breathing pattern is evident with protective guarding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows localized edema and small hematoma within the 5th–6th intercostal muscles. Rib imaging is normal; no fracture or pneumothorax.

puzzles.explanation: Intercostal muscle strain is acute mechanical injury from repetitive or forceful activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Adult presents with intermittent neck pain and episodes of stiffness. Symptoms worse with certain head movements. History of connective tissue laxity with generalized joint hypermobility. No recent trauma reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot turn head fully to either side without discomfort. Difficulty looking down when flexing neck. Avoids rapid or large cervical movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known connective tissue laxity throughout the body with hypermobile joints in multiple limbs. Two episodes of transient arm weakness and paresthesias over past 6 months. Never had rheumatoid arthritis screening. No recent trauma or falls.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sharp-Purser test reproduces transient tingling in fingers. Bilateral upper limb hyperreflexia (3+) and positive Babinski bilaterally. Lower limb strength intact 5/5. Sensation intact. Generalized hypermobility evident on Beighton score.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the cervical spine demonstrates increased atlantodental interval measuring greater than 5mm. Cervical myelopathy is evident with T2 signal changes in the spinal cord at C1-C2 level. The odontoid process is displaced posteriorly with significant cord compression. Imaging confirms preganglionic compression of the cervical myelon.

puzzles.explanation: Atlantoaxial instability is a serious condition with risk of serious neurological or vascular compromise requiring specialist care.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports a palpable lump on the wrist or hand that may be painless. The cyst may enlarge and shrink over time. Some patients report aching pain or functional limitation depending on cyst location and size.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty with sustained gripping or repetitive wrist extension. Pain worsens when bearing weight through the hand (e.g., leaning on palm).

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient recalls gradual onset without clear injury, though minor wrist trauma occurred 2-3 months prior. No constitutional symptoms. No prior treatment attempted. Similar lesions have not appeared elsewhere.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpation reveals a smooth, compressible mass that transilluminates brightly, confirming fluid-filled nature. Mass is fixed to underlying joint capsule. Wrist extension ROM is restricted compared to unaffected side. Scaphoid tenderness is absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates a unilocular, anechoic lesion arising directly from the scapholunate joint capsule, measuring 1.2 cm with clear cystic boundaries and no internal septations.

puzzles.explanation: Classic ganglion cyst with painless presentation and typical dorsal location.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old female rugby player sustains direct blow to left thigh during match. Acute severe pain and rapid swelling over lateral thigh. Unable to continue playing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to weight-bear on left leg. Rapid onset of extensive bruising visible within minutes. Unable to perform kicking or change of direction movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tackle occurred 2 hours ago. No previous thigh injuries or bleeding disorders. Patient takes no anticoagulants. Swelling has continued to progress since impact.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant swelling and bruising visible over lateral thigh. Hip abduction painful. Knee ROM limited by thigh pain. Strength testing limited by guarding. Visible muscle deformation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows muscle hematoma with fiber disruption. Vastus lateralis and biceps femoris involvement noted. Hematoma measures 6x8 cm. No fracture or compartment syndrome signs.

puzzles.explanation: Muscle contusion shows severe response with risk of myositis ossificans and extensive hematoma formation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old with acute onset severe hip pain and significant walking difficulty. Pain is constant, worsened by movement, partially relieved by rest. Patient reports feeling unstable and requiring walking aids. No trauma or fall reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot bend hip fully or cross legs without sharp pain and protective muscle tightening. Low chairs and car transfers are impossible. Side-lying preferred over back-lying. Pain and stiffness worse with adduction movements across the body midline.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset 2 weeks ago. Patient reports prior chronic hip pain, morning stiffness, and progressive difficulty with walking over previous months. Recent period of reduced activity. No fall, trauma, or infection signs. Started supervised physiotherapy within 48 hours of symptom onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion and adduction severely restricted and guarded. Hip abduction and external rotation relatively preserved. Anterior hip and proximal thigh tender to palpation. Positive Trendelenburg sign. Normal temperature. No warmth, redness, or effusion. No hip instability on gentle passive motion within restricted ranges.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show femoral and acetabular metallic components in appropriate neutral alignment. Acetabular component anteversion 15–20 degrees. Femoral component well-fixed without subsidence. Symmetric soft tissue contours. No radiolucencies, osteolytic changes, or periprosthetic lucency. No evidence of malposition or loosening.

puzzles.explanation: Suboptimal THA outcomes with high acetabular version require modified precautions, hip flexor stretching emphasis, careful activity modification, and consideration of potential surgical revision if symptoms persist despite comprehensive rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 62-year-old woman with shoulder pain and stiffness. Onset 8 weeks ago. Pain worse with movement, especially overhead activities. Some days better than others. Taking regular analgesia.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot lift arm above shoulder height independently. Difficulty reaching behind back or across body. Cannot carry shopping bags or perform sustained gripping tasks. Pain limiting most upper limb activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Shoulder gradually became painful and weak over several months before recent intervention. No clear traumatic event recalled. No prior shoulder problems or surgery. Not diabetic. Motivated and compliant with rehabilitation program.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive forward flexion 110°, abduction 95°. Active forward flexion 45°, abduction 20°. Weakness on isometric supraspinatus testing with guarding. Scapular dyskinesis visible. Palpable scar tender. Neurovascular intact. Pain limits strength assessment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows repaired supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons in anatomical position with no gap, retear, or fluid collection. Subacromial bursa unremarkable. Repair integrity maintained. No acute inflammation or haematoma.

puzzles.explanation: Repairs of massive or fatty infiltrated rotator cuff tears often have prolonged recovery with residual weakness. This requires extended strengthening progression, scapular stabilization emphasis, and realistic functional goal setting.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old athlete presents with severe leg pain following a crush injury to the lower leg during contact sport. Pain severity seems excessive relative to the visible soft tissue damage.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain dramatically worsens with passive stretching of the ankle and foot. Active ankle movements are increasingly difficult. Pain continues to escalate despite standard pain relief measures.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 4-6 hours ago from direct blunt crush during sport. Visible swelling began immediately and has worsened steadily. No fracture was identified on initial assessment. Patient reports the pain trajectory is unusually rapid for a simple contusion.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Anterior leg is visibly swollen and firm/tense. Passive ankle plantarflexion elicits severe pain. Foot sensation is diminished in a stocking-like pattern. Pulses are palpable bilaterally, but the skin appears pale. No obvious fracture deformity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Compartment pressure measurement via needle manometer shows anterior compartment pressure of 65 mmHg at rest, exceeding the critical threshold of 60 mmHg. CT imaging of the leg demonstrates swollen muscles with no fracture. MRI shows edema within the anterior compartment muscles.

puzzles.explanation: Compartment syndrome presents as surgical emergency with severe pain, swelling, and positive passive stretch test requiring immediate intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports knee pain that comes and goes unpredictably. Sometimes the knee feels like it's catching or getting stuck, requiring a shift in position to feel normal again. Swelling develops, particularly after activities like walking or standing for extended periods.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform rapid directional changes or pivoting movements without fear of the knee giving way. Deep squatting and kneeling are intolerable. Stair descent is cautious and controlled.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tear may follow a specific injury with twisting mechanism or develop insidiously in degenerative knees. Previous meniscal pathology or knee injuries increase risk. Degenerative tears are common in middle-aged and older patients.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive McMurray test with reproduction of clicking at joint line. Thessaly test positive at 20 degrees knee flexion. Significant joint effusion with positive ballottement sign. Medial or lateral joint line tenderness present depending on meniscal location.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates a vertical or complex tear within the medial meniscus extending to the articular surface, with elevated T2 signal indicating fluid within the tear. Tear configuration and location guide meniscectomy vs. repair planning.

puzzles.explanation: Meniscal tears produce mechanical symptoms such as catching and locking due to displaced fragments.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old right-handed graphic designer. Gradual onset of dorsal wrist pain over 8 weeks. Pain is sharp, localized to one side of the wrist, and triggered by specific hand movements. No swelling, no numbness or tingling in fingers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with sustained gripping and repetitive wrist extension (reaching, lifting objects, prolonged mouse use). Symptoms improve with rest. No pain at night or with passive wrist movements. Pain-free range of motion exists, but resisted movements in one direction reproduce symptoms significantly.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began after increasing workload and poor desk ergonomics 2 months ago. No history of wrist trauma, rheumatoid arthritis, or systemic inflammatory disease. Previous similar episode 3 years ago that resolved with activity modification. No morning stiffness. Symptoms do not affect the opposite wrist.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness along the dorsal wrist over the bony prominence of the radius. Pain reproduced with resisted wrist extension against resistance. No joint swelling, warmth, or effusion. Grip strength reduced on affected side due to pain avoidance. Sensation and nerve palpation normal. Pain-free passive wrist flexion and extension present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows thickened extensor carpi radialis brevis tendon (3.2 mm, normal <2 mm) with hypoechoic areas and increased Doppler flow signal. No tendon tear or free fluid. MRI confirms T2 signal hyperintensity within the tendon substance without discontinuity. Findings consistent with reactive tendinopathy.

puzzles.explanation: ECU tendinopathy from repetitive wrist extension and deviation activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports progressive weakness and pain in the forearm during gripping tasks. Symptoms began insidiously over weeks. Age 45, plays recreational tennis twice weekly.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Activities involving sustained grip or repetitive pronation/supination significantly worsen symptoms. Patient reports pain when opening jars or using a screwdriver, worse by end of day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms followed a period of intensive racquet sports training 3 months ago. No prior trauma or surgery. Patient reports similar symptoms did not improve with rest alone over 6 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness localized to a point 3–4 cm distal to the lateral epicondyle along the dorsal forearm. Resisted supination with elbow extended reproduces pain more reliably than resisted pronation. Finger extensor strength 4/5 on manual testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution ultrasound shows focal enlargement of the radial nerve at the proximal third of the forearm, consistent with compression. Nerve conduction studies demonstrate slowing of motor conduction across the proximal forearm with preserved distal segments.

puzzles.explanation: Radial tunnel syndrome with motor predominance and preserved sensation, distinguished from lateral epicondylitis by exam findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports sharp shoulder pain with onset following a specific shoulder injury. Pain is worse with overhead reaching and when crossing the arm across the body.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform pressing movements (e.g., push-ups, bench press) and struggles with activities requiring arm adduction across midline. Overhead reaching is severely limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred from direct shoulder impact or fall onto outstretched arm approximately 1 week ago. Patient reports visible deformity or prominence at the top of the shoulder. No significant prior shoulder history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited directly over the AC joint. Crossover adduction test reproduces pain at the AC joint. The O'Brien test may also elicit AC joint pain with positive result.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate clavicular displacement and AC joint space abnormalities based on sprain grade. Weighted views show degree of clavicular elevation if AC ligament is disrupted. MRI reveals ligament injury and associated findings.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic AC joint arthropathy and instability from old high-grade separation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old woman with a non-healing wound on the lower leg for several months. History of leg swelling. Denies recent trauma to the area.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Swelling worsens with prolonged standing. Patient reports heaviness and aching in the leg by end of day. Compression clothing provides temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Swelling began gradually over 10 years. Prior leg ulcer healed 2 years ago. Current wound opened in the same medial lower leg location 3 months ago despite initial healing. No significant trauma or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Medial leg ulceration with surrounding hemosiderin staining and lipodermatosclerosis. Edema pitting on pressure. Calf circumference increased bilaterally. Venous stasis signs present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Venous duplex ultrasound demonstrates valvular incompetence in superficial and deep veins. Reflux pattern documented. Ankle-brachial index normal excluding arterial disease.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced venous ulcer disease with bilateral involvement and significant complications. Chronic venous insufficiency with poor healing despite treatment, complicated by superinfection and extensive tissue damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Infant presents with persistent head tilt to one side. Parents report the child prefers to keep head in this position and seems resistant to turning the other way. Age of presentation varies from birth to early infancy.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Child demonstrates difficulty tracking objects or following caregivers' faces when turning head toward the restricted side. Passive attempts to correct the head position meet resistance, and the child returns to the preferred tilt.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Birth history unremarkable or notable for difficult/instrumented delivery or breech presentation. Condition present from birth or noticed within first 2–3 months of life. No recent trauma. Family history not contributory.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, a tight mass or rope-like sternocleidomastoid muscle is palpable on the affected side. Head tilt toward the affected side with chin rotation away is evident. Facial asymmetry may be present with flattening on the affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the sternocleidomastoid muscle demonstrates focal enlargement and echogenic appearance consistent with fibrosis. MRI may show muscle pathology and structural changes. Imaging rules out spinal or skeletal abnormalities.

puzzles.explanation: Progressive muscular torticollis from birth trauma can develop secondary facial and cranial deformities without early intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports acute onset severe elbow pain and rapid swelling following a direct blow or fall. The arm is held protectively in a flexed position and any attempt to straighten it causes intense pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform any functional activities requiring the arm — cannot push off a chair, cannot use the arm for self-care, cannot support weight through the affected arm.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fracture typically follows direct blow to the olecranon or fall on flexed elbow. High energy trauma results in displaced fractures. Low energy injuries may result in undisplaced fractures.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, significant swelling and ecchymosis are present over the olecranon. A palpable step-off may be felt at the fracture site. Crepitus may be elicited with movement of fracture fragments.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate olecranon fracture with classification of displacement and comminution. Fracture line is visible with relationship to the joint surface assessed. Imaging guides treatment decisions regarding operative intervention.

puzzles.explanation: Olecranon fracture with loss of triceps function from direct trauma.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old otherwise healthy adult presents with sudden onset severe vertigo upon waking this morning. No hearing loss reported. Recent upper respiratory viral illness 5 days prior to symptom onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vertigo is constant and disabling, with dizziness exacerbated by head movements and eye closure. Patient experiences significant nausea with vomiting. Symptoms slightly improve with bed rest and head immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No trauma, otalgia, or recent air travel. Patient reports mild imbalance persisting even at rest, but no positional component and no fluctuating symptoms suggestive of Menière disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Head impulse test shows positive catch-up saccade indicating unilateral vestibular deficit. Dix-Hallpike maneuver negative excluding BPPV. Caloric testing shows reduced response in affected ear (50% unilateral paresis). Romberg test positive with falls toward affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain and internal auditory canal normal with no T2 signal abnormality or enhancement of vestibulocochlear nerve. Excludes demyelinating disease and structural lesions. Findings consistent with peripheral vestibular origin.

puzzles.explanation: Vestibular Neuritis is acute post-viral vestibular dysfunction with sudden severe vertigo, no hearing loss, and unilateral vestibular deficits in acute phase.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old presents with significant back pain and stiffness. Reports acute worsening 2–3 weeks ago. Movement is severely limited; cannot bend forward or rotate trunk. No trauma reported. Pain is constant, worse with any spinal movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate sitting longer than 10–15 minutes. Bed mobility requires assistance; rolling over causes sharp pain. Walking limited to short distances with support. Bending, twisting, and lifting are impossible. Dressing and personal hygiene tasks require adaptive strategies or help.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Major spinal procedure performed 2–4 weeks ago for longstanding degenerative disease and nerve compression. Pre-operative symptoms included radiating leg pain and claudication with walking. Post-operative restrictions include strict bracing and no spinal flexion/extension. This is first spinal intervention. Incision healing without signs of infection.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Surgical scar clean, dry, well-healed without dehiscence. Spinal range of motion severely restricted (<10° flexion/extension, minimal rotation). Muscle strength in lower extremities reduced (4/5 in key myotomes). Sensory changes persist in dermatomal pattern. Reflexes intact or slightly diminished. No signs of active infection or wound complications.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-rays demonstrate correct hardware positioning, intact instrumentation, no screw loosening or migration. Early bridging callus visible at fusion levels. MRI shows adequate decompression of neural structures, no epidural hematoma, no residual canal stenosis. Vertebral body alignment maintained.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Spinal Fusion shows early post-operative period (8 weeks) with expected limited mobility, activity restriction, and early fusion integration.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adult reports gradual onset of involuntary shaking at rest and difficulty with fine motor tasks. Movement feels slower and stiffer overall. Recent falls despite no acute injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot button clothing or use cutlery with usual dexterity. Walking requires concentration and has become slow and shuffling. Difficulty rising from chairs and turning in bed.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began gradually 18 months ago. No acute injury or recent medication change. One relative diagnosed with similar movement disorder in their 70s. Symptoms have progressively worsened without remission.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, resting tremor is evident at 4-6 Hz frequency. Rigidity is present with cogwheel quality on passive motion testing. Gait demonstrates shuffling pattern with reduced arm swing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brain may show midbrain atrophy. PET imaging demonstrates reduced dopamine uptake in striatum confirming diagnosis. DaTscan shows reduced dopaminergic activity in the putamen.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced idiopathic Parkinson's disease with prominent rigidity, postural instability, freezing gait, and significant dopamine depletion.

aliases: Benign Nocturnal Limb Pains of Childhood

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A school-age child reports intermittent pain in the lower legs, typically in the anterior tibia or calf region. Pain is bilateral and comes and goes unpredictably over weeks to months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain occurs predominantly at rest in the evenings and nights. Activity during the day does not typically provoke symptoms. Child is symptom-free by morning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms correlate with known growth spurts in children typically between ages 3-10. No history of injury or trauma. Family history of growing pains is common. Symptoms are self-limited and have not required intervention.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, no objective findings are present. Lower extremity range of motion is normal and pain-free. Gait is normal during examination with no limp observed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays are normal with no evidence of fracture, physeal injury, or bony lesion. Diagnosis is clinical and based on characteristic presentation; imaging is typically unnecessary unless red flags present.

puzzles.explanation: Growing pains are benign nocturnal limb pains in children during growth periods, resolving with reassurance and symptomatic treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports sudden brief episodes of severe facial pain. Pain is electric shock-like in quality affecting one side of the face. Pain episodes are unpredictable but may be triggered by touch or chewing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports functional avoidance of eating, speaking, and facial grooming due to pain episodes. Difficulty maintaining oral hygiene and sustaining conversations. Quality of life significantly impacted by unpredictability of triggers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Trigeminal neuralgia typically presents in older adults with increased prevalence after age 50. Multiple sclerosis increases risk particularly in younger patients. Vascular compression of the trigeminal nerve root is proposed mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Facial sensation intact in most cases with no dermatomal loss. Pain reproducibly triggered by light touch to perioral region, cheek, or chin. Trigeminal motor function (masseter strength, jaw deviation) normal. No other cranial nerve deficits elicited.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brain and brainstem assesses for vascular loop compressing the trigeminal nerve. T1 weighted imaging demonstrates anatomical relationships. High-resolution CISS sequences show nerve-vessel interface.

puzzles.explanation: Classic trigeminal neuralgia with sudden severe lancinating pain, clear trigger areas, unilateral distribution, and MRI-confirmed vascular compression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete reports progressive medial-sided elbow pain that worsens with repetitive overhead activity over several weeks. Pain is activity-dependent and improves with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform throwing activities without exacerbating symptoms. Pain onset correlates with throwing frequency and intensity. Complete cessation of throwing resolves symptoms within days to weeks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Little League elbow occurs in young pitchers typically before skeletal maturity. Excessive throwing volume and poor mechanics increase risk. Rapid growth during adolescence predisposes to apophyseal injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited over the medial epicondyle. Valgus stress testing reproduces medial-sided elbow pain. Forearm pronation and wrist flexion against resistance may reproduce symptoms.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show medial epicondyle apophysitis with fragmentation or widening of the apophysis. MRI demonstrates edema and inflammatory changes at the medial epicondyle. Advanced imaging confirms extent of injury.

puzzles.explanation: Little Leaguer's elbow is medial epicondyle apophysitis from repetitive throwing, requiring activity modification and rest.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An elderly patient reports acute severe pain in the hip region following a fall. Pain is constant and significantly worsened by any leg movement. Patient is unable to ambulate or weight bear on the affected side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Inability to internally rotate the hip. Pain is severely reproduced by any attempt to move the leg, particularly flexion and external rotation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Osteoporosis documented on prior DEXA scan. Patient lives alone and has had multiple falls over the past 18 months. No significant trauma history otherwise.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the hip is often held in external rotation and flexion. Tenderness over the hip and groin is severe. Crepitus may be palpated with leg movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs confirm femoral neck fracture with posterior displacement. CT shows comminution of the intertrochanteric region. No associated pelvic injury.

puzzles.explanation: Intertrochanteric hip fractures in very elderly with dementia have poor prognosis and require careful surgical decision-making.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Chronic pelvic pain

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with menstruation and deep penetration during intercourse

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive dysmenorrhea over years; infertility workup initiated; prior pelvic surgery negative

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Nodularity palpable along uterosacral ligaments; fixed retroverted uterus; tenderness with cervical motion

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows T2 hyperintense lesions in ovary with restricted diffusion and chocolate cysts bilaterally

puzzles.explanation: Endometriosis-related pain requires multidisciplinary management including gynecologic treatment, pelvic floor therapy for associated myofascial dysfunction, pain management strategies, and psychological support given chronic nature of condition.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old recreational athlete reports anterior knee pain for 6 weeks that worsens with explosive movements and is aggravated by training volume increases. Pain is localized around the knee but character and specific location not yet defined.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform jumping or deceleration-based movements without sharp anterior knee pain. Pain-free with walking and static activities. Symptoms reproducible and consistent with sport participation but resolve completely with 48-hour rest. No night pain or swelling that persists overnight.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset correlating with increased training volume (volleyball 4x per week). Repetitive jumping and landing mechanics in sport. No acute traumatic event. History of patellar tendon soreness 2 years ago. Adequate flexibility initially but recently decreased.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness directly over proximal patellar tendon at superior patellar pole. Quadriceps contraction against resistance elicits pain. Single-leg squat shows valgus knee position (femoral internal rotation) suggesting biomechanical dysfunction. Knee flexion 0-120 degrees pain-free but 120-140 degrees aggravating. Palpable swelling at insertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows hypoechoic thickening of proximal quadriceps tendon (8mm versus normal 5-6mm) with loss of fibrillar echotexture. Increased Doppler signal indicating inflammation and neovascularity. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration without full-thickness tear. T2 signal abnormality within proximal third of tendon.

puzzles.explanation: Quadriceps tendinopathy typically presents at the insertion above the patella, often activity-related.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old reports overwhelming fatigue disproportionate to activity level, persistent for several months. Also notes generalized weakness, poor sleep quality, and difficulty concentrating. No recent illness or fever.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to return to pre-illness exercise routine. Becomes short of breath and fatigued after 10-15 minutes of light walking. Difficulty with household tasks, climbing stairs. Symptoms do not improve with rest alone.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Decline began 6-9 months ago following intensive chemotherapy and/or radiation therapy completed 4-6 months prior. Previously very active. Reports chemotherapy-related peripheral neuropathy in hands and feet. No cardiac history before treatment.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting heart rate 105 bpm. Blood pressure slightly elevated. Reduced exercise tolerance on low-intensity treadmill test—dyspnea and fatigue at 50% predicted exertion. Diminished grip strength bilaterally. Mild edema in lower legs. Lung bases show reduced breath sounds.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram shows reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (40-50%) consistent with chemotherapy-induced cardiotoxicity. Chest X-ray reveals ground-glass opacities in lower lobes suggesting pulmonary toxicity from prior radiation. Pulmonary function testing shows reduced FEV1 and diffusion capacity.

puzzles.explanation: Cancer-related fatigue requires structured exercise program, psychological support for anxiety and depression, symptom management including sleep optimization, energy conservation strategies, and gradual return-to-work planning with occupational rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner reports lateral midfoot pain with acute onset during a pushing-off movement. Pain is sharp and localized to the lateral foot, worsened by weight bearing and running.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Running and rapid directional changes provoke pain. Single-leg stance and explosive push-off activities are not tolerated. Swelling develops in the lateral midfoot with prolonged activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Six-week history of progressive lateral midfoot pain in a distance runner. Recent increase in training volume. Alternatively, acute onset after inversion injury during sport. Area notably slow to improve with conservative rest and ice.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pinpoint tenderness at the lateral aspect of the midfoot, just proximal to the fifth metatarsal base. Pain reproduced by single-leg stance and hopping. Lateral foot swelling present. Inability to bear weight on lateral border of foot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate transverse fracture at the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal. CT imaging assesses fracture displacement and healing. Advanced imaging guides timing of weight bearing progression.

puzzles.explanation: Recurrent or non-union Jones fractures require careful management due to poor healing potential.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports anterior chest and shoulder pain. Onset gradual over 2-3 weeks. Aggravated by upper extremity activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to perform overhead pressing at gym. Throwing activities impossible. Horizontal adduction movements consistently reproduce pain. Symptoms worse with repetitive scapular protraction-based activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient is competitive swimmer training 5 days per week with recent increase in volume. Acute on chronic presentation — underlying tight pectoralis minor noted on previous physio assessment 6 months ago. No direct trauma. Pain started during training block.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness and spasm in pectoralis minor in anterior axilla. Resisted horizontal adduction and scapular protraction reproduce sharp pain. Scapular positioning asymmetrical — anteriorly tilted on affected side. No neurological signs. Shoulder ROM otherwise preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates hyperechoic edema within pectoralis minor muscle belly with disruption of normal fibre echotexture. No full-thickness tear. MRI T2 signal changes localised to medial pectoralis minor, consistent with acute muscle strain. Anterior shoulder structures otherwise unremarkable.

puzzles.explanation: Pectoralis minor strain from repetitive or forceful activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 72-year-old presents with 5 days of cough and shortness of breath. Reports feeling unwell, with fatigue and difficulty sleeping. No recent travel or sick contacts reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk more than a few metres without stopping to rest. Difficulty lying flat at night. Minimal exertion triggers breathing difficulty. Previously independent with activities of daily living.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing history of chronic lung disease. Symptoms onset acute over 5 days. No recent falls or trauma. Current smoker. Lives at home with family. Rapid functional decline from baseline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Respiratory rate 28 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 88% on room air, temperature 38.5°C, heart rate elevated. Auscultation reveals crackles in right lower lung field. Increased tactile fremitus over same area. No focal limb weakness or neurological deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows segmental consolidation in right lower lobe with air bronchograms. Blood cultures positive for bacterial pathogen. Sputum culture confirms gram-positive cocci consistent with community-acquired infection.

puzzles.explanation: Typical community-acquired pneumonia with acute onset, focal findings, and imaging confirmation of infiltrate. Likely bacterial etiology given clinical presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Worker reports episodic finger blanching and color changes triggered by cold or vibration exposure. Fingers turn white, then red and painful as they rewarm. Numbness and tingling accompany episodes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worsen with prolonged grip or repetitive hand tasks. Patient avoids cold environments due to severe finger reactions. Occupational exposure is unavoidable given job demands.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Vibration white finger develops from chronic exposure to vibrating power tools. Jackhammers, chainsaws, and pneumatic tools are common culprits. Duration and intensity of tool exposure correlate with symptom severity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Affected fingers show poor color return after cold provocation test. Grip strength reduced in affected hand. Two-point discrimination diminished in fingertips. Pulse oximetry shows reduced oxygen saturation in affected digits compared to unaffected fingers.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nailfold capillaroscopy demonstrates abnormal capillary patterns characteristic of vascular dysfunction. Thermography shows abnormal temperature distribution in affected fingers. Doppler ultrasound assesses digital artery flow and perfusion.

puzzles.explanation: Vibration white finger shows hand-arm vibration syndrome with Raynaud-like episodes and sensory dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 34-year-old woman with 4 weeks of progressive bilateral leg weakness. Reports fatigue and difficulty concentrating at work. Symptoms began 2 months after a viral illness-like episode.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot climb stairs due to leg weakness. Difficulty with balance and coordination. Numbness in feet and lower legs. Increased bladder urgency. Pain noted when moving eyes.

clues.label: History
